entries.farsi: گفت چون باشد سگى كور پليد / جست او از خواب خود را شير ديد
entries.english: He said, “How is a blind filthy dog that sprang up from sleep and found itself to be a lion?—

entries.farsi: نه چنان شيرى كه كس تيرش زند / بل ز بيمش تيغ و پيكان بشكند
entries.english: Not such a lion as anyone could shoot; nay, but by the terror of it sword and javelin would be shattered.

entries.farsi: كور بر اشكم رونده همچو مار / چشمها بگشاد در باغ و بهار
entries.english: The blind man, going on his belly, like a snake— he has opened his eyes in the garden and in spring?”

entries.farsi: چون بود آن چون كه از چونى رهيد / در حياتستان بى‏چونى رسيد
entries.english: How is the “how” that has been freed from “how-ness” and has attained to the abounding life of “how-lessness”?

entries.farsi: گشت چونى بخش اندر المكان / ‏گرد خوانش جمله چونها چون سگان
entries.english: He has become a dispenser of “how-ness” in the world beyond locality: all “how’s” are round his table, like dogs.

entries.farsi: او ز بى‏چونى دهدشان استخوان / ‏در جنابت تن زن اين سوره مخوان
entries.english: He gives them a bone from “how-lessness.” In the state of pollution, keep silence: do not recite this Súra.

entries.farsi: تا ز چونى غسل نارى تو تمام / ‏تو بر اين مصحف منه كف اى غالم
entries.english: Until you wash yourself entirely clean of “how-ness,” do not put your hand on this Book, O youth.

entries.farsi: گر پليدم ور نظيفم اى شهان / ‏اين نخوانم پس چه خوانم در جهان
entries.english: Whether I am dirty or clean, O princes, if I do not recite this, then what in the world shall I recite?

entries.farsi: تو مرا گويى كه از بهر ثواب / ‏غسل ناكرده مرو در حوض آب
entries.english: You say to me, “For the sake of the reward, do not go into the water tank without having washed”;

entries.farsi: از برون حوض غير خاك نيست / ‏هر كه او در حوض نايد پاك نيست
entries.english: Outside of the tank there is nothing but earth: no one who does not enter the tank is clean.

entries.farsi: گر نباشد آبها را اين كرم / ‏كاو پذيرد مر خبث را دم به دم
entries.english: If the waters have not the grace to receive filth continually,

entries.farsi: واى بر مشتاق و بر اوميد او / حسرتا بر حسرت جاويد او
entries.english: Alas for the longing lover and his hope! Oh, sorrow for his everlasting sorrow!

entries.farsi: آب دارد صد كرم صد احتشام / ‏كه پليدان را پذيرد و السلام
entries.english: The water has a hundred graces, a hundred pities, for it receives the defiled ones and purifies them—and peace be with you!

entries.farsi: اى ضياء الحق حسام الدين كه نور / پاسبان تست از شر الطيور
entries.english: O you Radiance of God, Husámu’ddín, the Light is your protector from the worst of flying creatures.

entries.farsi: پاسبان تست نور و ارتقاش / ‏اى تو خورشيد مستر از خفاش
entries.english: The Light and its ascent are your protector, O Sun who art concealed from the bat.

entries.farsi: پرده‏ى خورشيد هم نور رب است / ‏بى‏نصيب از وى خفاش است و شب است
entries.english: The veil before the face of the Sun, what is it but excess of brilliance and intensity of splendour?

entries.farsi: چيست پرده پيش روى آفتاب / ‏جز فزونى شعشعه و تيزى تاب
entries.english: The veil over the Sun is just the Light of the Lord: the bat and the night have no lot therein.

entries.farsi: هر دو چون در بعد و پرده مانده‏اند / يا سيه رو يا فسرده مانده‏اند
entries.english: Inasmuch as both have remained far and veiled, they have remained either black-faced or cold.

entries.farsi: آن هالل و بدر دارند اتحاد / از دويى دورند و از نقص و فساد
entries.english: Since you have written part of the story of the New-moon, put into words the tale of the Full-moon.

entries.farsi: چون نبشتى بعضى از قصه‏ى هالل / ‏داستان بدر آر اندر مقال
entries.english: The New-moon and the Full-moon have oneness: they are far from duality and from imperfection and corruption.

entries.farsi: آن هالل از نقص در باطن برى است / ‏آن به ظاهر نقص تدريج آورى است
entries.english: The new-moon is inwardly free from imperfection: its apparent imperfection is increasing gradually.

entries.farsi: در تانى گويد اى عجول خام / ‏پايه پايه بر توان رفتن به بام
entries.english: Night by night it gives a lesson in gradualness and with deliberation it produces relief.

entries.farsi: درس گويد شب به شب تدريج را / در تانى بر دهد تفريج را
entries.english: With deliberation it says, “O hasty fool, only step by step can one mount to the roof.”

entries.farsi: ديگ را تدريج و استادانه جوش / ‏كار نايد قليه‏ى ديوانه جوش
entries.english: Let the cooking-pot boil gradually, as a skilful does: the stew boiled in a mad hurry is of no use.

entries.farsi: حق نه قادر بود بر خلق فلك / ‏در يكى لحظه به كن بى‏هيچ شك
entries.english: Was not God able to create heaven in one moment by “Be”? Without any doubt.

entries.farsi: خلقت طفل از چه اندر نه مه است / ‏ز انكه تدريج از شعار آن شه است
entries.english: Why, then, O seeker of instruction, did He extend for it to six days, every day a thousand years?

entries.farsi: پس چرا شش روز آن را در كشيد / كل يوم الف عام اى مستفيد
entries.english: What is the reason for the creation of a child taking nine months? Because gradualness is a characteristic of that King.

entries.farsi: خلقت آدم چرا چل صبح بود / اندر آن گل اندك اندك مى‏فزود
entries.english: Why was the creation of Adam forty mornings? He was adding to that clay little by little,

entries.farsi: نه چو تو اى خام كاكنون تاختى / ‏طفلى و خود را تو شيخى ساختى
entries.english: Not like you, O foolish one, who have rushed forward just now: you are a child, and you have made yourself out to be an Elder.

entries.farsi: بر دويدى چون كدو فوق همه / ‏كو ترا پاى جهاد و ملحمه
entries.english: You have run up, like a gourd, to the top of all, where is the warfare and combat to sustain you?

entries.farsi: تكيه كردى بر درختان و جدار / بر شدى اى اقرعك هم قرع‏وار
entries.english: You have rested on trees and walls for support: you have climbed up like a pumpkin, O little baldhead.

entries.farsi: اول ار شد مركبت سرو سهى / ‏ليك آخر خشك و بى‏مغزى تهى
entries.english: If at first you mounted on a tall cypress, yet in the end you are dry and pulp less and empty.

entries.farsi: رنگ سبزت زرد شد اى قرع زود / ز انكه از گلگونه بود اصلى نبود
entries.english: Your green colour soon turned yellow, O pumpkin, for it was derived from rouge, it was not original.

sections.title_fa: داستان آن عجوزه كه روى زشت خويشتن را جندره و گلگونه مى‏ساخت و ساخته نمى‏شد و پذيرا نمى‏آمد
sections.title_en: Story of the old woman who used to depilate and rouge her ugly face,
though it could never be put right and become pleasing.

entries.farsi: بود كمپيرى نود ساله كالن / ‏پر تشنج روى و رنگش زعفران
entries.english: There was a decrepit old woman aged ninety years, her face covered with wrinkles and her complexion saffron.

entries.farsi: چون سر سفره رخ او توى توى / ‏ليك در وى بود مانده عشق شوى
entries.english: Her face was in folds like the surface of a traveller’s food-wallet, but there remained in her the passionate desire for a husband.

entries.farsi: ريخت دندانهاش و مو چون شير شد / قد كمان و هر حسش تغيير شد
entries.english: Her teeth had dropped out and her hair had become as milk: her figure was like a bow, and every sense in her was decayed.

entries.farsi: عشق شوى و شهوت و حرصش تمام / ‏عشق صيد و پاره پاره گشته دام
entries.english: Her passion for a husband and her lust and desire were in full force: the passion for snaring, though the trap had fallen to pieces.

entries.farsi: مرغ بى‏هنگام و راه بى‏رهى / ‏آتشى پر در بن ديگ تهى
entries.english: She was like a cock that crows at the wrong time, a road that leads nowhere, a big fire beneath an empty kettle;

entries.farsi: عاشق ميدان و اسب و پاى نى / ‏عاشق زمر و لب و سرناى نى
entries.english: Like one who is exceedingly fond of the race-course, but has no horse and no means of running; exceedingly fond of piping, but having neither lip nor pipe.

entries.farsi: حرص در پيرى جهودان را مباد / اى شقيى كه خداش اين حرص داد
entries.english: May Jews have no greediness in old age! Oh, miserable is he on whom God has bestowed this selfishness!

entries.farsi: ريخت دندانهاى سگ چون پير شد / ترك مردم كرد و سرگين گير شد
entries.english: A dog’s teeth drop out when it grows old: it leaves people and takes to eating dung;

entries.farsi: اين سگان شصت ساله را نگر / هر دمى دندان سگشان تيزتر
entries.english: Look at these sexagenarian dogs! Their dog-teeth get sharper at every moment.

entries.farsi: پير سگ را ريخت پشم از پوستين / ‏اين سگان پير اطلس پوش بين
entries.english: The hairs drop from the fur of an old dog; see these old dogs clad in satin!

entries.farsi: عشقشان و حرصشان در فرج و زر / دم‏به‏دم چون نسل سگ بين بيشتر
entries.english: See how their passionate desire and greed for women and gold, like the progeny of dogs, is increasing continually!

entries.farsi: اين چنين عمرى كه مايه‏ى دوزخ است / ‏مر قصابان غضب را مسلخ است
entries.english: Such a life as this, which is Hell’s stock-in-trade, is a shambles for the butchers of Wrath;

entries.farsi: چون بگويندش كه عمر تو دراز / مى‏شود دل خوش دهانش از خنده باز
entries.english: When people say to him, “May your life be long!” he is delighted and opens his mouth in laughter.

entries.farsi: اين چنين نفرين دعا پندارد او / چشم نگشايد سرى برنارد او
entries.english: He thinks a curse like this is a benediction: he never uncloses his eye or raises his head once.

entries.farsi: گر بديدى يك سر موى از معاد / اوش گفتى اين چنين عمر تو باد
entries.english: If he had seen a hair’s tip of the future state, he would have said to him, “May your life be like this!”

sections.title_fa: داستان آن درويش كه آن گيالنى را دعا كرد كه خدا ترا به سالمت به خان و مان باز رساناد
sections.title_en: Story of the dervish who blessed a man of Gilan, saying, “May God bring you back in safety to your home and household!”

entries.farsi: چون ستد زو نان بگفت اى مستعان / ‏خوش به خان و مان خود بازش رسان
entries.english: One day a sturdy beggar, very fond of bread and carried a basket, accosted a Khwaja of Gilan.

entries.farsi: گفت يك روزى به خواجه‏ى گيليى / ‏نان پرستى نر گدا زنبيليى
entries.english: On receiving some bread from him, he cried, “O You, whose help is sought, bring him back happy to his home and household!”

entries.farsi: گفت اگر آن است خان كه ديده‏ام / ‏حق ترا آن جا رساند اى دژم
entries.english: He said, “If the house is the one that I have seen, may God bring you there, O squalid wretch!”

entries.farsi: هر محدث را خسان بد دل كنند / حرفش ار عالى بود نازل كنند
entries.english: Worthless folk humiliate every story-teller: if his words are lofty, they make them low;

entries.farsi: ز انكه قدر مستمع آيد نبا / بر قد خواجه برد درزى قبا
entries.english: For the tale is in proportion to the hearer: the tailor cuts the coat according to the Khwaja’s figure.

sections.title_fa: صفت آن عجوز
sections.title_en: Description of the old woman.

entries.farsi: چون كه مجلس بى‏چنين پيغاره نيست / ‏از حديث پست نازل چاره نيست
entries.english: Since the audience is not free from such reproach, there is no means of avoiding low and undignified talk.

entries.farsi: واستان هين اين سخن را از گرو / سوى افسانه‏ى عجوزه باز رو
entries.english: Listen, redeem this topic from pawn: return to the tale of the old woman.

entries.farsi: چون مسن گشت و در اين ره نيست مرد / تو بنه نامش عجوز سال خورد
entries.english: When he has become advanced in years and is not a man in this Way, bestow the name of “aged crone” upon him.

entries.farsi: نه دهنده نه پذيرنده‏ى خوشى / ‏نه در او معنى و نه معنى كشى
entries.english: He has neither capital and basis, nor is he capable of receiving stock-in-trade.

entries.farsi: نه مر او را راس مال و پايه‏اى / ‏نه پذيراى قبول مايه‏اى
entries.english: He is neither a giver nor a receiver of delight; in him there is neither reality nor absorbing reality.

entries.farsi: نه زبان نه گوش و نه عقل و بصر / نه هش و نه بى‏هشى و نه فكر
entries.english: Neither tongue nor ear nor understanding and insight nor consciousness nor unconsciousness nor reflections;

entries.farsi: نه رهى ببريده او نه پاى راه / ‏نه تبش آن قحبه را نه سوز و آه
entries.english: Neither humble supplication nor any beauty to show pride: his, coat on coat, is stinking, like an onion.

entries.farsi: نه نياز و نه جمالى بهر ناز / تو بتويش گنده مانند پياز
entries.english: He has not traversed any path, nor the foot for the path: that shameless one has neither glow nor burning (passion) and sighs.

sections.title_fa: ‏قصه‏ى درويش كه از آن خانه هر چه مى‏خواست مى‏گفت نيست
sections.title_en: Story of the dervish to whom, whenever he begged anything from a certain house, he used to say, “It is not to be had here.”

entries.farsi: سائلى آمد به سوى خانه‏اى / ‏خشك نانه خواست يا تر نانه‏اى
entries.english: A beggar came to a house and asked for a piece of dry bread or a piece of moist bread.

entries.farsi: گفت صاحب خانه نان اينجا كجاست / ‏خيره‏اى كى اين دكان نانباست
entries.english: The owner of the house said, “Where is bread in this place? Are you crazy? How is this a baker’s shop?”

entries.farsi: گفت بارى اندكى پى هم بياب / ‏گفت آخر نيست دكان قصاب
entries.english: “At least,” he begged, “get me a little bit of fat.” “Why,” said he, “it isn’t a butcher’s shop.”

entries.farsi: گفت پاره‏ى آرد ده اى كدخدا / گفت پندارى كه هست اين آسيا
entries.english: He said, “O master of the house, give me a pittance of flour.” “Do you think this is a mill?” he replied.

entries.farsi: گفت بارى آب ده از مكرعه / ‏گفت آخر نيست جويا مشرعه
entries.english: “Well then,” said he, “give me some water from the reservoir.” “Why,” he replied, “it isn’t a river or a watering-place.”

entries.farsi: هر چه او درخواست از نان تا سبوس / ‏چربكى مى‏گفت و مى‏كردش فسوس
entries.english: Whatever he asked for, from bread to bran, he was mocking and deriding him.

entries.farsi: آن گدا در رفت و دامن بر كشيد / اندر آن خانه به حسبت خواست ريد
entries.english: The beggar went in and drew up his skirt: he jumped into the house and wanted to relieve himself.

entries.farsi: گفت هى‏هى گفت تن زن اى دژم / ‏تا در اين ويرانه خود فارغ كنم
entries.english: He (the householder) cried, “Hey, hey!” “Be quiet, O morose man,” said he, “Since this is a ruin, I had better answer nature’s call.

entries.farsi: چون در اينجا نيست وجه زيستن / ‏بر چنين خانه ببايد ريستن
entries.english: Since there is no means of living (zístan) here, upon a house like this defecate (rístan) oportet.”

entries.farsi: نيستى طاوس با صد نقش بند / كه به نقشت چشمها روشن كنند
entries.english: Since you are not a falcon, so as to catch the prey, a falcon hand trained for the King’s hunting;

entries.farsi: چون نه‏اى بازى كه گيرى تو شكار / دست آموز شكار شهريار
entries.english: Nor a peacock painted with a hundred designs, so that eyes should be illumined by the picture which you present;

entries.farsi: هم نه‏اى طوطى كه چون قندت دهند / گوش سوى گفت شيرينت نهند
entries.english: Nor a parrot, so that when sugar is given to you, ears should bend to your sweet talk;

entries.farsi: هم نه‏اى بلبل كه عاشق‏وار زار / خوش بنالى در چمن يا الله‏زار
entries.english: Nor a nightingale to sing, like a lover, sweetly and plaintively in the meadow or the tulip-garden;

entries.farsi: هم نه‏اى هدهد كه پيكيها كنى / ‏نه چو لكلك كه وطن بالا كنى
entries.english: Nor a hoopoe to bring messages, nor are you like a stork to make your nest on high—

entries.farsi: در چه كارى تو و بهر چت خرند / تو چه مرغى و ترا با چه خورند
entries.english: In what work are you, and for what are you bought? What bird are you, and with what are you eaten?

entries.farsi: زين دكان با مكاسان برتر آ / ‏تا دكان فضل كاهلل اشترى
entries.english: Mount beyond this shop of hagglers to the shop of Bounty where God is the purchaser.

entries.farsi: كاله‏اى كه هيچ خلقش ننگريد / از خالقت آن كريم آن را خريد
entries.english: That Gracious One has purchased the piece of goods that no people would look at on account of its shabbiness.

entries.farsi: هيچ قلبى پيش او مردود نيست / ز انكه قصدش از خريدن سود نيست
entries.english: With Him no base coin is rejected, for His object in buying is not profit.

sections.title_fa: رجوع بداستان آن كمپير
sections.title_en: Return to the tale of the old woman.

entries.farsi: چون عروسى خواست رفتن آن خريف / ‏موى ابرو پاك كرد آن مستخيف
entries.english: Since that autumn desired to be wed, that lustful one plucked out the hair of her eyebrows.

entries.farsi: پيش رو آيينه بگرفت آن عجوز / تا بيارايد رخ و رخسار و پوز
entries.english: The old woman took the mirror before her face, that she might beautify her cheeks and face and mouth.

entries.farsi: چند گلگونه بماليد از بطر / سفره‏ى رويش نشد پوشيده‏تر
entries.english: She rubbed gleefully rouge several times, the creases of her face did not become more concealed,

entries.farsi: عشرهاى مصحف از جا مى‏بريد / مى‏بچسبانيد بر رو آن پليد
entries.english: That filthy hag was cutting out portions of the Holy Book and sticking them on her face,

entries.farsi: تا كه سفره‏ى روى او پنهان شود / تا نگين حلقه‏ى خوبان شود
entries.english: In order that the creases of her face might be hidden and that she might become the bezel in the ring of fair.

entries.farsi: عشرها بر روى هر جا مى‏نهاد / چون كه بر مى‏بست چادر مى‏فتاد
entries.english: She was putting bits of the Book all over her face; they always dropped off when she put on her veil (chador);

entries.farsi: باز او آن عشرها را با خدو / مى‏بچسبانيد بر اطراف رو
entries.english: Then she would stick them on again with spittle on all sides of her face,

entries.farsi: باز چادر راست كردى آن نگين / ‏عشرها افتادى از رو بر زمين
entries.english: And once more that bezel would arrange her veil, and the bits of the Book would fall from her face to the ground.

entries.farsi: چون بسى مى‏كرد فن و آن مى‏فتاد / گفت صد لعنت بر آن ابليس باد
entries.english: Since they always dropped off though she tried many an artifice, she exclaimed, “A hundred curses on Iblís!”

entries.farsi: شد مصور آن زمان ابليس زود / گفت اى قحبه‏ى قديد بى‏ورود
entries.english: Immediately Iblís took shape and said, “O luckless dried-up harlot,

entries.farsi: من همه عمر اين نينديشيده‏ام / ‏نه ز جز تو قحبه اى اين ديده‏ام
entries.english: In all my life I have never thought of this: I have never seen this by any harlot except you.

entries.farsi: تخم نادر در فضيحت كاشتى / ‏در جهان تو مصحفى نگذاشتى
entries.english: You have sown unique seed in infamy: you have not left a single Scripture in the world.

entries.farsi: صد بليسى تو خميس اندر خميس / ‏ترك من گوى اى عجوزه‏ى دردبيس
entries.english: You are a hundred Devils, troop on troop: let me alone, O foul hag!”

entries.farsi: چند دزدى عشر از علم كتاب / ‏تا شود رويت ملون همچو سيب
entries.english: How long will you steal portions of the lore of the Book, in order that your face may be coloured like an apple?

entries.farsi: چند دزدى حرف مردان خدا / تا فروشى و ستانى مرحبا
entries.english: How long will you steal the words of the men of God that you may sell and obtain applause?

entries.farsi: رنگ بر بسته ترا گلگون نكرد / شاخ بر بسته فن عرجون نكرد
entries.english: The daubed-on colour never made you rosy; the tied-on bough never performed the function of the stump.

entries.farsi: عاقبت چون چادر مرگت رسد / از رخت اين عشرها اندر فتد
entries.english: At last, when the veil of death comes over you, these bits of the Book drop away from your face.

entries.farsi: چون كه آيد خيز خيز آن رحيل / ‏گم شود ز آن پس فنون قال و قيل
entries.english: When the call comes to arise and depart, thereafter the arts of disputation vanish.

entries.farsi: عالم خاموشى آيد پيش بيست / ‏واى آن كه در درون انسيش نيست
entries.english: The world of silence comes into view. Stop! Alas for him that has not a familiarity within him!

entries.farsi: صيقلى كن يك دو روزى سينه را / دفتر خود ساز آن آيينه را
entries.english: Polish your heart for a day or two: make that mirror your book,

entries.farsi: كه ز سايه‏ى يوسف صاحب قران / ‏شد زليخاى عجوز از سر جوان
entries.english: For from the reflection of the imperial Joseph old Zalikha became young anew.

entries.farsi: مى‏شود مبدل به خورشيد تموز / آن مزاج بارد برد العجوز
entries.english: The chilly temperature of “the old woman’s cold spell” is changed by the July sun.

entries.farsi: مى‏شود مبدل به سوز مريمى / ‏شاخ لب خشكى به نخلى خرمى
entries.english: A dry-lipped bough is changed into a flourishing palm-tree by the burning of a Mary.

entries.farsi: اى عجوزه چند كوشى با قضا / ‏نقد جو اكنون رها كن ما مضى
entries.english: O old woman, how long will you strive with the destiny? Seek the cash now: let bygones be.

entries.farsi: چون رخت را نيست در خوبى اميد / خواه گلگونه نه و خواهى مداد
entries.english: Since your face has no hope of beauty, you may either put rouge or, if you wish, ink.

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن رنجور كه طبيب در او اوميد صحت نديد
sections.title_en: Story of the sick man of whose recovery the physician despaired.

entries.farsi: آن يكى رنجور شد سوى طبيب / ‏گفت نبضم را فرو بين اى لبيب
entries.english: Story of the sick man of whose recovery the physician despaired.

entries.farsi: كه ز نبض آگه شوى بر حال دل / ‏كه رگ دست است با دل متصل
entries.english: That by the pulse you may diagnose the state of my heart, for the hand-vein is connected with the heart.”

entries.farsi: چون كه دل غيب است خواهى زو مثال / ‏زو بجو كه با دل استش اتصال
entries.english: Since the heart is invisible, if you want a symbol of it, seek from him who has connection with the heart.

entries.farsi: باد پنهان است از چشم اى امين / ‏در غبار و جنبش برگش ببين
entries.english: The wind is hidden from the eye, O trusty friend; see it in the dust and in the movement of the leaves,

entries.farsi: كز يمين است او وزان يا از شمال / ‏جنبش برگت بگويد وصف حال
entries.english: Observe whether it is blowing from the right or from the left: the movement of the leaves will describe its condition to you.

entries.farsi: مستى دل را نمى‏دانى كه كو / وصف او از نرگس مخمور جو
entries.english: You know not intoxication of the heart where, seek the description of it from the inebriated eye.

entries.farsi: چون ز ذات حق بعيدى وصف ذات / ‏باز دانى از رسول و معجزات
entries.english: Since you are far from the Essence of God, you may recognise the description of the Essence in the Prophet and evidentiary miracles.

entries.farsi: معجزاتى و كراماتى خفى / ‏بر زند بر دل ز پيران صفى
entries.english: Certain secret miracles and graces from the elect Elders impress the heart;

entries.farsi: كه درونشان صد قيامت نقد هست / ‏كمترين آن كه شود همسايه مست
entries.english: For within them there are a hundred immediate resurrections, the least is this, that their neighbour becomes intoxicated;

entries.farsi: پس جليس اللهَّ گشت آن نيك بخت / ‏كاو به پهلوى سعيدى برد رخت
entries.english: Hence that fortunate who has devoted himself to a blessed has become the companion of God.

entries.farsi: معجزه‏ى كان بر جمادى زد اثر / يا عصا يا بحر يا شق القمر
entries.english: The evidentiary miracle that produced an effect upon something inanimate either the rod or the sea or the splitting of the moon.

entries.farsi: گر اثر بر جان زند بى‏واسطه / ‏متصل گردد به پنهان رابطه
entries.english: If it produces an immediate effect upon the soul, it is brought into connection by means of a hidden link.

entries.farsi: بر جمادات آن اثرها عاريه ست / ‏آن پى روح خوش متواريه ست
entries.english: The effects produced upon inanimate objects are accessory: they are for the sake of the fair invisible spirit,

entries.farsi: تا از آن جامد اثر گيرد ضمير / حبذا نان بى‏هيوالى خمير
entries.english: In order that the inmost heart may be affected by means of that inanimate object. How excellent is bread without the substance, dough!

entries.farsi: بر زند از جان كامل معجزات / ‏بر ضمير جان طالب چون حيات
entries.english: How excellent is the Messiah’s table of food without stint! How excellent is Mary’s fruit without an orchard!

entries.farsi: حبذا خوان مسيحى بى‏كمى / ‏حبذا بى‏باغ ميوه‏ى مريمى
entries.english: Miracles from the spirit of the perfect affect the soul of the seeker as life.

entries.farsi: معجزه بحر است و ناقص مرغ خاك / ‏مرغ آبى در وى ايمن از هالك
entries.english: The miracle is the sea, and the deficient man is the land bird; the water-bird is safe from destruction there.

entries.farsi: عجز بخش جان هر نامحرمى / ‏ليك قدرت بخش جان هم دمى
entries.english: It bestows infirmity on any one that is uninitiated, but it bestows power on the spirit of an intimate.

entries.farsi: چون نيابى اين سعادت در ضمير / پس ز ظاهر هر دم استدالل گير
entries.english: Since you do not feel this bliss in your inmost heart, and then continually seek the clue to it from outside,

entries.farsi: كه اثرها بر مشاعر ظاهر است / ‏وين اثرها از موثر مخبر است
entries.english: For effects are apparent to the senses, and these effects give information concerning their producer.

entries.farsi: هست پنهان معنى هر دارويى / ‏همچو سحر و صنعت هر جادويى
entries.english: The virtue of every drug is hidden like magic and the art of any sorcerer;

entries.farsi: چون نظر در فعل و آثارش كنى / ‏گر چه پنهان است اظهارش كنى
entries.english: When you regard its action and effects, you bring it to light though it is hidden.

entries.farsi: قوتى كان در درونش مضمر است / ‏چون به فعل آيد عيان و مظهر است
entries.english: The potency that is concealed within it is clearly seen and made manifest when it comes into action.

entries.farsi: نه سببها و اثرها مغز و پوست / چون بجويى جملگى آثار اوست
entries.english: Since all these things are revealed to you by means of effects, how is not God revealed to you by the production of effects?

entries.farsi: چون به آثار اين همه پيدا شدت / ‏چون نشد پيدا ز تاثير ايزدت
entries.english: Causes and effects, kernel and husk—are not the whole, when you investigate, effects produced by Him?

entries.farsi: از خيالى دوست گيرى خلق را / چون نگيرى شاه غرب و شرق را
entries.english: You make friends with things because of the effect: why, then, are you ignorant of Him who produces effects?

entries.farsi: دوست گيرى چيزها را از اثر / پس چرا ز آثار بخشى بى‏خبر
entries.english: You make friends with people on the ground of a phantasy: why do not you make friends with the King of west and east?

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد اى قباد / حرص ما را اندر اين پايان مباد
entries.english: This topic has no end. O emperor, May there be no end to our desire for this!

sections.title_fa: رجوع به قصه‏ى رنجور
sections.title_en: Returning to the Story of the sick man.

entries.farsi: باز گرد و قصه‏ى رنجور گو / با طبيب آگه ستار خو
entries.english: Return and tell the story of the sick man and the wise physician whose nature was to palliate.

entries.farsi: نبض او بگرفت و واقف شد ز حال / ‏كه اميد صحت او بد محال
entries.english: He felt his pulse and ascertained his state: that it was absurd to hope for his recovery.

entries.farsi: هر چه خواهد خاطر تو وامگير / تا نگردد صبر و پرهيزت زحير
entries.english: He said, “Do whatever your heart desires, in order that this old malady may quit your body.

entries.farsi: گفت هر چت دل بخواهد آن بكن / ‏تا رود از جسمت اين رنج كهن
entries.english: Do not withhold anything that your inclination craves, lest your self-restraint and abstinence turn to gripes.

entries.farsi: صبر و پرهيز اين مرض را دان زيان / ‏هر چه خواهد دل در آرش در ميان
entries.english: Know that self-restraint and abstinence are injurious to this disease: proffer to your heart whatever it may desire.

entries.farsi: اين چنين رنجور را گفت اى عمو / ‏حق تعالى اعْ مَلُوا ما شئتم
entries.english: O uncle, in reference to a sick man like this God most High said, ‘Do what you will.”

entries.farsi: گفت رو هين خير بادت جان عم / ‏من تماشاى لب جو مى‏روم
entries.english: He said, “go; look you, my dear nephew, I am going for a walk on the bank of the river.”

entries.farsi: بر مراد دل همى‏گشت او بر آب / ‏تا كه صحت را بيابد فتح باب
entries.english: He was strolling beside the water, as his heart desired, in order that he might find the door to health opened to him.

entries.farsi: بر لب جو صوفيى بنشسته بود / دست‏ورو مى‏شست و پاكى مى‏فزود
entries.english: On the river-bank a Sufi was seated, washing his hands and face and cleansing himself more and more.

entries.farsi: او قفايش ديد چون تخييلى / ‏كرد او را آرزوى سيليى
entries.english: He saw the nape of his neck and, like a crazy man, felt a longing to give it a slap;

entries.farsi: بر قفاى صوفى حمزه پرست / ‏راست مى‏كرد از براى صفع دست
entries.english: He raised his hand to inflict a blow on the nape of the pottage-worshipping Sufi,

entries.farsi: كآرزو را گر نرانم تا رود / آن طبيبم گفت كآن علت شود
entries.english: Saying, “The physician told me it would make me ill if I would not let my desire have its way.

entries.farsi: سيلى‏اش اندر برم در معركه / ‏ز انكه ال تلقوا بايدى تهلكه
entries.english: I will give him a slap in quarrel, for, ‘Do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction.’

entries.farsi: تهلكه‏ست اين صبر و پرهيز اى فالن / ‏خوش بكوبش تن مزن چون ديگران
entries.english: O such-and-such, this self-restraint and abstinence is destruction: give him a good blow, do not keep quiet like the others.”

entries.farsi: چون زدش سيلى بر آمد يك طراق / ‏گفت صوفى هى‏هى اى قواد عاق
entries.english: When he slapped him, there was the sound of a crack: the Sufi cried, “Hey, hey, O rascally pimp!”

entries.farsi: خواست صوفى تا دو سه مشتش زند / سبلت و ريشش يكايك بر كند
entries.english: The Sufi was about to give him two or three blows with his fist and tear out his moustache and beard piecemeal.

entries.farsi: خلق رنجور دق و بى‏چاره‏اند / و ز خداع ديو سيلى‏باره‏اند
entries.english: Mankind suffers from a wasting disease, without remedy, and through the Devil’s deception they are passionately addicted to slapping.

entries.farsi: جمله در ايذاى بى‏جرمان حريص / ‏در قفاى همدگر جويان نقيص
entries.english: All are eager to injure the innocent and are seeking fault behind each others’ backs.

entries.farsi: اى زننده بى‏گناهان را قفا / در قفاى خود نمى‏بينى جزا
entries.english: O you who strike the necks of the guiltless, don’t you see the retribution behind you?

entries.farsi: اى هوا را طب خود پنداشته / ‏بر ضعيفان صفع را بگماشته
entries.english: O you who fancy that desire is your medicine and inflict slaps on the weak,

entries.farsi: بر تو خنديد آن كه گفتت اين دو است / ‏اوست كآدم را به گندم رهنماست
entries.english: He who told you that this is the cure mocked at you: it is he that guided Adam to the wheat,

entries.farsi: اوش لغزانيد و او را زد قفا / آن قفا واگشت و گشت اين را جزا
entries.english: Saying, “O you two who implore help, eat this grain as a remedy that you may abide in Paradise forever.”

entries.farsi: كه خوريد اين دانه اى دو مستعين / ‏بهر دارو تا تكونا خالدين
entries.english: He caused him to stumble and gave him a slap on the nape: that slap recoiled and became a retribution for him.

entries.farsi: اوش لغزانيد سخت اندر زلق / ‏ليك پشت و دستگيرش بود حق
entries.english: He caused him to stumble terribly in backsliding, but God was his support and helper.

entries.farsi: كوه بود آدم اگر پر مار شد / كان ترياق است و بى‏اضرار شد
entries.english: Adam was a mountain: if he was filled with serpents, he is a mine of the antidote and was unhurt.

entries.farsi: تو كه ترياقى ندارى ذره‏اى / ‏از خالص خود چرايى غره‏اى
entries.english: You, who do not possess an atom of the antidote, why are you deluded by your deliverance?

entries.farsi: آن توكل كو خليالنه ترا / و آن كرامت چون كليمت از كجا
entries.english: Where, in your case, is trust in God like Abraham, and when will you get the grace like Moses,

entries.farsi: تا نبرد تيغت اسماعيل را / تا كنى شه راه قعر نيل را
entries.english: So that your knife should not cut Ishmael and that you should make the depths of the Nile?

entries.farsi: گر سعيدى از مناره اوفتيد / بادش اندر جامه افتاد و رهيد
entries.english: If a blessed one fell from the minaret was saved by the wind filling his raiment,

entries.farsi: چون يقينت نيست آن بخت اى حسن / ‏تو چرا بر باد دادى خويشتن
entries.english: Why have you, O good man, committed yourself to the wind when you are not sure of that fortune?

entries.farsi: زين مناره صد هزاران همچو عاد / در فتادند و سر و سر باد داد
entries.english: From this minaret hundreds of thousands like Ad fell down and gave to the wind their lives and souls.

entries.farsi: سر نگون افتادگان را زين منار / مى‏نگر تو صد هزار اندر هزار
entries.english: Behold those who have fallen headlong from this minaret, hundreds of thousands on thousands!

entries.farsi: تو رسن بازى نمى‏دانى يقين / ‏شكر پاها گوى و مى‏رو بر زمين
entries.english: You have no sure skill in rope-dancing, give thanks for your feet and walk on the ground.

entries.farsi: پر مساز از كاغذ و از كه مپر / كه در آن سودا بسى رفته ست سر
entries.english: Don’t make wings of paper and fly from the mountain, for many a head has gone in this craze.

entries.farsi: گر چه آن صوفى پر آتش شد ز خشم / ‏ليك او بر عاقبت انداخت چشم
entries.english: Although the Sufi was afire with anger, yet he cast his eye on the consequence.

entries.farsi: آن ز پايان ديد احمد بود كاو / ديد دوزخ را همين جا مو به مو
entries.english: The highest success belongs permanently to him who does not take the bait and sees imprisonment in the trap.

entries.farsi: اول صف بر كسى ماند به كام / ‏كاو نگيرد دانه بيند بند دام
entries.english: How excellent are two noble end-discerning eyes that preserve the body from corruption!

entries.farsi: حبذا دو چشم پايان بين راد / كه نگه دارند تن را از فساد
entries.english: That was from the vision of the end that was seen by Ahmad, who even here saw Hell, hair by hair,

entries.farsi: ديد عرش و كرسى و جنات را / تا دريد او پرده‏ى غفالت را
entries.english: And saw the Throne and the Footstool and the Gardens, so that he rent the veil of forgetfulness.

entries.farsi: گر همى‏خواهى سالمت از ضرر / چشم از اول بند و پايان را نگر
entries.english: If you desire to be safe from harm, close your eye to the beginning and contemplate the end,

entries.farsi: اين ببين بارى كه هر كش عقل هست / ‏روز و شب در جستجوى نيست است
entries.english: That you may regard all nonentities as existent and look upon entities, perceived by the senses, as of low degree.

entries.farsi: تا عدمها را ببينى جمله هست / ‏هستها را بنگرى محسوس پست
entries.english: At least consider this, that everyone who possesses reason is daily and nightly in quest of the non-existent.

entries.farsi: در مزارع طالب دخلى كه نيست / ‏در مغارس طالب نخلى كه نيست
entries.english: In begging, he seeks a munificence that is not in being; in the shops he seeks a profit that is not in being.

entries.farsi: در گدايى طالب جودى كه نيست / ‏بر دكانها طالب سودى كه نيست
entries.english: In the cornfields he seeks an income that is not in being; in the plantations he seeks a date-palm that is not in being.

entries.farsi: در مدارس طالب علمى كه نيست / در صوامع طالب حلمى كه نيست
entries.english: In the colleges he seeks a knowledge that is not in being; in the Christian monasteries he seeks a morality that is not in being.

entries.farsi: هستها را سوى پس افكنده‏اند / نيستها را طالبند و بنده‏اند
entries.english: They have thrown the existent things behind them and are seekers of, and devoted to, the non-existent things,

entries.farsi: ز انكه كان و مخزن صنع خدا / نيست غير نيستى در انجال
entries.english: Because the mine and treasury of God’s doing is not other than non-existence in being brought into manifestation.

entries.farsi: پيش از اين رمزى بگفتستيم از اين / ‏اين و آن را تو يكى بين دو مبين
entries.english: We have previously given some indication of this: regard this and that as one, not as two.

entries.farsi: گفته شد كه هر صناعت گر كه رست / ‏در صناعت جايگاه نيست جست
entries.english: It was stated that every craftsman who appeared sought the abode of non-existence in his craft.

entries.farsi: جست بنا موضعى ناساخته / ‏گشته ويران سقفها انداخته
entries.english: The builder sought an unrepaired place that had become ruined and the roofs fallen in.

entries.farsi: جست سقا كوزه‏اى كش آب نيست / ‏و آن دروگر خانه‏اى كش باب نيست
entries.english: The water-carrier sought a pot with no water in it, and the carpenter a door less house.

entries.farsi: وقت صيد اندر عدم بد حمله شان / ‏از عدم آن گه گريزان جمله شان
entries.english: At the moment of pursuing they rushed into nonexistence; then they all are fleeing from non-existence.

entries.farsi: چون اميدت الست زو پرهيز چيست / ‏با انيس طمع خود استيز چيست
entries.english: Since your hope is non-existence, why avoidance of it? Why strife with what is congenial to your desire?

entries.farsi: چون انيس طمع تو آن نيستى است / ‏از فنا و نيست اين پرهيز چيست
entries.english: Since that non-existence is congenial to your desire, why this avoidance of nonentity and non-existence?

entries.farsi: گر انيس النه‏اى اى جان به سر / در كمين ال چرايى منتظر
entries.english: O soul, if you are not inwardly congenial to non-existence, why are you waiting in ambush for non-existence?

entries.farsi: ز آن كه دارى جمله دل بر كنده‏اى / ‏شست دل در بحر ال افكنده‏اى
entries.english: You have torn your heart away from all that you own; you have cast the net of your heart into the sea of non-existence.

entries.farsi: پس گريز از چيست زين بحر مراد / كه به شستت صد هزاران صيد داد
entries.english: Why, then, flee from this sea of desire that has put hundreds of thousands of prey into your net?

entries.farsi: از چه نام برگ را كردى تو مرگ / ‏جادويى بين كه نمودت مرگ برگ
entries.english: Why have you given the name “death” to food for the spirit? Observe the sorcery that has caused the provision to seem to you death. barg marg The magic of His doing has bound both your eyes, so that desire for the pit has come over your soul.

entries.farsi: هر دو چشمت بست سحر صنعتش / ‏تا كه جان را در چه آمد رغبتش
entries.english: Through the contrivance of the Creator, in its fancy all the expanse above the pit is poison and snakes;

entries.farsi: در خيال او ز مكر كردگار / جمله صحرا فوق چه زهر است و مار
entries.english: Consequently it has made the pit a refuge, so that death has cast it into the pit.

entries.farsi: الجرم چه را پناهى ساخته ست / ‏تا كه مرگ او را به چاه انداخته ست
entries.english: What I have said concerning your misapprehensions, O dear friend, hear also the utterance of Attar on this same.

sections.title_fa: قصه‏ى سلطان محمود و غالم هندو
sections.title_en: Story of Sultan Mahmud and the Hindu boy.

entries.farsi: آن چه گفتم از غلطهات اى عزيز / هم بر اين بشنو دم عطار نيز
entries.english: He, God have mercy upon him, has told it: he has strung together the tale of King Mahmud, the Ghazi—

entries.farsi: رحمه اللهَّ عليه گفته است / ‏ذكر شه محمود غازى سفته است
entries.english: How, amongst the booty of his campaign in India, a boy was brought into the presence of that sovereign.

entries.farsi: كز غزاى هند پيش آن همام / ‏در غنيمت اوفتادش يك غالم
entries.english: Afterwards he made him his vicegerent and seated him on the throne and gave him preferment above the army and called him “son.”

entries.farsi: پس خليفه‏ش كرد و بر تختش نشاند / بر سپه بگزيدش و فرزند خواند
entries.english: Seek the length and breadth and all particulars of the story in the discourse of that prince of the Faith.

entries.farsi: طول و عرض و وصف قصه تو به تو / در كالم آن بزرگ دين بجو
entries.english: In short, the lad was seated on this throne of gold beside the King-emperor.

entries.farsi: حاصل آن كودك بر اين تخت نضار / شسته پهلوى قباد شهريار
entries.english: He wept and shed tears in burning grief. The King said to him, “O you whose day is triumphant,

entries.farsi: از چه گريى دولتت شد ناگوار / فوق امالكى قرين شهريار
entries.english: Why should you weep? Has your fortune become disagreeable to you? You are above kings, the familiar companion of the Emperor.

entries.farsi: تو بر اين تخت و وزيران و سپاه / ‏پيش تختت صف زده چون نجم و ماه
entries.english: You are on this throne, while the viziers and soldiers are ranged in file before your throne, like the stars and the moon.”

entries.farsi: گفت كودك گريه‏ام ز آن است زار / كه مرا مادر در آن شهر و ديار
entries.english: The boy said, “The cause of my weeping bitterly is that in yonder city and country my mother

entries.farsi: از توام تهديد كردى هر زمان / ‏بينمت در دست محمود ارسالن
entries.english: Was always threatening me with you, ‘May I see you in the hands of the lion, Mahmud!’

entries.farsi: پس پدر مر مادرم را در جواب / ‏جنگ كردى كاين چه خشم است و عذاب
entries.english: Then my father would wrangle with my mother in reply, ‘What wrath and torment is this?

entries.farsi: مى‏نيابى هيچ نفرينى دگر / زين چنين نفرين مهلك سهلتر
entries.english: Cannot you find any other curse lighter than this deadly curse?

entries.farsi: سخت بى‏رحمى و بس سنگين دلى / ‏كه به صد شمشير او را قاتلى
entries.english: You are very pitiless and exceedingly hard-hearted, for you are killing him with a hundred swords.’

entries.farsi: من ز گفت هر دو حيران گشتمى / ‏در دل افتادى مرا بيم و غمى
entries.english: I used to be dismayed by the talk of both: a terror and pain would come into my heart,

entries.farsi: تا چه دوزخ خوست محمود اى عجب / ‏كه مثل گشته ست در ويل و كرب
entries.english: Oh, wonderful! What a hellish person Mahmud must be, since he has become proverbial for woe and anguish!’

entries.farsi: من همى‏لرزيدمى از بيم تو / غافل از اكرام و از تعظيم تو
entries.english: I used to tremble in fear of you, being ignorant of your gracious treatment and high regard.

entries.farsi: مادرم كو تا ببيند اين زمان / ‏مر مرا بر تخت اى شاه جهان
entries.english: Where is my mother, that she might see me now on the throne, O King of the world?”

entries.farsi: فقر آن محمود تست اى بى‏سعت / ‏طبع از او دايم همى‏ترساندت
entries.english: Spiritual poverty is your Mahmud, O man without affluence: your nature is always making you afraid of it.

entries.farsi: گر بدانى رحم اين محمود راد / خوش بگويى عاقبت محمود باد
entries.english: If you come to know the mercifulness of this noble Mahmud, you will cry joyously, “May the end be praised!”

entries.farsi: فقر آن محمود تست اى بيم دل / ‏كم شنو زين مادر طبع مضل
entries.english: Poverty is your Mahmud, O craven-hearted one: do not listen to this mother, namely, your misguiding nature.

entries.farsi: چون شكار فقر گردى تو يقين / ‏همچو كودك اشك بارى يوم دين
entries.english: When you become a prey to poverty, you will certainly shed tears, like the Hindu boy, on the Day of Judgment.

entries.farsi: گر چه اندر پرورش تن مادر است / ‏ليك از صد دشمنت دشمن تر است
entries.english: Although the body is a mother in fostering, yet it is more inimical to you than a hundred enemies.

entries.farsi: تن چو شد بيمار دارو جوت كرد / ور قوى شد مر ترا طاغوت كرد
entries.english: When your body falls ill it makes you seek medicine; and if it grows strong it makes you an outrageous devil.

entries.farsi: چون زره دان اين تن پر حيف را / نى شتا را شايد و نه صيف را
entries.english: Know that this iniquitous body is like a coat of mail: it serves neither for winter nor summer.

entries.farsi: يار بد نيكوست بهر صبر را / كه گشايد صبر كردن صدر را
entries.english: The bad associate is good because of the patience, for the exercise of patience expands the heart.

entries.farsi: صبر مه با شب منور داردش / ‏صبر گل با خار اذفر داردش
entries.english: The patience shown by the moon to the night keeps it illumined; the patience shown by the rose to the thorn keeps it fragrant.

entries.farsi: صبر شير اندر ميان فرث و خون / ‏كرده او را ناعش ابن اللبون
entries.english: The patience shown by the milk betwixt the feces and the blood enables it to rear the camel-foal till he has entered on his third year.

entries.farsi: صبر جمله‏ى انبيا با منكران / ‏كردشان خاص حق و صاحب قران
entries.english: The patience shown by all the prophets to the unbelievers made them the elect of God and lords of the planetary conjunction.

entries.farsi: هر كه را بينى يكى جامه‏ى درست / ‏دان كه او آن را به صبر و كسب جست
entries.english: When you see anyone wearing goodly raiment, know that he has gained it by patience and work.

entries.farsi: هر كه را ديدى برهنه و بى‏نوا / هست بر بى‏صبرى او آن گوا
entries.english: If you have seen any one naked and destitute, that is a testimony of his lack of patience.

entries.farsi: هر كه مستوحش بود پر غصه جان / ‏كرده باشد با دغايى اقتران
entries.english: Anyone who feels lonely and whose soul is full of anguish must have associated with an impostor.

entries.farsi: صبر اگر كردى و الف با وفا / از فراق او نخوردى اين قفا
entries.english: If he had shown patience and loyal friendship, he would not have suffered this affliction through being separated from Him.

entries.farsi: الجرم تنها نماندى همچنان / ‏كاتشى مانده به راه از كاروان
entries.english: He would have consorted with God as honey with milk, saying, “I love not them that set.”

entries.farsi: خوى با حق ساختى چون انگبين / ‏با لبن كه ال أُحِبُّ اآلفلين
entries.english: Assuredly he would not have remained alone, even as a fire left on the road by caravaneers.

entries.farsi: چون ز بى‏صبرى قرين غير شد / در فراقش پر غم و بى‏خير شد
entries.english: Since from lack of patience he associated himself with others, in separation from Him he became sorrowful and deprived of good.

entries.farsi: صحبتت چون هست زر ده دهى / ‏پيش خاين چون امانت مى‏نهى
entries.english: Since your friendship is as pure gold, how are you placing it in trust with a traitor?

entries.farsi: خوى با او كن كامانتهاى تو / ايمن آيد از افول و از عتو
entries.english: Consort with Him with whom your trusts are safe from loss and violation.

entries.farsi: بره‏اى بدهى رمه بازت دهد / پرورنده‏ى هر صفت خود رب بود
entries.english: Consort with Him who created nature and fostered the natures of the prophets.

entries.farsi: خوى با او كن كه خو را آفريد / خويهاى انبيا را پروريد
entries.english: If you give a lamb, He will give you back a flock: truly the Lord fosters every quality.

entries.farsi: بره پيش گرگ امانت مى‏نهى / ‏گرگ و يوسف را مفرما همرهى
entries.english: Will you entrust the lamb to the wolf? Do not tell the wolf and Joseph to travel in company with each other.

entries.farsi: گرگ اگر با تو نمايد روبهى / ‏هين مكن باور كه نايد زو بهى
entries.english: If the wolf show foxiness towards you, beware, do not believe, for no goodness comes from him.

entries.farsi: جاهل ار با تو نمايد هم دلى / ‏عاقبت زخمت زند از جاهلى
entries.english: If a churl shows sympathy towards you, in the end he will inflict blows upon you because of his churlishness.

entries.farsi: او دو آلت دارد و خنثى بود / فعل هر دو بى‏گمان پيدا شود
entries.english: He has two organs and is androgynous. The purpose of both organs is apparent.

entries.farsi: او ذكر را از زنان پنهان كند / تا كه خود را خواهر ايشان كند
entries.english: He hides his penis from women so he can appear to be one of them

entries.farsi: شله از مردان به كف پنهان كند / تا كه خود را جنس آن مردان كند
entries.english: He hides his vagina from men so that he claims to be one of them.

entries.farsi: گفت يزدان ز آن كس مكتوم او / شله‏اى سازيم بر خرطوم او
entries.english: God saw that from his hidden penis we would make a snout,

entries.farsi: تا كه بينايان ما ز آن ذو دالل / ‏درنيايند از فن او در جوال
entries.english: In order that Our seers may not be entrapped by the artfulness of that ogler.”

entries.farsi: حاصل آنك از هر ذكر نايد نرى / ‏هين ز جاهل ترس اگر دانش‏ورى
entries.english: The gist is that masculinity does not come from every male: beware of the ignorant man if you are wise.

entries.farsi: دوستى جاهل شيرين سخن / ‏كم شنو كان هست چون سم كهن
entries.english: Do not listen to the friendliness of the fair-spoken ignorant man, for it is like old poison.

entries.farsi: جان مادر چشم روشن گويدت / ‏جز غم و حسرت از آن نفزويدت
entries.english: He says to you, “O soul of your mother! O light of my eye!” from those only grief and sorrow are added to you.

entries.farsi: مر پدر را گويد آن مادر چهار / كه ز مكتب بچه‏ام شد بس نزار
entries.english: That mother says plainly to your father, “My child has grown very thin because of school.

entries.farsi: از زن ديگر گرش آورديى / ‏بر وى اين جور و جفا كم كرديى
entries.english: If you had gotten him by another wife, you would not have treated him with such cruelty and unkindness.”

entries.farsi: از جز تو گر بدى اين بچه‏ام / ‏اين فشار آن زن بگفتى نيز هم
entries.english: “Had this child of mine been of another, not of you, that wife too would have talked this nonsense.”

entries.farsi: هين بجه زين مادر و تيباى او / سيلى بابا به از حلواى او
entries.english: Beware, recoil from this mother and from her blandishments: your father’s slaps are better than her sweetmeat.

entries.farsi: هست مادر نفس و بابا عقل راد / اولش تنگى و آخر صد گشاد
entries.english: The mother is the carnal soul, and the father is noble reason: its beginning is constraint, but its end is a hundred expansions.

entries.farsi: اى دهنده‏ى عقلها فرياد رس / ‏تا نخواهى تو نخواهد هيچ كس
entries.english: O Giver of understandings, come to my help: none wills unless You will.

entries.farsi: هم طلب از تست و هم آن نيكويى / ‏ما كييم اول تويى آخر تويى
entries.english: Both the desire and the good action proceed from You: who are we? You are the First, You are the Last.

entries.farsi: هم بگو تو هم تو بشنو هم تو باش / ‏ما همه الشيم با چندين تراش
entries.english: Speak and hear and be! We are wholly nothing notwithstanding all this hewing.

entries.farsi: زين حواله رغبت افزا در سجود / كاهلى جبر مفرست و خمود
entries.english: Because of this resignation do You increase our desire for worship: do not send the sloth and stagnation of necessitarianism.

entries.farsi: جبر باشد پر و بال كامالن / ‏جبر هم زندان و بند كاهالن
entries.english: Necessitarianism is the wing and pinion of the perfect; necessitarianism is also the prison and chains of the slothful.

entries.farsi: همچو آب نيل دان اين جبر را / آب مومن را و خون مر گبر را
entries.english: Know that this necessitarianism is like the water of the Nile— water to the true believer and blood to the infidel.

entries.farsi: باز گرد اكنون تو در شرح عدم / ‏كه چو پازهر است و پنداريش سم
entries.english: Wings carry falcons to the king; wings carry crows to the graveyard.

entries.farsi: بال بازان را سوى سلطان برد / بال زاغان را به گورستان برد
entries.english: Now return to the description of non-existence, for it is like undigested animal fodder, though you think it is poison.

entries.farsi: همچو هندو بچه هين اى خواجه‏تاش / ‏رو ز محمود عدم ترسان مباش
entries.english: Listen, O fellow-servant, go and, like the Hindu boy, be not afraid of the Mahmud of non-existence.

entries.farsi: از وجودى ترس كاكنون در ويى / ‏آن خيالت الشى و تو الشيى
entries.english: Be afraid of the existence in which you are now: that phantasy of yours is nothing and you are nothing.

entries.farsi: الشيى بر الشيى عاشق شده ست / ‏هيچ نى مر هيچ نى را ره زده ست
entries.english: One nothing has fallen in love with another nothing: has any nothing ever waylaid any other nothing?

entries.farsi: چون برون شد اين خيالات از ميان / ‏گشت نامعقول تو بر تو عيان
entries.english: When these phantasies have departed from before you that which your understanding has not conceived becomes clear to you.

sections.title_fa: ‏ليس للماضين هم الموت انما لهم حسره الفوت
sections.title_en: Those who have passed away do not grieve on account of death; their only regret is to have missed the opportunities.

entries.farsi: راست گفته ست آن سپهدار بشر / كه هر آن كه كرد از دنيا گذر
entries.english: That captain of mankind has said truly that no one who has passed away from this world

entries.farsi: نيستش درد و دريغ و غبن موت / ‏بلكه هستش صد دريغ از بهر فوت
entries.english: Feels sorrow and regret and disappointment on account of death; nay, but he feels a hundred regrets for having missed the opportunity,

entries.farsi: كه چرا قبله نكردم مرگ را / مخزن هر دولت و هر برگ را
entries.english: Saying, “Why did not I make death my object — the store-house of every fortune and every provision—

entries.farsi: قبله كردم من همه عمر از حول / ‏آن خيالاتى كه گم شد در اجل
entries.english: Tthrough seeing double, did I make the lifelong object of my attention those phantoms that vanished at the fated hour?”

entries.farsi: حسرت آن مردگان از مرگ نيست / ‏ز آنست كاندر نقشها كرديم ايست
entries.english: The grief of the dead is not on account of death; it is because “we dwelt upon the forms,

entries.farsi: ما نديديم اين كه آن نقش است و كف / ‏كف ز دريا جنبد و يابد علف
entries.english: And this we did not perceive, that those are form and foam, the foam is moved and fed by the Sea.”

entries.farsi: چون كه بحر افكند كفها را به بر / تو به گورستان رو آن كفها نگر
entries.english: When the Sea has cast the foam-flakes on the shore, go to the graveyard and behold those flakes of foam!

entries.farsi: پس بگو كو جنبش و جوالنتان / ‏بحر افكنده ست در بحرانتان
entries.english: Then say, “Where is your movement and gyration? The Sea has cast you into the crisis”

entries.farsi: تا بگويندت به لب نى بل به حال / ‏كه ز دريا كن نه از ما اين سؤال
entries.english: In order that they may say to you, not with their lips but implicitly, “Ask this question of the Sea, not of us.”

entries.farsi: نقش چون كف كى بجنبد بى‏ز موج / ‏خاك بى‏بادى كجا آيد بر اوج
entries.english: How should the foam-like form move without the wave? How should the dust rise to the zenith without a wind?

entries.farsi: هين ببين كز تو نظر آيد به كار / باقيت شحمى و لحمى پود و تار
entries.english: Since you have perceived the dust, namely, the form, perceive the wind; since you have perceived the foam, perceive the ocean of Creative Energy.

entries.farsi: چون غبار نقش ديدى باد بين / ‏كف چو ديدى قلزم ايجاد بين
entries.english: Come, perceive, for insight in you avails: the rest of you is a piece of fat and flesh, a weft and warp.

entries.farsi: شحم تو در شمعها نفزود تاب / ‏لحم تو مخمور را نامد كباب
entries.english: Your fat never increased the light in candles, your flesh never became roasted meat for any one drunk with wine.

entries.farsi: در گداز اين جمله تن را در بصر / در نظر رو در نظر رو در نظر
entries.english: Dissolve the whole of this body of yours in vision: pass into sight, pass into sight, into sight!

entries.farsi: يك نظر دو گز همى‏بيند ز راه / ‏يك نظر دو كون ديد و روى شاه
entries.english: One sight perceives two yards of the road; another sight has beheld the two worlds and the Face of the King.

entries.farsi: در ميان اين دو فرقى بى‏شمار / سرمه جو و اهلل اعلم بالسرار
entries.english: Between these twain there is an incalculable difference: seek the eye salve—and God best knows the hidden things.

entries.farsi: چون شنيدى شرح بحر نيستى / ‏كوش دايم تا بر اين بحر ايستى
entries.english: Since you have heard the description of the sea of non-existence, continually endeavour to stand upon this sea.

entries.farsi: چون كه اصل كارگاه آن نيستى است / ‏كه خال و بى‏نشان است و تهى است
entries.english: Inasmuch as the foundation of the workshop is that non-existence which is void and traceless and empty,

entries.farsi: جمله استادان پى اظهار كار / نيستى جويند و جاى انكسار
entries.english: All master-craftsmen seek non-existence and a place of breakage for the purpose of exhibiting their skill,

entries.farsi: الجرم استاد استادان صمد / كارگاهش نيستى و ال بود
entries.english: Necessarily the Lord the Master of masters— His workshop is non-existence and nothingness.

entries.farsi: هر كجا اين نيستى افزون تر است / ‏كار حق و كارگاهش آن سر است
entries.english: Wherever this non-existence is greater, in that quarter is the work and workshop of God.

entries.farsi: نيستى چون هست بالايين طبق / ‏بر همه بردند درويشان سبق
entries.english: Since the highest stage is non-existence, the dervishes have outstripped all,

entries.farsi: خاصه درويشى كه شد بى‏جسم و مال / ‏كار فقر جسم دارد نه سؤال
entries.english: Especially the dervish that has become devoid of body and goods: poverty of body is the matter, not beggary.

entries.farsi: سائل آن باشد كه مال او گداخت / ‏قانع آن باشد كه جسم خويش باخت
entries.english: The beggar is he whose goods have melted away; the contented man is he who has gambled away his body.

entries.farsi: پس ز درد اكنون شكايت بر مدار / كاوست سوى نيست اسبى راهوار
entries.english: Therefore do not now complain of affliction, for it is a smooth-paced horse towards non-existence.

entries.farsi: اين قدر گفتيم باقى فكر كن / ‏فكر اگر جامد بود رو ذكر كن
entries.english: We have said so much: think of the remainder, if thought be frozen, practise recollection.

entries.farsi: ذكر آرد فكر را در اهتزاز / ذكر را خورشيد اين افسرده ساز
entries.english: Recollection brings thought into movement: make recollection to be the sun for this congealed.

entries.farsi: اصل خود جذب است ليك اى خواجه‏تاش / ‏كار كن موقوف آن جذبه مباش
entries.english: God’s pulling is, indeed, the original source; but, O fellow-servant, exert yourself, do not be dependent on that pulling;

entries.farsi: ز انكه ترك كار چون نازى بود / ناز كى در خورد جان‏بازى بود
entries.english: For to renounce exertion is like an act of disdain: how should disdain be seemly for a devoted lover?

entries.farsi: مرغ جذبه ناگهان پرد ز عش / ‏چون بديدى صبح شمع آن گه بكش
entries.english: O youth, think neither of acceptance nor refusal: regard always the command and prohibition.

entries.farsi: نه قبول انديش نه رد اى غالم / ‏امر را و نهى را مى‏بين مدام
entries.english: Suddenly the bird, namely, the attraction, will fly from its nest: put out the candle as soon as you see the dawn.

entries.farsi: چشمها چون شد گذاره نور اوست / ‏مغزها مى‏بيند او در عين پوست
entries.english: When the eyes have become piercing, it is its light: in the very husk it beholds the kernels.

entries.farsi: بيند اندر ذره خورشيد بقا / بيند اندر قطره كل بحر را
entries.english: In the mote it beholds the everlasting Sun, in the drop it beholds the entire Sea.

sections.title_fa: ‏بار ديگر رجوع كردن به قصه‏ى صوفى و قاضى
sections.title_en: Returning once more to the Story of the Sufi and the Cadi

entries.farsi: گفت صوفى در قصاص يك قفا / ‏سر نشايد باد دادن از عمى
entries.english: The Sufi said, “It does not behove me blindly to lose my head by taking retaliation for a single slap on the nape.

entries.farsi: خرقه‏ى تسليم اندر كردنم / ‏بر من آسان كرد سيلى خوردنم
entries.english: My putting on the mantle of resignation has made it easy for me to suffer blows.”

entries.farsi: ديد صوفى خصم خود را سخت زار / گفت اگر مشتش زنم من خصم‏وار
entries.english: The Sufi observed that his adversary was exceedingly frail: he said, “If I give him a hostile blow with my fist,

entries.farsi: او به يك مشتم بريزد چون رصاص / ‏شاه فرمايد مرا زجر و قصاص
entries.english: At my first blow he will crumble like dead, and then the king will punish me and exact retaliation.

entries.farsi: خيمه ويران است و بشكسته وتد / او بهانه مى‏جود تا در فتد
entries.english: The tent is ruined and the tent-pin broken: it is seeking excuse to collapse.

entries.farsi: بهر اين مرده دريغ آيد دريغ / ‏كه قصاصم افتد اندر زير تيغ
entries.english: It would be a pity, a pity that on account of this dead man retaliation should fall upon me under the sword.”

entries.farsi: چون نمى‏تانست كف بر خصم زد / عزمش آن شد كش سوى قاضى برد
entries.english: Since he dares not give his adversary a fisticuff, he resolved to take him to the Cadi.

entries.farsi: كه ترازوى حق است و كيله‏اش / ‏مخلص است از مكر ديو و حيله‏اش
entries.english: Saying, “He is God’s scales and measure; he is the means of deliverance from the deceit and cunning of the Devil.

entries.farsi: هست او مقراض احقاد و جدال / ‏قاطع جنگ دو خصم و قيل و قال
entries.english: He is the scissors for enmities and wrangling, he is the decider of the quarrels and disputes of the two litigants.

entries.farsi: ديو در شيشه كند افسون او / فتنه‏ها ساكن كند قانون او
entries.english: His spells put the Devil in the bottle, his legal ruling makes dissensions cease.

entries.farsi: چون ترازو ديد خصم پر طمع / ‏سركشى بگذارد و گردد تبع
entries.english: When the covetous adversary sees the scales, he abandons rebelliousness and becomes submissive;

entries.farsi: ور ترازو نيست گر افزون دهيش / ‏از قسم راضى نگردد آگهيش
entries.english: But if there are no scales though you give him more his shrewdness will never be satisfied with the portion.”

entries.farsi: هست قاضى رحمت و دفع ستيز / قطره‏اى از بحر عدل رستخيز
entries.english: The cadi is a mercy and the means of removing strife: he is a drop from the ocean of the justice of the Resurrection.

entries.farsi: از غبار ار پاك دارى كله را / تو ز يك قطره ببينى دجله را
entries.english: Though the drop be small and short of foot, by it the purity of the ocean’s water is made manifest.

entries.farsi: جزوها بر حال كلها شاهد است / ‏تا شفق غماز خورشيد آمده ست
entries.english: If you keep the veil free from dust, you will see the Tigris in a single drop.

entries.farsi: قطره گر چه خرد و كوته پا بود / لطف آب بحر از او پيدا بود
entries.english: The parts bear witness to the state of wholes, so that the afterglow of sunset has become an informer concerning the sun.

entries.farsi: آن قسم بر جسم احمد راند حق / ‏آن چه فرموده ست كال و الشفق
entries.english: God applied that oath, His Words Truly by the afterglow of sunset, to the body of Ahmad.

entries.farsi: مور بر دانه چرا لرزان بدى / ‏گر از آن يك دانه خرمن دان بدى
entries.english: Why should the ant have been trembling for the grain, if from that single grain it had known the stack?

entries.farsi: بر سر حرف آ كه صوفى بى‏دل است / ‏در مكافات جفا مستعجل است
entries.english: Come to the topic, for the Sufi is distraught and is making haste to exact redress for the injury.

entries.farsi: اى تو كرده ظلمها چون خوش دلى / ‏از تقاضاى مكافى غافلى
entries.english: O you that has committed deeds of injustice, how are you glad at heart? Are you unaware of the demand by him who exacts the penalty?

entries.farsi: يا فراموشت شده ست از كرده‏هات / ‏كه فرو آويخت غفلت پرده‏هات
entries.english: Or have you forgotten those deeds of your, since heedlessness has let down curtains over you?

entries.farsi: گر نه خصميهاستى اندر قفات / ‏جرم گردون رشك بردى بر صفات
entries.english: If there were no litigations pursuing you the celestial orb would envy your happiness,

entries.farsi: ليك محبوسى براى آن حقوق / ‏اندك اندك عذر مى‏خواه از عقوق
entries.english: But on account of those just claims you are embarrassed. Little by little, beg to be excused for your unrighteousness.

entries.farsi: تا به يك بارت نگيرد محتسب / ‏آب خود روشن كن اكنون با محب
entries.english: Lest the Inspector suddenly arrest you, now make your water clear towards the lover.

entries.farsi: رفت صوفى سوى آن سيلى زنش / ‏دست زد چون مدعى در دامنش
entries.english: The Sufi went to the man who had slapped him, and laid hold of his skirt like a plaintiff.

entries.farsi: اندر آوردش بر قاضى كشان / ‏كاين خر ادبار را بر خر نشان
entries.english: Haling him along, he brought him to the Cadi, saying, “Mount this asinine miscreant on an ass,

entries.farsi: يا به زخم دره او را ده جزا / آن چنان كه راى تو بيند سزا
entries.english: Or punish him with blows of the whip, according as your judgment may deem fitting;

entries.farsi: كان كه از زجر تو ميرد در دمار / بر تو تاوان نيست آن باشد جبار
entries.english: For one who dies under your chastisement, no fine is on you in vengeance: that is not penalised.”

entries.farsi: در حد و تعزير قاضى هر كه مرد / نيست بر قاضى ضمان كاو نيست خرد
entries.english: When any one has died under the punishment and flagellation of the cadi no responsibility lies on the cadi, for he is not a person of small account.

entries.farsi: كاو ادب از بهر مظلومى كند / نه براى عرض و خشم و دخل خود
entries.english: He is God’s deputy and the shadow of God’s justice, the mirror of every plaintiff and defendant;

entries.farsi: نايب حق است و سايه‏ى عدل حق / ‏آينه‏ى هر مستحق و مستحق
entries.english: For he inflicts correction for the sake of one who has been wronged, not for the sake of his honour or his anger or his income.

entries.farsi: چون براى حق و روز آجله ست / ‏گر خطايى شد ديت بر عاقله است
entries.english: Since it is for the sake of God and the Day hereafter, if a mistake is made the blood-wit falls upon the kinsmen on the father’s side.

entries.farsi: آن كه بهر خود زند او ضامن است / ‏وان كه بهر حق زند او آمن است
entries.english: He who strikes for his own sake is responsible, while he who strikes for God’s sake is secure.

entries.farsi: گر پدر زد مر پسر را او بمرد / آن پدر را خون بها بايد شمرد
entries.english: If a father strikes his son and he dies, the father must pay the blood-price,

entries.farsi: ز انكه او را بهر كار خويش زد / خدمت او هست واجب بر ولد
entries.english: Because he struck him for his own benefit, it is the duty of the son to serve him.

entries.farsi: چون معلم زد صبى را شد تلف / ‏بر معلم نيست چيزى ال تخف
entries.english: When a teacher strikes a boy and he perishes, nothing is on the teacher; no fear’

entries.farsi: كان معلم نايب افتاد و امين / ‏هر امين را هست حكمش همچنين
entries.english: For the teacher is a deputy and a trustee; and the ease of every trustee is the same as this

entries.farsi: نيست واجب خدمت استا بر او / پس نبود استا به زجرش كار جو
entries.english: It is not his duty to serve his master therefore in chastising him the master was not seeking benefit,

entries.farsi: ور پدر زد او براى خود زده ست / ‏الجرم از خون بها دادن نرست
entries.english: But if his father struck him, he struck for his own sake: consequently he was not freed from paying the blood-price.

entries.farsi: پس خودى را سر ببر اى ذو الفقار / بى‏خودى شو فانيى درويش‏وار
entries.english: Behead selfhood, then, O Dhu ‘l-faqár: become a selfless non-existent one like the dervish.

entries.farsi: چون شدى بى‏خود هر آن چه تو كنى / ‏ما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ ايمنى
entries.english: When you have become selfless, everything that you do you did not throw when you threw, you are safe.

entries.farsi: آن ضمان بر حق بود نه بر امين / ‏هست تفصيلش به فقه اندر مبين
entries.english: The responsibility lies on God, not on the trustee: it is set forth plainly in jurisprudence.

entries.farsi: در دكان كفشگر چرم است خوب / ‏قالب كفش است اگر بينى تو چوب
entries.english: Every shop has a different merchandise: the Masnavi is the shop for poverty, O son.

entries.farsi: هر دكانى راست سودايى دگر / مثنوى دكان فقر است اى پسر
entries.english: In the shoemaker’s shop there is fine leather: if you see wood, it is the mould for the shoe.

entries.farsi: پيش بزازان قز و ادكن بود / بهر گز باشد اگر آهن بود
entries.english: The drapers have silk and dun-coloured cloth: if iron be, it is for a yard-measure.

entries.farsi: مثنوى ما دكان وحدت است / ‏غير واحد هر چه بينى آن بت است
entries.english: Our Masnavi is the shop for Unity: anything that you see except the One is an idol

entries.farsi: بت ستودن بهر دام عامه را / ‏همچنان دان كالغرانيق العلى
entries.english: Know that to praise an idol for the purpose of ensnaring the vulgar is just like “the most exalted Cranes

entries.farsi: جمله كفار آن زمان ساجد شدند / هم سرى بود آن كه سر بر در زدند
entries.english: He recited it those words quickly in the Súra Wa’l-Najm, but it was a temptation, it was not part of the Súra.

entries.farsi: خواندش در سوره‏ى و النجم زود / ليك آن فتنه بد از سوره نبود
entries.english: Thereupon all the infidels prostrated themselves: it was a mystery, too, that they knocked their heads upon the door.

entries.farsi: بعد از اين حرفى است پيچا پيچ و دور / با سليمان باش و ديوان را مشور
entries.english: After this there is a perplexing and abstruse argument stay with Solomon and do not stir up the demons!

entries.farsi: هين حديث صوفى و قاضى بيار / و آن ستمكار ضعيف زار زار
entries.english: Listen; relate the story of the Sufi and the Cadi and the offender who was feeble and wretchedly ill.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى ثبت العرش اى پسر / تا بر او نقشى كنم از خير و شر
entries.english: The Cadi said, “Make the roof firm, O son, in order that I may decorate it with good and evil

entries.farsi: كو زننده كو محل انتقام / ‏اين خيالى گشته است اندر سقام
entries.english: Where is the assailant? Where is that which is subject to vengeance? This man in sickness has become a phantom.

entries.farsi: شرع بهر زندگان و اغنياست / ‏شرع بر اصحاب گورستان كجاست
entries.english: The law is for the living and self-sufficient: where is the law upon the occupants of the graveyard?”

entries.farsi: آن گروهى كز فقيرى بى‏سرند / صد جهت ز آن مردگان فانى‏ترند
entries.english: Those who are headless because of poverty are in a hundred respects more non-existent than those dead.

entries.farsi: مرده از يك روست فانى در گزند / صوفيان از صد جهت فانى شدند
entries.english: The dead man is non-existent from one point of view, as regards loss; the Sufis have been non-existent in a hundred respects.

entries.farsi: مرگ يك قتل است و اين سيصد هزار / هر يكى را خونبهايى بى‏شمار
entries.english: Death is a single killing, while this is three hundred thousand, for each one of which there is a blood-price beyond reckoning.

entries.farsi: گر چه كشت اين قوم را حق بارها / ريخت بهر خونبها انبارها
entries.english: Though God has killed these folk many a time, He has poured forth stores in payment of the blood-price.

entries.farsi: همچو جرجيس‏اند هر يك در سرار / كشته گشته زنده گشته شصت بار
entries.english: Every one is inwardly like Jirjis: they have been killed and brought to life sixty times.

entries.farsi: كشته از ذوق سنان دادگر / مى‏بسوزد كه بزن زخمى دگر
entries.english: From his delight in the spear-point of the Judge, the killed one is ever burning and crying. Strike another blow!”

entries.farsi: و اهلل از عشق وجود جان پرست / ‏كشته بر قتل دوم عاشق‏تر است
entries.english: By God, from love for the existence that fosters the spirit, the killed one longs - more passionately to be killed a second time.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى من قضا دار حى‏ام / ‏حاكم اصحاب گورستان كى‏ام
entries.english: The Cadi said, “I am the cadi for the living: how am I the judge of the occupants of the graveyard?

entries.farsi: اين به صورت گر نه در گور است پست / ‏گورها در دودمانش آمده ست
entries.english: If to outward seeming this man is not laid low in the grave; graves have entered into his household

entries.farsi: بس بديدى مرده اندر گور تو / گور را در مرده بين اى كور تو
entries.english: You have seen many a dead man in the grave, O, blind one, see the grave in a dead man.

entries.farsi: گر ز گورى خشت بر تو اوفتاد / عاقالن از گور كى خواهند داد
entries.english: If bricks from a grave have fallen on you, how should reason able persons seek redress from the grave?

entries.farsi: خشم احيا خشم حق و زخم اوست / ‏كه به حق زنده ست آن پاكيزه پوست
entries.english: Do not concern yourself with anger and hatred against a dead man: beware, do not wake war on the pictures in a bath-house.

entries.farsi: شكر كن كه زنده‏اى بر تو نزد / كان كه زنده رد كند حق كرد رد
entries.english: Give thanks that a living one did not strike you, for he whom the living one rejects is rejected of God.

entries.farsi: گرد خشم و كينه‏ى مرده مگرد / هين مكن با نقش گرمابه نبرد
entries.english: The anger of the living ones is God’s anger and His blows for that pure-skinned one are living through God.

entries.farsi: حق بكشت او را و در پاچه ش دميد / زود قصابانه پوست از وى كشيد
entries.english: God killed him and breathed on his trotters and quickly, like a butcher, stripped off his skin.

entries.farsi: نفخ در وى باقى آمد تا مآب / ‏نفخ حق نبود چو نفخه‏ى آن قصاب
entries.english: The breath remains in him till the final boundary: the breathing of God is not as the breathing of the butcher.

entries.farsi: فرق بسيار است بين النفختين / ‏اين همه زين است و آن سر جمله شين
entries.english: There is a great difference between the two breathings: this is wholly honour, while that side is entirely, shame.

entries.farsi: اين حيات از وى بريد و شد مضر / و آن حيات از نفخ حق شد مستمر
entries.english: This took life away from it and injured it, while by the breathing of God that life was made perpetual.

entries.farsi: اين دم آن دم نيست كايد آن به شرح / ‏هين بر آ زين قعر چه بالاى صرح
entries.english: This breath is not a breath ‘that can be described listen, come up from the bottom of the pit to the top of the palace.

entries.farsi: نيستش بر خر نشاندن مجتهد / نقش هيزم را كسى بر خر نهد
entries.english: It is not a sound legal decision to mount him on an ass: does anyone lay upon an ass a picture of firewood?

entries.farsi: بر نشست او نه پشت خر سزد / پشت تابوتيش اوليتر سزد
entries.english: The back of an ass is not his proper seat: the back of a bier is more fitting for him.

entries.farsi: ظلم چه بود وضع غير موضعش / ‏هين مكن در غير موضع ضايعش
entries.english: What is injustice? To put out of its proper place: beware, do not let it be lost out of its place.”

entries.farsi: گفت صوفى پس روا دارى كه او / سيلى‏ام زد بى‏قصاص و بى‏تسو
entries.english: The Sufi said, “Then do you think it right for him to slap me without retaliation and without a farthing?

entries.farsi: اين روا باشد كه خر خرسى قالش / ‏صوفيان را صفع اندازد به الش
entries.english: Is it right that a big rascally bear should inflict slaps on Sufis for nothing?”

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى تو چه دارى بيش و كم / ‏گفت دارم در جهان من شش درم
entries.english: The Cadi said, “What have you, larger or smaller?” He replied, “I have six dirhems in the world.”

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى سه درم تو خرج كن / ‏آن سه ديگر را به او ده بى‏سخن
entries.english: Said the Cadi, “Spend three dirhems and give the other three to him without words.

entries.farsi: زار و رنجور است و درويش و ضعيف / ‏سه درم در بايدش تره و رغيف
entries.english: “He is weak and ill and poor and infirm: he will need three dirhems for vegetables and loaves.”

entries.farsi: بر قفاى قاضى افتادش نظر / از قفاى صوفى آن بد خوبتر
entries.english: His eye fall on the nape of the Cadi’s neck: it was better than the nape of the Sufi.

entries.farsi: راست مى‏كرد از پى سيليش دست / ‏كه قصاص سيلى‏ام ارزان شده است
entries.english: He raised his hand to slap it, saying, “The retaliation for my slap has been made cheap.”

entries.farsi: سوى گوش قاضى آمد بهر راز / سيليى آورد قاضى را فراز
entries.english: He approached the Cadi’s ear for the purpose of a secret, and dealt the Cadi a blow with his palm.

entries.farsi: گفت هر شش را بياريد اى دو خصم / ‏من شوم آزاد بى‏خرخاش و وصم
entries.english: “O my two enemies,” he cried, “take all the six dirhems: I shall be free without trouble and anxiety.”

sections.title_fa: طيره شدن قاضى از سيلى درويش و سرزنش كردن صوفى قاضى را
sections.title_en: How the Cadi was incensed by the slap of the poor man and how the Sufi taunted the Cadi.

entries.farsi: گشت قاضى طيره صوفى گفت هى / ‏حكم تو عدل است ال شك نيست غى
entries.english: The Cadi was incensed. “Hey,” cried the Sufi, “your decision is just, no doubt: there is no error.

entries.farsi: آن چه نپسندى به خود اى شيخ دين / ‏چون پسندى بر برادر اى امين
entries.english: O Shaykh of the religion, how can you approve for a brother what you disapprove for yourself, O man of trust?

entries.farsi: اين ندانى كه پى من چه كنى / ‏هم در آن چه عاقبت خود افكنى
entries.english: Don’t you know this; that you dig a pit for me you will at last let yourself fall into the same pit?

entries.farsi: من حفر بئرا نخواندى از خبر / آن چه خواندى كن عمل جان پدر
entries.english: Haven’t you read in the Traditions, ‘Whoever digs a pit’? Practise what you have read, O soul of your father!

entries.farsi: اين يكى حكمت چنين بد در قضا / كه ترا آورد سيلى بر قفا
entries.english: This one judicial decision of yours was like this, for it has brought you a slap on the nape.

entries.farsi: واى بر احكام ديگرهاى تو / تا چه آرد بر سر و بر پاى تو
entries.english: Alas for your other decisions! What they will bring upon your head and feet.

entries.farsi: دست ظالم را ببر چه جاى آن / ‏كه به دست او نهى حكم و عنان
entries.english: From kindness you take pity on a wrong-doer, saying, ‘May you have three dirhems to spend!’

entries.farsi: ظالمى را رحم آرى از كرم / ‏كه براى نفقه بادت سه درم
entries.english: Cut off the wrong-doer’s hand: what occasion is there for you to put the control and reins in his hand?

entries.farsi: تو بدان بز مانى اى مجهول داد / كه نژاد گرگ را او شير داد
entries.english: O you from whom justice is unknown, you resemble the goat that gave her milk to the wolf-cub.”

sections.title_fa: جواب دادن قاضى صوفى را
sections.title_en: The Cadi’s reply to the Sufi.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى واجب آيدمان رضا / هر قفا و هر جفا كارد قضا
entries.english: The Cadi said, “It is our duty to acquiesce, whatever slap or cruelty the destiny may bring to pass.

entries.farsi: خوش دلم در باطن از حكم زبر / گر چه شد رويم ترش كالحق مر
entries.english: I am inwardly pleased with the decision in the Scrolls, though my face has become sour—for Truth is bitter.

entries.farsi: اين دلم باغ است و چشمم ابروش / ‏ابر گريد باغ خندد شاد و خوش
entries.english: This heart of mine is an orchard, and my eye is like the cloud: the cloud weeps the orchard laughs joyously and happily.

entries.farsi: ز امر حق و ابكوا كثيرا خوانده‏اى / ‏چون سر بريان چه خندان مانده‏اى
entries.english: In a year of drought the orchards are reduced to death and agony by the sun laughing unconscionably.

entries.farsi: سال قحط از آفتاب خيره خند / باغها در مرگ و جان كندن رسند
entries.english: You have read in God’s Commandment and weep much: why have you remained grinning like a roast head?

entries.farsi: روشنى خانه باشى همچو شمع / ‏گر فرو پاشى تو همچون شمع دمع
entries.english: You will be the light of the house, like the candle, if like the candle you shed showers of tears.

entries.farsi: آن ترش رويى مادر يا پدر / حافظ فرزند شد از هر ضرر
entries.english: The mother’s or father’s sourness of face preserves the child from all harm.

entries.farsi: ذوق خنده ديده‏اى اى خيره خند / ذوق گريه بين كه هست آن كان قند
entries.english: You have experienced the pleasure of laughing, O inordinate laugher: experience the pleasure of weeping that it is a mine of sugar.

entries.farsi: چون جهنم گريه آرد ياد آن / ‏پس جهنم خوشتر آيد از جنان
entries.english: Since thinking of Hell causes weeping, therefore Hell is better than Paradise.

entries.farsi: خنده‏ها در گريه‏ها آمد كتيم / ‏گنج در ويرانه‏ها جو اى سليم
entries.english: In tears there is concealed laughter: seek treasure amidst ruins, O simple man.

entries.farsi: ذوق در غمهاست پى گم كرده‏اند / آب حيوان را به ظلمت برده‏اند
entries.english: Pleasure is in pains: the track has been lost, the Water of Life has been taken away into the Darkness.

entries.farsi: باژگونه نعل در ره تا رباط / چشمها را چار كن در احتياط
entries.english: On the way to the Caravanseray the shoes are upside down: make your eyes to be four in precaution.

entries.farsi: چشمها را چار كن در اعتبار / يار كن با چشم خود دو چشم يار
entries.english: Make your eyes to be four in careful consideration: join to your own eye the two eyes of the Friend.

entries.farsi: أَمْرُهُمْ شُورى‏ بخوان اندر صحف / ‏يار را باش و مگوش از ناز اف
entries.english: Read in the pages their affair is a matter for consultation: be to the Friend and do not say to him disdainfully, ‘Fie!’

entries.farsi: يار باشد راه را پشت و پناه / ‏چون كه نيكو بنگرى يار است راه
entries.english: The Friend is the support and refuge on the Way: when you consider well; the Friend is the Way.

entries.farsi: چون كه در ياران رسى خامش نشين / ‏اندر آن حلقه مكن خود را نگين
entries.english: When you come into a company of Mends, sit silent: do not make yourself the bezel in that ring.

entries.farsi: در نماز جمعه بنگر خوش به هوش / ‏جمله جمعند و يك انديش و خموش
entries.english: At the Friday prayer-service look well and attentively: all are concentrated and possessed by a single ought and silent.

entries.farsi: رختها را سوى خاموشى كشان / ‏چون نشان جويى مكن خود را نشان
entries.english: Direct your course towards silence: when you seek the marks, do not make yourself a mark.

entries.farsi: گفت پيغمبر كه در بحر هموم / ‏در داللت دان تو ياران را نجوم
entries.english: The Prophet said, ‘Know that amidst the sea of cares Companions are stars in respect of guidance.’

entries.farsi: چشم در استارگان نه ره بجو / نطق تشويش نظر باشد مگو
entries.english: Fix your eye on the stars, seek the Way; speech is a cause of confusion to the sight: do not speak.

entries.farsi: گر دو حرف صدق گويى اى فالن / ‏گفت تيره در تبع گردد روان
entries.english: If you utter two true words, O such-and-such, the dark speech will begin to flow in their train.

entries.farsi: اين نخواندى كالكالم اى مستهام / ‏فى شجون جره جر الكالم
entries.english: Haven’t you read that talk concerning grief, O frenzied is drawn along by the draw of talk?

entries.farsi: هين مشو شارع در آن حرف رشد / كه سخن زو مر سخن را مى‏كشد
