entries.english: Beware; do not begin those right words, for words quickly draw words.

entries.farsi: آن كه معصوم ره وحى خداست / ‏چون همه صاف است بگشايد رواست
entries.english: When you have opened your mouth, they are not in your control: the dark flows on the heels of the pure.

entries.farsi: نيست در ضبطت چو بگشادى دهان / ‏از پى صافى شود تيره روان
entries.english: He may open who is preserved in the way of inspiration it is permissible since he is entirely pure

entries.farsi: خويشتن را ساز منطيقى ز حال / ‏تا نگردى همچو من سخره‏ى مقال
entries.english: For a prophet does not speak from self-will: how should self-will proceed from him who is preserved by God?

entries.farsi: ز انكه ما ينطق رسول بالهوى / كى هوا زايد ز معصوم خدا
entries.english: Make yourself one that speaks eloquently from ecstatic feeling, lest you become a slave to argumentation like me.”

sections.title_fa: سؤال كردن آن صوفى قاضى را
sections.title_en: How the Sufi questioned the Cadi.

entries.farsi: گفت صوفى چون ز يك كان است زر / اين چرا نفع است و آن ديگر ضرر
entries.english: The Sufi said, “Since the gold is from a single mine, why is this beneficial and that other harmful?

entries.farsi: چون كه جمله از يكى دست آمده ست / ‏اين چرا هشيار و آن مست آمده ست
entries.english: Since the whole has come from a single Hand, why has has this one come sober and that one intoxicated?

entries.farsi: چون ز يك درياست اين جوها روان / ‏اين چرا نوش است و آن زهر دهان
entries.english: Since these rivers flow from a single Sea, why is this one honey and that one poison in the mouth?

entries.farsi: چون همه انوار از شمس بقاست / ‏صبح صادق صبح كاذب از چه خاست
entries.english: Since all lights are from the everlasting Sun, where fore did the true dawn and the false dawn rise?

entries.farsi: چون ز يك سرمه ست ناظر را كحل / ‏از چه آمد راست بينى و حول
entries.english: Since the blackness of every seeing person’s eye is from a single eye salve, why did true, sight and being crossed eyed come?

entries.farsi: چون كه دار الضرب را سلطان خداست / ‏نقد را چون ضرب خوب و نارواست
entries.english: Since God is the Governor of the Mint, how is it that good and spurious coins are struck?

entries.farsi: چون خدا فرمود ره را راه من / ‏اين خفير از چيست و آن يك راه زن
entries.english: Since God has called the Way ‘My Way,’ why is this one a trusty escort and that one a brigand?

entries.farsi: از يك اشكم چون رسد حر و سفيه / ‏چون يقين شد الولد سر ابيه
entries.english: How can the freeman and the fool come from a single womb, since it is certain that the son is his father’s inmost nature?

entries.farsi: وحدتى كه ديد با چندين هزار / صد هزاران جنبش از عين قرار
entries.english: Who saw a Unity with so many thousand, a hundred thousand mo­ tions from the essence of Rest?”

sections.title_fa: جواب گفتن آن قاضى صوفى را
sections.title_en: The Cadi’s reply to the Sufi.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى صوفيا خيره مشو / يك مثالى در بيان اين شنو
entries.english: The Cadi said, “O Sufi’ do not be perplexed: listen to a parable in explanation of this

entries.farsi: او چو كه در ناز ثابت آمده / ‏عاشقان چون برگها لرزان شده
entries.english: Just as the disquiet of lovers is the result of the tranquility of the one who captivates their hearts.

entries.farsi: همچنان كه بى‏قرارى عاشقان / ‏حاصل آمد از قرار دلستان
entries.english: He stands immovable, like a mountain, in his disdain, while his lovers are quivering like leaves.

entries.farsi: خنده‏ى او گريه‏ها انگيخته / ‏آب رويش آبروها ريخته
entries.english: His laughter stirs tears, his glory causes their glories to fade.

entries.farsi: اين همه چون و چگونه چون زبد / بر سر درياى بى‏چون مى‏طپد
entries.english: All this conditionality is tossing like foam on the surface of the unconditioned Sea.

entries.farsi: ضد و ندش نيست در ذات و عمل / ‏ز آن بپوشيدند هستيها حلل
entries.english: In its essence and action there is neither opposite nor like: by it are existences clothed in robes.

entries.farsi: ضد، ضد را بود و هستى كى دهد / بلك ازو بگريزد و بيرون جهد
entries.english: How should an opposite bestow being and existence on its opposite? Nay, it flees and escapes from it.

entries.farsi: ند چه بود مثل مثل نيك و بد / مثل مثل خويشتن را كى كند
entries.english: What is nidd? The like (mithl), the like of good or bad. How should a like make its own like?

entries.farsi: چون كه دو مثل آمدند اى متقى / ‏اين چه اوليتر از آن در خالقى
entries.english: When there are two likes, O God-fearing man, why should this one be fitter than that one for creating?

entries.farsi: بر شمار برگ بستان ضدو ند / چون كفى بر بحر بى‏ند است و ضد
entries.english: Opposites and likes, in number as the leaves of the orchard, are as a flake of foam on the Sea that has no like or opposite.

entries.farsi: بى‏چگونه بين تو برد و مات بحر / چون چگونه گنجد اندر ذات بحر
entries.english: Perceive that the victory and defeat of the Sea are unconditioned: how should there be room for conditionality in the essence of the Sea?

entries.farsi: كمترين لعبت او جان تست / ‏اين چگونه و چون جان كى شد درست
entries.english: Your soul is the least of its playthings; how can the quality and description of the soul be ascertained?

entries.farsi: پس چنان بحرى كه در هر قطر آن / ‏از بدن ناشى تر آمد عقل و جان
entries.english: Such a Sea, then, with every drop whereof the intellect and the spirit are more unfamiliar than the body—

entries.farsi: كى بگنجد در مضيق چند و چون / ‏عقل كل آن جاست از ال يعلمون
entries.english: How should it be contained in the narrow room of quantity and quality? There Universal Reason is one of the ignorant.

entries.farsi: عقل گويد مر جسد را كاى جماد / بوى بردى هيچ از آن بحر معاد
entries.english: Reason says to the body, ‘O lifeless thing, have you ever had a scent of the Sea whither all return?’

entries.farsi: جسم گويد من يقين سايه‏ى توام / ‏يارى از سايه كه جويد جان عم
entries.english: The body replies, ‘assuredly I am your shadow: who would seek help from a shadow, O soul of your uncle?’

entries.farsi: عقل گويد كاين نه آن حيرت سراست / ‏كه سزا گستاخ‏تر از ناسزاست
entries.english: Reason says, ‘this is the house of bewilderment, not a house where the worthy is bolder than the unworthy.’

entries.farsi: اندر اينجا آفتاب انورى / ‏خدمت ذره كند چون چاكرى
entries.english: Here the resplendent sun pays homage to the mote, like a menial.

entries.farsi: شير اين سو پيش آهو سر نهد / باز اينجا نزد تيهو پر نهد
entries.english: In this quarter the lion lays his head before the deer; here the falcon lays his wings before the partridge.

entries.farsi: اين ترا باور نيايد مصطفى / چون ز مسكينان همى‏جويد دعا
entries.english: If you cannot believe this, how is it that Mustafa seeks a blessing from the lowly poor?

entries.farsi: گر بگويى از پى تعليم بود / عين تجهيل از چه رو تفهيم بود
entries.english: If you reply that it was for the purpose of teaching in what respect was his leaving them in absolute ignorance a means of causing them to understand?

entries.farsi: بد گمانى نعل معكوس وى است / گر چه هر جزويش جاسوس وى است
entries.english: Nay, but he knows that the King deposits the royal treasure in ruined places.

entries.farsi: بلكه مى‏داند كه گنج شاهوار / در خرابيها نهد آن شهريار
entries.english: Evil thoughts are his presenting an appearance contrary to the reality, though every part of him is his spy.

entries.farsi: بل حقيقت در حقيقت غرقه شد / زين سبب هفتاد بل صد فرقه شد
entries.english: Nay, the Truth is absorbed in the Truth; hence seventy, nay, a hundred sects hive arisen.

entries.farsi: با تو قلماشيت خواهم گفت هان / ‏صوفيا خوش پهن بگشا گوش جان
entries.english: I will talk to you of matters indifferent. Listen, O Sufi , open your spiritual ear very wide.

entries.farsi: مر ترا هر زخم كايد ز آسمان / ‏منتظر مى‏باش خلعت بعد از آن
entries.english: Whatever blow may come to you from Heaven, always be expecting a gift of honour after it;

entries.farsi: كاو نه آن شاه است كت سيلى زند / پس نبخشد تاج و تخت مستند
entries.english: For He is not the king to slap you and then not give you a crown and a throne on which to recline.

entries.farsi: جمله دنيا را پر پشه بها / سيليى را رشوت بى‏منتها
entries.english: The whole world has the value of a gnat’s wing; for one slap there is an infinite reward.

entries.farsi: گردنت زين طوق زرين جهان / ‏چست در دزد و ز حق سيلى ستان
entries.english: Nimbly slip your neck out of this golden collar, the world, and take the slaps from God.

entries.farsi: آن قفاها كانبيا برداشتند / ز آن بال سرهاى خود افراشتند
entries.english: Since the prophets suffered those blows on the nape, in consequence of that affliction they have lifted their heads.

entries.farsi: ليك حاضر باش در خود اى فتى / تا به خانه او بيابد مر ترا
entries.english: But be present in yourself O youth, in order that He may find you at home.

entries.farsi: ور نه خلعت را برد او باز پس / ‏كه نيابيدم به خانه هيچ كس
entries.english: Else He will take back His gift of honour, saying, ‘I found nobody in the house.”

sections.title_fa: ‏باز سؤال كردن صوفى از آن قاضى
sections.title_en: How the Sufi again questioned the Cadi.

entries.farsi: گفت آن صوفى چه بودى كاين جهان / ‏ابروى رحمت گشادى جاودان
entries.english: The Sufi said, “How would it be if this world were to unknit the eyebrow of mercy for evermore!

entries.farsi: هر دمى شورى نياوردى به پيش / ‏بر نيآوردى ز تلوينهاش نيش
entries.english: If it were not to bring on some trouble at every moment and produce anguish by its changes!

entries.farsi: شب ندزديدى چراغ روز را / دى نبردى باغ عيش آموز را
entries.english: If Night were not to steal the lamp of Day, and if December were not to sweep away the orchard that has learned to delight!

entries.farsi: جام صحت را نبودى سنگ تب / ‏ايمنى را خوف نآوردى كرب
entries.english: If there were no stone of fever to shatter the cup of health, and if fear did not bring anxieties for safety!

entries.farsi: خود چه كم گشتى ز جود و رحمتش / ‏گر نبودى خرخشه در نعمتش
entries.english: How, indeed, would His munificence and mercy be diminished if in His bounty there were no torment?”

sections.title_fa: ‏جواب قاضى سؤال صوفى را و قصه‏ى ترك و درزى را مثل آوردن
sections.title_en: The Cadi’s answer to the questions of the Sufi, and how he adduced the Story of the Turk and the Tailor as a parable.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى بس تهى رو صوفيى / ‏خالى از فطنت چو كاف كوفيى
entries.english: The Cadi said, “You are a very idle vagabond Sufi: you are devoid of intelligence, like the Kufic káf.

entries.farsi: تو بنشنيدى كه آن پر قند لب / ‏غدر خياطان همى‏گفتى به شب
entries.english: Haven’t you heard that a certain sugar-lipped used to tell at nightfall of the perfidy of tailors,

entries.farsi: خلق را در دزدى آن طايفه / ‏مى‏نمود افسانه‏هاى سالفه
entries.english: Setting forth to the people old stories concerning the thievery of that class?

entries.farsi: قصه‏ى پاره ربايى در برين / ‏مى حكايت كرد او با آن و اين
entries.english: To that one and this one he would relate tales of their snatching pieces of cloth while cutting it,

entries.farsi: در سمر مى‏خواند درزى نامه‏اى / ‏گرد او جمع آمده هنگامه‏اى
entries.english: And during the night-talk he would read aloud a book on tailors, when a throng had gathered round him.

entries.farsi: مستمع چون يافت جاذب ز آن وفود / جمله اجزايش حكايت گشته بود
entries.english: Since he found eager listeners among those who came, all parts of him had become the story.

sections.title_fa: ‏قال النَّبىّ عليه السلام ان اللهَّ يلقن الحكمة على لسان الواعظين بقدر همم المستمعين
sections.title_en: The Prophet, on whom be peace, said, ‘Truly God teaches wisdom by the tongues of the preachers
according to the measure of the aspirations of those who hear them.’

entries.farsi: جذب سمع است ار كسى را خوش لبى است / ‏گرمى و جد معلم از صبى است
entries.english: If anyone has suave eloquence, hearing draws it out: the teacher’s enthusiasm and energy are from the boy.

entries.farsi: چنگيى را كاو نوازد بيست و چار / چون نيابد گوش گردد چنگ بار
entries.english: When the harpist who plays the four-and-twenty finds no ear, his harp becomes a burden;

entries.farsi: نه حراره يادش آيد نه غزل / ‏نه ده انگشتش بجنبد در عمل
entries.english: Neither ditty nor ode comes into his memory: his ten fingers will not get to work.

entries.farsi: گر نبودى گوشهاى غيب گير / وحى نآوردى ز گردون يك بشير
entries.english: If there were no ears to receive the Unseen, no announcer would have brought a Revelation from Heaven;

entries.farsi: ور نبودى ديده‏هاى صنع بين / ‏نه فلك گشتى نه خنديدى زمين
entries.english: And if there were no eyes to see the works of God, neither would the sky have revolved nor would the earth have smiled.

entries.farsi: آن دم لوالك اين باشد كه كار / از براى چشم تيز است و نظار
entries.english: The declaration lawláka means this; that the affair is for the sake of the piercing eye and the seer.

entries.farsi: عامه را از عشق هم خوابه و طبق / ‏كى بود پرواى عشق صنع حق
entries.english: How should the vulgar, in their love for bedfellow and dishes, have any care for love of God’s work?

entries.farsi: آب تتماجى نريزى در تغار / تا سگى چندى نباشد طعمه خوار
entries.english: You do not pour tutmáj broth into a trough till there are a number of greedy dogs to drink it.

entries.farsi: رو سگ كهف خداونديش باش / ‏تا رهاند زين تغارت اصطفاش
entries.english: Go, be the Cave-dog of His Lordship in order that His election may deliver you from this trough.

entries.farsi: چون كه دزديهاى بى‏رحمانه گفت / ‏كه كنند آن در زيان اندر نهفت
entries.english: When he related the pitiless thefts which those tailors commit in secret,

entries.farsi: اندر آن هنگامه تركى از خطا / سخت طيره شد ز كشف آن غطا
entries.english: A Turk from Khitá amongst the crowd was exceedingly annoyed by that exposure.

entries.farsi: شب چو روز رستخيز آن رازها / ‏كشف مى‏كرد از پى اهل نهى
entries.english: At night-time he was exposing those secrets for the benefit of the intelligent, as on the Day of Resurrection.

entries.farsi: هر كجا آيى تو در جنگى فراز / بينى آن جا دو عدو در كشف راز
entries.english: Wherever you come to close quarters with a wrangle, you will see there two enemies in exposing secret.

entries.farsi: آن زمان را محشر مذكور دان / ‏و آن گلوى رازگو را صور دان
entries.english: Know that that hour is the Last Judgment mentioned, and know that the throat which tells the secret is the trumpet;

entries.farsi: كه خدا اسباب خشمى ساخته است / ‏و آن فضايح را به كوى انداخته است
entries.english: For God has provided the motives of anger and has caused those shameful things to be divulged.

entries.farsi: بس كه غدر درزيان را ذكر كرد / حيف آمد ترك را و خشم و درد
entries.english: When he had related many instances of the perfidy of tailors, the Turk became annoyed and angry and aggrieved,

entries.farsi: گفت اى قصاص در شهر شما / كيست استاتر در اين مكر و دغا
entries.english: And said, ‘O story-teller, in your city who is the greatest expert in this deceit and fraud?’

sections.title_fa: ‏دعوى‏كردن ترك و گرو بستن او كه درزى از من چيزى نتواند بردن
sections.title_en: How the Turk boasted and wagered that the tailor would not be able to steal anything from him.

entries.farsi: گفت خياطى است نامش پور شش / ‏اندرين چستى و دزدى خلق كش
entries.english: He replied, ‘There is a tailor named Pír-i Shush who beats folk in light-fingeredness and thievery.’

entries.farsi: گفت من ضامن كه با صد اضطراب / ‏او نيارد برد پيشم رشته تاب
entries.english: ‘I warrant,’ said he, ‘that with a hundred efforts he will not be able to take away a coil of thread in my presence.’

entries.farsi: پس بگفتندش كه از تو چيست تر / مات او گشتند در دعوى مپر
entries.english: Then they told him, ‘Cleverer persons than you have been checkmated by him: do not soar in your pretensions.

entries.farsi: رو به عقل خود چنين غره مباش / ‏كه شوى ياوه تو در تزويرهاش
entries.english: Go to, be not so deluded by your intelligence, else you will be lost in his wiles.’

entries.farsi: گرم‏تر شد ترك و بست آن جا گرو / كه نيارد برد نى كهنه نه نو
entries.english: The Turk became hotter and made a wager there that he would not be able to rob either old or new.

entries.farsi: مطمعانش گرم‏تر كردند زود / او گرو بست و رهان را بر گشود
entries.english: Those who flattered his hopes made him hotter: immediately he wagered and declared the stakes,

entries.farsi: كه گرو اين مركب تازى من / ‏بدهم ار دزدد قماشم او به فن
entries.english: Saying, ‘I will pay this Arab horse of mine as a forfeit if he artfully steals my stuff;

entries.farsi: ور نتاند برد اسبى از شما / واستانم بهر رهن مبتدا
entries.english: And if he cannot rob I shall receive a horse from you for the first stake.’

entries.farsi: ترك را آن شب نبرد از غصه خواب / ‏با خيال دزد مى‏كرد او حراب
entries.english: Because of his anxiety sleep did not overcome the Turk that night: he was fighting with the phantom of the thief.

entries.farsi: بامدادان اطلسى زد در بغل / ‏شد به بازار و دكان آن دغل
entries.english: In the morning he put a piece of satin under his arm, went to the bazaar, and the shop of that cunning rogue.

entries.farsi: پس سالمش كرد گرم و اوستاد / جست از جا لب به ترحيبش گشاد
entries.english: Then he saluted him warmly, and the master-tailor sprang up from his seat and opened his lips to bid him welcome.

entries.farsi: گرم پرسيدش ز حد ترك بيش / ‏تا فگند اندر دل او مهر خويش
entries.english: He inquired with a cordiality exceeding that of the Turk, so that he planted in his heart affection for him.

entries.farsi: چون بديد از وى نواى بلبلى / ‏پيشش افكند اطلس استنبلى
entries.english: When he heard from him a song like the nightingales, he threw down before him the piece of Stamboul satin,

entries.farsi: كه ببر اين را قباى روز جنگ / ‏زير نافم واسع و بالاش تنگ
entries.english: Saying, ‘Cut this into a coat for the day of battle: wide below my navel and tight above it—

entries.farsi: تنگ بالا بهر جسم آراى را / زير واسع تا نگيرد پاى را
entries.english: Tight above, to show off my body; wide below, so as not to hamper my legs.’

entries.farsi: پس بپيمود و بديد او روى كار / بعد از آن بگشاد لب را در فشار
entries.english: He replied, ‘O kindly man, I will do a hundred services,’ and in accepting it he laid his hand upon his eye.

entries.farsi: گفت صد خدمت كنم اى ذو وداد / در قبولش دست بر ديده نهاد
entries.english: Then he measured and inspected the working surface and, after that, opened his lips in idle chat.

entries.farsi: از حكايتهاى ميران دگر / و ز كرمها و عطاى آن نفر
entries.english: Of stories about other Amírs and of the bounties and gifts of those persons

entries.farsi: همچو آتش كرد مقراضى برون / ‏مى‏بريد و لب پر افسانه و فسون
entries.english: And about the misers and their economies— he gave a sample for the purpose of laughter.

entries.farsi: و ز بخيالن و ز تحشيراتشان / ‏از براى خنده هم داد او نشان
entries.english: In a flash he whipped out a pair of scissors and went on cutting while his lips were full of tales and beguiling talk.

sections.title_fa: ‏مضاحك گفتن درزى و ترك را از قوت خنده بسته شدن دو چشم تنگ او و فرصت يافتن درزى
sections.title_en: How the tailor told laughable jests, and how the narrow eyes of the Turk were closed by the vio­ lence of his laughter, and how the tailor found an opportunity.

entries.farsi: ترك خنديدن گرفت از داستان / ‏چشم تنگش گشت بسته آن زمان
entries.english: The Turk began to laugh at the stories, and at that moment his narrow eyes closed.

entries.farsi: حق همى‏ديد آن ولى ستار خوست / ‏ليك چون از حد برى غماز اوست
entries.english: He filched a shred and put it under his thigh, hidden from all living beings except God.

entries.farsi: پاره‏اى دزديد و كردش زير ران / ‏از جز حق از همه احيا نهان
entries.english: God saw it, but He is disposed to cover up; yet when you carry beyond bounds He is a tell-tale.

entries.farsi: اطلس چه دعوى چه رهن چى / ‏ترك سر مست است در الغ اچى
entries.english: From his delight in his anecdotes the Turk’s former boast went out of his head.

entries.farsi: ترك را از لذت افسانه‏اش / ‏رفت از دل دعوى پيشانه‏اش
entries.english: What satin? What boast? What wager? The Turk is intoxicated with the jokes of the pasha.

entries.farsi: البه كردش ترك كز بهر خدا / الغ مى‏گو كه مرا شد مغتذا
entries.english: The Turk implored him, crying, ‘For God’s sake go on telling jokes, for they are meat to me.’

entries.farsi: پاره‏اى اطلس سبك بر نيفه زد / ترك غافل خوش مضاحك مى‏مزد
entries.english: The rascal told such a ridiculous story that he fell on his back in an explosion of laughter.

entries.farsi: گفت الغى خندمينى آن دغا / كه فتاد از قهقهه او بر قفا
entries.english: He swiftly clapped a shred of satin to the hem of his under garments, while the Turk was paying no attention and greedily sucking in the jests.

entries.farsi: همچنين بار سوم ترك خطا / گفت الغى گوى از بهر خدا
entries.english: Still, the Turk of Khitá said for the third time, ‘Tell me a joke for God’s sake!’

entries.farsi: گفت الغى خندمين‏تر ز آن دو بار / كرد او اين ترك را كلى شكار
entries.english: He told a story more laughable than on the two previous occasions, and made this Turk entirely his prey.

entries.farsi: پس سوم بار از قبا دزديد شاخ / ‏كه ز خنده‏ش يافت ميدان فراخ
entries.english: His eyes shut, his reason flown, bewildered, the boastful Turk was intoxicated with guffaws.

entries.farsi: چشم بسته عقل جسته مولهه / ‏مست ترك مدعى از قهقهه
entries.english: Then for the third time he filched a strip from the coat, since the Turk’s laughter gave him ample scope.

entries.farsi: چون چهارم بار آن ترك خطا / الغ از آن استا همى‏كرد اقتضا
entries.english: When for the fourth time the Turk of Khitá was demanding a jest from the master-tailor,

entries.farsi: رحم آمد بر وى آن استاد را / كرد در باقى فن و بى‏داد را
entries.english: The master took pity on him and put aside artfulness and injustice.

entries.farsi: گفت مولع گشت اين مفتون در اين / ‏بى‏خبر كاين چه خسار است و غبين
entries.english: He said, ‘This infatuated man has a great desire for these, not knowing what a loss and swindle they are.’

entries.farsi: بوسه افشان كرد بر استاد او / كه به من بهر خدا افسانه گو
entries.english: He showered kisses on the master, crying, ‘For God’s sake tell me a story!’

entries.farsi: اى فسانه گشته و محو از وجود / چند افسانه بخواهى آزمود
entries.english: O you who have become a story and dead to existence, how long will you wish to make trial of stories?

entries.farsi: خندمين‏تر از تو هيچ افسانه نيست / ‏بر لب گور خراب خويش ايست
entries.english: No story is more laughable than you: stand on the edge of your own ruinous grave!

entries.farsi: اى فرو رفته به گور جهل و شك / ‏چند جويى الغ و دستان فلك
entries.english: O you who have gone down into the grave of ignorance and doubt, how long will you seek the jests and tales of Time?

entries.farsi: تا به كى نوشى تو عشوه‏ى اين جهان / ‏كه نه عقلت ماند بر قانون نه جان
entries.english: How long will you listen to the blandishments of this world that leave neither your mind un-deranged nor your spirit?

entries.farsi: الغ اين چرخ نديم كرد و مرد / آب روى صد هزاران چون تو برد
entries.english: The jests of Time, this mean and petty boon-companion, have robbed of honour a hundred thousand like you.

entries.farsi: مى‏درد مى‏دوزد اين درزى عام / ‏جامه‏ى صد سالگان طفل خام
entries.english: This Universal Tailor is ever tearing and stitching the garments of a hundred travellers silly as children.

entries.farsi: الغ او گر باغها را داد داد / چون دى آمد داده را بر باد داد
entries.english: If his jests conferred a gift on the orchards, when December came they gave that gift to the winds.

entries.farsi: پيره طفالن شسته پيشش بهر كد / تا به سعد و نحس او الغى كند
entries.english: The old children sit down beside him to beg that he will jest by fortunes good or bad.

sections.title_fa: گفتن درزى ترك را هى خاموش كه اگر مضاحك دگر گويم قبات تنگ آيد
sections.title_en: How the tailor said to the Turk, “Hey, hold your tongue: if I tell any more funny stories the coat will be tight for you.”

entries.farsi: گفت درزى‏اى طواشى بر گذر / واى بر تو گر كنم الغى دگر
entries.english: The tailor said, ‘Begone, unmanly fellow! Woe to you if I make another jest;

entries.farsi: پس قبايت تنگ آيد باز پس / ‏اين كند با خويشتن خود هيچ كس
entries.english: Then, after that, the coat will be tight for you: does anyone practice this on himself?

entries.farsi: خنده‏ى چه رمزى ار دانستيى / ‏تو بجاى خنده خون بگرستيى
entries.english: What laughter? If you had an inkling, instead of laughing you would weep blood.’

sections.title_en: Explaining that the idle folk who wish stories are like the Turk, and that the deluding and treacherous World is like the tailor, and that lusts and women are this World’s telling laughable jokes, and that Life resembles the piece of satin placed before this Tailor to be made into a coat of eternity and a garment of piety.

entries.farsi: اطلس عمرت به مقراض شهور / برده پاره پاره خياط غرور
entries.english: The Tailor, Worldly Vanity, takes away the satin of your life, bit by bit, with his scissors, the months.

entries.farsi: تو تمنا مى‏برى كاختر مدام / ‏الغ كردى سعد بودى بر دوام
entries.english: You wish that your star might always jest and your happiness continue forever.

entries.farsi: سخت مى‏تولى ز تربيعات او / و ز دالل و كينه و آفات او
entries.english: You are very angry with its quartile aspects and its disdain and enmity and mischief;

entries.farsi: سخت مى‏رنجى ز خاموشى او / و ز نحوس و قبض و كين كوشى او
entries.english: You are very annoyed with its silence and inauspiciousness and severity and its endeavour to show hostility,

entries.farsi: كه چرا زهره‏ى طرب در رقص نيست / ‏بر سعود و رقص سعد او مه ايست
entries.english: Saying, ‘Why doesn’t the merry Venus dance?’ Do not depend on its good luck and auspicious dance.

entries.farsi: اخترت گويد كه گر افزون كنم / ‏الغ را پس كلى‏ات مغبون كنم
entries.english: Your star says, ‘If I jest any more, I shall cause you to be swindled entirely.’

entries.farsi: تو مبين قالبى اين اختران / ‏عشق خود بر قلب زن بين اى مهان
entries.english: Do not regard the counterfeiting of these stars: regard your love for the counterfeiter, O despicable man.

sections.title_en: Parable

entries.farsi: آن يكى مى‏شد به ره سوى دكان / ‏پيش ره را بسته ديد او از زنان
entries.english: A certain man was on the way to his shop he found the road in front of him barred by women.

entries.farsi: پاى او مى‏سوخت از تعجيل و راه / ‏بسته از جوق زنان همچو ماه
entries.english: He was hurrying along hot-foot, and the way was blocked by a crowd of women as the moon.

entries.farsi: رو به يك زن كرد و گفت اى مستهان / ‏هى چه بسياريد اى دختر چگان
entries.english: He turned his face to one woman and said, ‘O vile, how numerous you are, little girls, eh!’

entries.farsi: رو بدو كرد آن زن و گفت اى امين / ‏هيچ بسيارى ما منكر مبين
entries.english: The woman turned towards him and replied, ‘O man of trust, do not think it dreadful that there are so many of us.

entries.farsi: بين كه با بسيارى ما بر بساط / تنگ مى‏آيد شما را انبساط
entries.english: Consider that notwithstanding the multitude of us on the earth you find it insufficient for your enjoyment.

entries.farsi: تو مبين اين واقعات روزگار / كز فلك مى‏گردد اينجا ناگوار
entries.english: Never minding the number of women on the earth, men find them insufficient and turn to other men for enjoyment.’

entries.farsi: تو مبين تحشير روزى و معاش / ‏تو مبين اين قحط و خوف و ارتعاش
entries.english: Do not regard these happenings of Time which from heaven come to pass intolerably here.

entries.farsi: در لواطه مى‏فتيد از قحط زن / ‏فاعل و مفعول رسواى زمن
entries.english: Do not regard the husbanding of daily bread and livelihood and this dearth and fear and trembling,

entries.farsi: بين كه با اين جمله تلخيهاى او / مرده‏ى اوييد و ناپرواى او
entries.english: Consider that in spite of all its bitterness you are mortally enamoured of it and recklessly devoted to it.

entries.farsi: آن براهيم از تلف نگريخت و ماند / اين براهيم از شرف بگريخت و راند
entries.english: Deem bitter tribulation to be a mercy, deem the kingdom of Merv and Balkh to be a vengeance.

entries.farsi: رحمتى دان امتحان تلخ را / نقمتى دان ملك مرو و بلخ را
entries.english: That Ibrahim fled not from destruction and remained, while this Ibrahim fled from honour and rode away.

entries.farsi: آن نسوزد وين بسوزد اى عجب / ‏نعل معكوس است در راه طلب
entries.english: That one is not burnt, and this one is burnt. Oh, wonderful! In the Way of search everything is upside down.”

sections.title_fa: باز مكرر كردن صوفى سؤال را
sections.title_en: How the Sufi repeated his questions.

entries.farsi: گفت صوفى قادر است آن مستعان / ‏كه كند سوداى ما را بى‏زيان
entries.english: The Sufi said, “He whose help is invoked has the power to make our trading free from loss.

entries.farsi: آن كه آتش را كند ورد و شجر / هم تواند كرد اين را بى‏ضرر
entries.english: He who turns the fire into roses and trees is also able to make this harmless.

entries.farsi: آن كه گل آرد برون از عين خار / هم تواند كرد اين دى را بهار
entries.english: He who brings forth roses from the very midst of thorns is also able to turn this winter into spring.

entries.farsi: آن كه زو هر سرو آزادى كند / قادر است ار غصه را شادى كند
entries.english: He by whom every cypress is made ‘free’ has the power if He would turn sorrow into joy.

entries.farsi: آن كه شد موجود از وى هر عدم / ‏گر بدارد باقى‏اش او را چه كم
entries.english: He by whom every non-existence is made existent— what damage would He suffer if He were to preserve it forever?

entries.farsi: آن كه تن را جان دهد تا حى شود / گر نميراند زيانش كى شود
entries.english: He who gives the body a soul that it may live— how would He be a loser if He did not cause it to die?

entries.farsi: خود چه باشد گر ببخشد آن جواد / بنده را مقصود جان بى‏اجتهاد
entries.english: What, indeed, would it matter if that Bounteous One should bestow on His servant the desire of his soul without toil,

entries.farsi: دور دارد از ضعيفان در كمين / ‏مكر نفس و فتنه‏ى ديو لعين
entries.english: And keep far off from poor the cunning of the flesh and the temptation of the Devil in ambush?”

sections.title_fa: جواب دادن قاضى صوفى را
sections.title_en: The Cadi’s reply to the Sufi.

entries.farsi: گفت قاضى گر نبودى امر مر / ور نبودى خوب و زشت و سنگ و در
entries.english: The Cadi said, “Were there no bitter Commandment and were there no good and evil and no pebbles and pearls,

entries.farsi: ور نبودى نفس و شيطان و هوا / ور نبودى زخم و چاليش و وغا
entries.english: And were there no flesh and Devil and passions, and were there no blows and battle and war,

entries.farsi: پس به چه نام و لقب خواندى ملك / ‏بندگان خويش را اى منتهك
entries.english: Then by what name and title would the King call His servants, O abandoned man?

entries.farsi: چون بگفتى اى صبور و اى حليم / ‏چون بگفتى اى شجاع و اى حكيم
entries.english: How could He say, ‘O steadfast one’ and ‘O forbearing one’? How could He say, ‘O brave one’ and ‘O wise one’?

entries.farsi: رستم و حمزه و مخنث يك بدى / ‏علم و حكمت باطل و مندك بدى
entries.english: How could there be steadfast and sincere and spending men without a brigand and accursed Devil?

entries.farsi: صابرين و صادقين و منفقين / ‏چون بدى بى‏رهزن و ديو لعين
entries.english: Rustam and Hamza and a catamite would be one; knowledge and wisdom would be annulled and utterly demolished.

entries.farsi: علم و حكمت بهر راه و بى‏رهى است / ‏چون همه ره باشد آن حكمت تهى است
entries.english: Knowledge and wisdom exist for the purpose of the right path and the wrong paths: when all are the right path, knowledge and wisdom are void.

entries.farsi: بهر اين دكان طبع شوره آب / ‏هر دو عالم را روا دارى خراب
entries.english: Do you think it allowable that both the worlds should be ruined for the sake of this briny shop of the nature?

entries.farsi: من همى‏دانم كه تو پاكى نه خام / ‏وين سؤالت هست از بهر عوام
entries.english: I know that you are pure, not raw, and that these questions of yours are for the sake of the vulgar.

entries.farsi: جور دوران و هر آن رنجى كه هست / ‏سهلتر از بعد حق و غفلت است
entries.english: The cruelty of Time and every affliction that exists are lighter than farness from God and forgetfulness,

entries.farsi: ز انكه اينها بگذرند آن نگذرد / دولت آن دارد كه جان آگه برد
entries.english: Because these will pass, that will not. He that brings his spirit awake is possessed of felicity.”

sections.title_fa: حكايت در تقرير آن كه صبر در رنج كار سهلتر از صبر در فراق يار بود
sections.title_en: A Story setting forth that patience in bearing worldly affliction is easier than patience in bearing separation from the Beloved.

entries.farsi: آن يكى زن شوى خود را گفت هى / ‏اى مروت را به يكره كرده طى
entries.english: A certain woman said to her husband, “Hey, O you who have finished with generosity once and for all,

entries.farsi: هيچ تيمارم نمى‏دارى چرا / تا به كى باشم در اين خوارى چرا
entries.english: Why have you no care for me? How long shall I dwell in this abode of misery?”

entries.farsi: گفت شو من نفقه چاره مى‏كنم / ‏گر چه عورم دست و پايى مى‏زنم
entries.english: The husband replied, “I am doing my best to earn money; though I am destitute, I am moving hand and foot.

entries.farsi: نفقه و كسوه است واجب اى صنم / ‏از منت اين هر دو هست و نيست گم
entries.english: O beloved, it is my duty money and clothes: you get both these from me and they are not insufficient.”

entries.farsi: آستين پيرهن بنمود زن / ‏بس درشت و پر وسخ بد پيرهن
entries.english: The wife showed the sleeve of her chemise: the chemise was very coarse and dirty.

entries.farsi: گفت از سختى تنم را مى‏خورد / كس كسى را كسوه زين سان آورد
entries.english: “It is so rough,” said she, “it eats my body: does anyone get a garment of this kind for any one?”

entries.farsi: گفت اى زن يك سؤالت مى‏كنم / ‏مرد درويشم همين آمد فنم
entries.english: He said, “O wife, I will ask you one question. I am a poor man: this is all I know.

entries.farsi: اين درشت است و غليظ و ناپسند / ليك بنديش اى زن انديشه‏مند
entries.english: This is rough and coarse and disagreeable, but think, O thoughtful wife!

entries.farsi: اين درشت و زشت‏تر يا خود طالق / ‏اين ترا مكروه‏تر يا خود فراق
entries.english: Is this rougher and nastier, or divorce? Is this more odious to you, or separation?”

entries.farsi: همچنان اى خواجه‏ى تشنيع زن / ‏از بال و فقر و از رنج و محن
entries.english: Even so, O Khwaja who are reviling on account of affliction and poverty and distress and tribulations,

entries.farsi: ال شك اين ترك هوا تلخى ده است / ‏ليك از تلخى بعد حق به است
entries.english: No doubt this renunciation of sensuality gives bitter pain, but it is better than the bitterness of being far from God.

entries.farsi: رنج كى ماند دمى كه ذو المنن / ‏گويدت چونى تو اى رنجور من
entries.english: If fighting and fasting are hard and rough, yet these are better than being far from Him who inflicts tribulation.

entries.farsi: گر جهاد و صوم سخت است و خشن / ‏ليك اين بهتر ز بعد ممتحن
entries.english: How should pain endure for a single moment when the Giver of favours says to you, “How are you, O My sick one?”

entries.farsi: ور نگويد كت نه آن فهم و فن است / ‏ليك آن ذوق تو پرسش كردن است
entries.english: And if He say not, because you have not the understanding and knowledge for it, yet your inward feeling is inquiring.

entries.farsi: آن مليحان كه طبيبان دلند / سوى رنجوران به پرسش مايل‏اند
entries.english: Those beauteous ones who are spiritual physicians turn towards the sick to inquire;

entries.farsi: ور حذر از ننگ و از نامى كنند / چاره‏اى سازند و پيغامى كنند
entries.english: And if they be afraid of disgrace and reputation, they devise some means and send a message;

entries.farsi: ور نه در دلشان بود آن مفتكر / نيست معشوقى ز عاشق بى‏خبر
entries.english: Or if not, that is pondered in their hearts: no beloved is unaware of his lover.

entries.farsi: اى تو جوياى نوادر داستان / ‏هم فسانه‏ى عشق بازان را بخوان
entries.english: O you, who desire a wondrous tale, read the story of them that play the game of love.

entries.farsi: بس بجوشيدى در اين عهد مديد / ترك جوشى هم نگشتى اى قديد
entries.english: You have been boiling mightily during this long time, O dried meat, you have not even become half-cooked.

entries.farsi: ديده‏اى عمرى تو داد و داورى / ‏وانگه ار ناديدگان ناشى‏ترى
entries.english: During a life-time you have seen the justice and jurisdiction, and then you are more ignorant than the blind.

entries.farsi: هر كه شاگرديش كرد استاد شد / تو سپستر رفته‏اى اى كور لد
entries.english: Whoever serves Him as a pupil becomes a master; you have gone backwards, O blind fool!

entries.farsi: خود نبود از والدينت اختيار / هم نبودت عبرت از ليل و نهار
entries.english: Truly you have learned nothing from your parents, nor have you taken a lesson from night and day.

sections.title_en: Parable.

entries.farsi: عارفى پرسيد از آن پير كشيش / ‏كه تويى خواجه مسن‏تر يا كه ريش
entries.english: A gnostic asked an old Christian priest, “Sire, are you the more advanced in age, or your beard?”

entries.farsi: گفت نه من پيش از او زاييده‏ام / ‏بى‏ز ريشى بى‏جهان را ديده‏ام
entries.english: He replied, “Nay; I was born before it: I have seen much of the world without a beard.”

entries.farsi: گفت ريشت شد سپيد از حال گشت / ‏خوى زشت تو نگرديده‏ست وشت
entries.english: He said, “Your beard has turned white, it has changed, your evil disposition has not become good.”

entries.farsi: او پس از تو زاد و از تو بگذريد / تو چنين خشكى ز سوداى ثريد
entries.english: It was born after you and it has surpassed you: you are so dry because of your passion for tharíd.

entries.farsi: تو بر آن رنگى كه اول زاده‏اى / ‏يك قدم ز آن پيشتر ننهاده‏اى
entries.english: You are of the same complexion with which you were born: you have not taken one step forward.

entries.farsi: همچنان دوغى ترش در معدنى / ‏خود نكردى زو مخلص روغنى
entries.english: Still you are sour buttermilk in the churn: in truth you have not extracted any oil from it.

entries.farsi: هم خميرى خمره‏ى طينه درى / ‏گر چه عمرى در تنور آذرى
entries.english: Still you are dough in the jar of clay, though you have been a lifetime in the fiery oven.

entries.farsi: چون حشيشى پا به گل بر پشته‏اى / ‏گر چه از باد هوس سر گشته‏اى
entries.english: You are like a herb on a hillock: foot in the earth, though your head is tossed by the wind of passion.

entries.farsi: همچو قوم موسى اندر حر تيه / ‏مانده‏اى بر جاى چل سال اى سفيه
entries.english: Like the people of Moses in the heat of the Desert, you have remained forty years in place, O foolish man.

entries.farsi: مى‏روى هر روز تا شب هروله / ‏خويش مى‏بينى در اول مرحله
entries.english: Daily you march rapidly till nightfall and find yourself in the first stage of your journey.

entries.farsi: نگذرى زين بعد سيصد ساله تو / تا كه دارى عشق آن گوساله تو
entries.english: You will never traverse this three hundred years’ distance so long as you have love for the calf.

entries.farsi: تا خيال عجل از جانشان نرفت / ‏بد بر ايشان تيه چون گرداب تفت
entries.english: Until the fancy of the calf went out of their hearts, the Desert was to them like a blazing whirlpool.

entries.farsi: غير اين عجلى كز او يابيده‏اى / ‏بى‏نهايت لطف و نعمت ديده‏اى
entries.english: Besides this calf which you have obtained from Him, you have experienced infinite graces and bounties.

entries.farsi: گاو طبعى ز آن نكوييهاى زفت / ‏از دلت در عشق اين گوساله رفت
entries.english: You have the nature of a cow; hence in your love for this calf mighty benefits have vanished from your heart.

entries.farsi: بارى اكنون تو ز هر جزوت بپرس / ‏صد زبان دارند اين اجزاى خرس
entries.english: Please now, ask each part of you: these dumb parts have a hundred tongues

entries.farsi: ذكر نعمتهاى رزاق جهان / ‏كه نهان شد آن در اوراق زمان
entries.english: To recall the bounties of the World-Provider which are hidden in the pages of Time.

entries.farsi: روز و شب افسانه جويانى تو چست / ‏جزو جزو تو فسانه گوى تست
entries.english: By day and night you are eagerly seeking stories, while each several part of you is telling you the story.

entries.farsi: جزو جزوت تا برسته‏ست از عدم / ‏چند شادى ديده‏اند و چند غم
entries.english: Since each several part of you grew up from non-existence, how much joy have they experienced and how much pain!

entries.farsi: جزو ماند و آن خوشى از ياد رفت / ‏بل نرفت آن خفيه شد از پنج و هفت
entries.english: For without pleasure no part will grow; on the contrary, at every spasm the part becomes thin.

entries.farsi: ز انكه بى‏لذت نرويد هيچ جزو / بلكه الغر گردد از هر پيچ جزو
entries.english: The part remained, but the pleasure vanished from memory; nay, it did not vanish, it became concealed from the five and the seven.

entries.farsi: همچو تابستان كه از وى پنبه زاد / ماند پنبه رفت تابستان ز ياد
entries.english: Like summer, from which cotton is born: the cotton remains, the summer is no more remembered;

entries.farsi: يا مثال يخ كه زايد از شتا / شد شتا پنهان و آن يخ پيش ما
entries.english: Or like the ice which is born of winter: winter disappears, but the ice is with us.

entries.farsi: هست آن يخ ز آن صعوبت يادگار / يادگار صيف در دى اين ثمار
entries.english: The ice is a souvenir of the hardships, and in December these fruits are a souvenir of summer.

entries.farsi: همچنان هر جزو جزوت اى فتى / ‏در تنت افسانه گوى نعمتى
entries.english: Similarly, O youth, every single part in your body is telling the story of a bounty,

entries.farsi: چون زنى كه بيست فرزندش بود / هر يكى حاكى حال خوش بود
entries.english: As, a woman who has twenty children; each tells of a delight.

entries.farsi: حمل نبود بى‏ز مستى و ز الغ / ‏بى‏بهارى كى شود زاينده باغ
entries.english: There is no pregnancy without rapture and amorous sport: how should the orchard produce without a Spring?

entries.farsi: حامالن و بچگانشان بر كنار / شد دليل عشق بازى با بهار
entries.english: The pregnant and the children on their laps are evidence of dalliance with the Spring.

entries.farsi: هر درختى در رضاع كودكان / ‏همچو مريم حامل از شاهى نهان
entries.english: Every tree in suckling its children is impregnated, like Mary, by a King unseen.

entries.farsi: گر چه در آب آتشى پوشيده شد / صد هزاران كف بر او جوشيده شد
entries.english: Although in water the heat of fire is concealed, a hundred thousand bubbles froth upon it,

entries.farsi: گر چه آتش سخت پنهان مى‏تند / كف به ده انگشت اشارت مى‏كند
entries.english: And though fire works very secretly, the froth indicates with ten fingers.

entries.farsi: همچنين اجزاى مستان وصال / ‏حامل از تمثالهاى حال و قال
entries.english: In like manner the parts of those intoxicated with union are pregnant with the forms of feelings and words.

entries.farsi: در جمال حال وامانده دهان / ‏چشم غايب گشته از نقش جهان
entries.english: Their mouths remain gaping at the beauty of ecstasy, their eyes are absent from the forms of this world.

entries.farsi: آن مواليد از ره اين چار نيست / ‏الجرم منظور اين ابصار نيست
entries.english: Those progenies are not by means of these four; consequently they are not seen by these eyes.

entries.farsi: آن مواليد از تجلى زاده‏اند / الجرم مستور پرده‏ى ساده‏اند
entries.english: Those progenies are born of illumination; consequently they are covered by a pure veil.

entries.farsi: زاده گفتيم و حقيقت زاد نيست / ‏وين عبارت جز پى ارشاد نيست
entries.english: We said “born,” but in reality they are not born, and this expression is only in order to guide.

entries.farsi: هين خمش كن تا بگويد شاه قل / ‏بلبلى مفروش با اين جنس گل
entries.english: Listen; be silent that the King of Say may speak: do not play the nightingale with a Rose of this kind.

entries.farsi: اين گل گوياست پر جوش و خروش / ‏بلبال ترك زبان كن باش گوش
entries.english: This eloquent Rose is full of song and cry: O nightingale let your tongue cease, be ear!

entries.farsi: هر دو گون تمثال پاكيزه مثال / ‏شاهد عدلند بر سر وصال
entries.english: Both kinds of pure ideal forms are valid witnesses to the mystery of union.

entries.farsi: هر دو گون حسن لطيف مرتضى / ‏شاهد احبال و حشر ما مضى
entries.english: Both kinds of subtle and delectable beauty are witnesses to pregnancies and growing big in the past,

entries.farsi: همچو يخ كاندر تموز مستجد / هر دم افسانه‏ى زمستان مى‏كند
entries.english: Like ice that in the brilliant Tamúz is ever telling the story of winter

entries.farsi: ذكر آن ارياح سرد و زمهرير / اندر آن ايام و ازمان عسير
entries.english: And recalling the cold winds and intense frost in those hard days and times;

entries.farsi: قصه‏ى دور تبسمهاى شمس / ‏و آن عروسان چمن را لمس و طمس
entries.english: Like fruit that in winter-time tells the story of God’s lovingkindness

entries.farsi: همچو آن ميوه كه در وقت شتا / مى‏كند افسانه‏ى لطف خدا
entries.english: And the tale of the season when the sun was smiling and embracing the brides of the orchard.

entries.farsi: حال رفت و ماند جزوت يادگار / يا از او واپرس يا خود ياد آر
entries.english: The ecstasy is gone but your part remains as a souvenir: either inquire of it, or yourself recall to mind.

entries.farsi: چون فرو گيرد غمت گر چستيى / ز آن دم نوميد كن واجستيى
entries.english: When grief takes possession of you, if you are a fit person you would question that moment of despair

entries.farsi: گفتييش اى غصه‏ى منكر به حال / ‏راتبه‏ى انعامها را ز آن كمال
entries.english: And would say to it, “O Sorrow that denies implicitly the allowance of favours by that Perfection,

entries.farsi: گر به هر دم نه‏ات بهار و خرمى است / ‏همچو چاش گل تنت انبار چيست
entries.english: If spring and fresh gladness are not always your, of what is your body, like a heap of roses, the storehouse?

entries.farsi: چاش گل تن فكر تو همچون گالب / ‏منكر گل شد گالب اينت عجاب
entries.english: Your body is a heap of roses, your thought is like rose-water; the rose-water denies the rose: lo, here is a marvel!”

entries.farsi: آن لجاج كفر، قانون كپى است / ‏و آن سپاس و شكر منهاج نبى است
entries.english: Straw is refused to those who apishly show ingratitude, sun and cloud are lavished on those who resemble the prophets in disposition.

entries.farsi: از كپى خويان كفران كه دريغ / ‏بر نبى خويان نثار مهر و ميغ
entries.english: That obstinacy in ingratitude is the rule followed by the ape, while that thankfulness and gratitude is the way of the prophet.

entries.farsi: با كپى خويان تهتكها چه كرد / با نبى رويان تنسكها چه كرد
entries.english: What was done to the apish by their deeds of shame; what was done to those of prophetic complexion by their acts of piety!

entries.farsi: در عمارتها سگانند و عقور / در خرابيهاست گنج عز و نور
entries.english: In well-cultivated places there are curs and biting; in ruined places there is the treasure of glory and light.

entries.farsi: گر نبودى اين بزوغ اندر خسوف / ‏گم نكردى راه چندين فيلسوف
entries.english: If this moonlight had not been in eclipse, so many philosophers would not have lost the way.

entries.farsi: زيركان و عاقالن از گمرهى / ‏ديده بر خرطوم داغ ابلهى
entries.english: Through losing their way the acute and intelligent saw the brand of foolishness on their noses.

sections.title_fa: ‏باقى قصه‏ى فقير روزى طلب بى‏واسطه‏ى كسب
sections.title_en: The remainder of the Story of the fakir who desired his daily bread without work as a means.

entries.farsi: آن يكى بى‏چاره‏ى مفلس ز درد / كه ز بى‏چيزى هزاران زهر خورد
entries.english: In his grief that wretched pauper, who suffered a thousand agonies on account of indigence,

entries.farsi: البه كردى در نماز و در دعا / كاى خداوند و نگهبان رعا
entries.english: Used to beseech in prayer and invocation, crying, “O Lord and Guardian of the shepherds,

entries.farsi: بى‏ز جهدى آفريدى مر مرا / بى‏فن من روزى‏ام ده زين سرا
entries.english: You created me without any exertion: give me daily bread from this mansion without contrivance.

entries.farsi: پنج گوهر دادى‏ام در درج سر / پنج حس ديگرى هم مستتر
entries.english: You gave me the five jewels in the casket of my head, and also five other occult senses.

entries.farsi: ال يعد اين داد و ال يحصى ز تو / من كليلم از بيانش شرم رو
entries.english: These gifts of Yours are not to be numbered or computed; in setting them forth I am tongue-tied and shamefaced.

entries.farsi: سالها زو اين دعا بسيار شد / عاقبت زارى او بر كار شد
entries.english: Since You are alone in my creation, do You adjust the matter of providing me with daily bread.”

entries.farsi: چون كه در خالقى‏ام تنها توى / ‏كار رزاقيم تو كن مستوى
entries.english: For years this prayer was frequently uttered by him, and at last his supplication took effect,

entries.farsi: همچو آن شخصى كه روزى حالل / ‏از خدا مى‏خواست بى‏كسب و كالل
entries.english: As the person who used to beg God to grant him a lawful livelihood without labour and fatigue,

entries.farsi: گاو آوردش سعادت عاقبت / ‏عهد داود لدنى معدلت
entries.english: At length the cow brought him happiness: the epoch of David whose justice was divinely inspired.

entries.farsi: اين متيم نيز زاريها نمود / هم ز ميدان اجابت گو ربود
entries.english: This thrall of love, too, made piteous entreaties, and he likewise carried off the ball from the field of response.

entries.farsi: گاه بد ظن مى‏شدى اندر دعا / از پى تاخير پاداش و جزا
entries.english: While praying he would at times become distrustful on account of the postponement of the recompense and reward;

entries.farsi: باز ار جاى خداوند كريم / ‏در دلش بشار گشتى و زعيم
entries.english: Again the gracious Lord’s deferment would bring a message of joy to his heart and become a surety.

entries.farsi: چون شدى نوميد در جهد از كالل / ‏از جناب حق شنيدى كه تعال
entries.english: Whenever in earnest supplication weariness caused him to despair, he would hear from the Presence of God “Come!”

entries.farsi: خافض است و رافع است اين كردگار / بى‏از اين دو بر نيايد هيچ كار
entries.english: This Maker is He who abases and exalts: without these two no work is accomplished.

entries.farsi: خفض ارضى بين و رفع آسمان / ‏بى‏از اين دو نيست دورانش اى فالن
entries.english: Consider the lowness of the earth and the loftiness of the sky: without these two its revolution is not, O such-and-such.

entries.farsi: خفض و رفع اين زمين نوعى دگر / نيم سالى شوره نيمى سبز و تر
entries.english: The lowness and loftiness of this earth are of another sort: for one half of the year it is barren and for half green and fresh.

entries.farsi: خفض و رفع اين مزاج ممتزج / ‏گاه صحت گاه رنجورى مضج
entries.english: The lowness and loftiness of distressful Time are of another sort: one half day and half night.

entries.farsi: خفض و رفع روزگار با كرب / ‏نوع ديگر نيم روز و نيم شب
entries.english: The lowness and loftiness of this blended temperament are now health and now sickness that causes to cry out.

entries.farsi: همچنين دان جمله احوال جهان / ‏قحط و جذب و صلح و جنگ از افتتان
entries.english: Know that even so are all the changing conditions of the world— famine and drought and peace and war—from probation.

entries.farsi: اين جهان با اين دو پر اندر هواست / ‏زين دو جانها موطن خوف و رجاست
entries.english: By means of these two wings this world is in the air; by means of these twain souls are habitations of fear and hope,

entries.farsi: تا جهان لرزان بود مانند برگ / ‏در شمال و در سموم بعث و مرگ
entries.english: To the end that the world may be trembling like a leaf in the north wind and simoom of resurrection and death,

entries.farsi: تا خم يك رنگى عيساى ما / بشكند نرخ خم صد رنگ را
entries.english: That the vat of the unicolority of our Jesus may destroy the value of the vat containing a hundred dyes;

entries.farsi: كآن جهان همچون نمكسار آمده است / ‏هر چه آن جا رفت بى‏تلوين شده ست
entries.english: For that world is like a salt-mine: whatever has gone thither has become exempt from coloration.

entries.farsi: اين نمكسار جسوم ظاهر است / ‏خود نمكسار معانى ديگر است
entries.english: Look at earth: it makes many-coloured humankind to be of one colour in their graves.

entries.farsi: خاك را بين خلق رنگارنگ را / مى‏كند يك رنگ اندر گورها
entries.english: This is the salt-mine for visible bodies, in truth the salt-mine for ideal things is different.

entries.farsi: آن نمكسار معانى معنوى است / ‏از ازل آن تا ابد اندر نوى است
entries.english: The salt-mine for ideal things is ideal: it remains new from eternity unto everlasting.

entries.farsi: اين نوى را كهنگى ضدش بود / آن نوى بى‏ضد و بى‏ند و عدد
entries.english: This newness has oldness as its opposite, but that newness is without opposite or like or number.

entries.farsi: آن چنانك از صقل نور مصطفى / صد هزاران نوع ظلمت شد ضيا
entries.english: It is as by the polishing action of the Light of Mustafá a hundred thousand sorts of darkness became radiant.

entries.farsi: از جهود و مشرك و ترسا و مغ / ‏جملگى يك رنگ شد ز آن الپ الغ
entries.english: Jew and polytheist and Christian and Magi— all were made of one colour by that Alp Ulugh (great hero).

entries.farsi: صد هزاران سايه كوتاه و دراز / شد يكى در نور آن خورشيد راز
entries.english: A hundred thousand shadows short and long became one in the light of that Sun of mystery.

entries.farsi: ليك يك رنگى كه اندر محشر است / ‏بر بد و بر نيك كشف و ظاهر است
entries.english: Neither a long remained nor a short nor a wide: shadows of every kind were given in pawn to the Sun.

entries.farsi: نه درازى ماند نه كوته نه پهن / ‏گونه گونه سايه در خورشيد رهن
entries.english: But the unicolority that is at the Resurrection is revealed and manifest to the evil and the good;

entries.farsi: كه معانى آن جهان صورت شود / نقشهامان در خور خصلت شود
entries.english: For in that world ideas are endued with form, and our shapes become congruous with our qualities.

entries.farsi: اين زمان سرها مثال گاو پيس / ‏دوك نطق اندر ملل صد رنگ ريس
entries.english: The thoughts will then become the form of the books: this lining will become the working surface of the garments.

entries.farsi: گردد آن گه فكر، نقش نامه‏ها / اين بطانه روى كار جامه‏ها
entries.english: During this time inward beliefs are as a piebald cow, and in the religious sects the spindle of speech is spinning a hundred colours.

entries.farsi: نوبت صد رنگى است و صد دلى / ‏عالم يك رنگ كى گردد جلى
entries.english: It is the turn of many-colouredness and many-mindedness: how should the one-coloured world be unveiled?

entries.farsi: نوبت زنگى است رومى شد نهان / ‏اين شب است و آفتاب اندر رهان
entries.english: It is the turn of the Ethiopian; the Greek is hidden: this is night, and the sun is in pawn.

entries.farsi: نوبت گرگ است و يوسف زير چاه / ‏نوبت قبط است و فرعون است شاه
entries.english: It is the turn of the wolf, and Joseph is at the bottom of the well; it is the turn of the Egyptians, and Pharaoh is king.

entries.farsi: تا ز رزق بى‏دريغ خيره خند / اين سگان را حصه باشد روز چند
entries.english: In order that for a few days these curs may have their allotted portion of the unstinted and deluding provision.

entries.farsi: در درون بيشه شيران منتظر / تا شود امر تَعالَوْا منتشر
entries.english: Within the jungle are lions, waiting for the command “Come!” to be spread abroad.

entries.farsi: پس برون آيند آن شيران ز مرج / ‏بى‏حجابى حق نمايد دخل و خرج
entries.english: Then those lions will come forth from the pasture, and God will show their income and expenditure without any veil.

entries.farsi: جوهر انسان بگيرد بر و بحر / پيسه گاوان بسمالن روز نحر
entries.english: The essence of Man will encompass land and sea, the piebald cattle will be killed as victims on the Day of Slaughter.

entries.farsi: روز نحر رستخيز سهمناك / مومنان را عيد و گاوان را هالك
entries.english: The terrible Day of Slaughter at the Resurrection is a festival for the true believers and destruction for the cattle.

entries.farsi: جمله‏ى مرغان آب آن روز نحر / همچو كشتيها روان بر روى بحر
entries.english: On that Day of Slaughter all the water-birds sailing along like ships on the surface of the Sea.

entries.farsi: تا كه يهلك من هلك عن بينة / ‏تا كه ينجو من نجا و استيقنه
entries.english: To the end that they who perish may perish by a clear proof, and that they who are saved and have sure knowledge thereof may be saved,

entries.farsi: تا كه بازان جانب سلطان روند / تا كه زاغان سوى گورستان روند
entries.english: And that the falcons may go to the Sultan and that the crows may go to the graveyard;

entries.farsi: كاستخوان و اجزاء سرگين همچو نان / ‏نقل زاغان آمده‏ست اندر جهان
entries.english: For in this world the dessert of the crows was bones and pieces of dung like bread.

entries.farsi: قند حكمت از كجا زاغ از كجا / كرم سرگين از كجا باغ از كجا
entries.english: How remote is the sugar of wisdom from the crow! How remote is the dung beetle from the orchard!

entries.farsi: نيست اليق غزو نفس و مرد غر / نيست اليق عود و مشك و كون خر
entries.english: It is not suitable for an effeminate man to go to fight against the carnal soul: aloes-wood and musk are not suitable for the arse of an ass.

entries.farsi: چون غزا ندهد زنان را هيچ دست / ‏كى دهد آن كه جهاد اكبر است
entries.english: Since women are not at all adapted for fighting, how should they be adapted for that which is the greater holy war?

entries.farsi: جز به نادر در تن زن رستمى / ‏گشته باشد خفيه همچون مريمى
entries.english: A Rustam may have been concealed in a woman’s body, as a Mary; only seldom.

entries.farsi: آن چنان كه در تن مردان زنان / ‏خفيه‏اند و ماده از ضعف جنان
entries.english: Similarly, women are concealed in men’s bodies, and they are female because of faintness of heart.

entries.farsi: آن جهان صورت شود آن مادگى / ‏هر كه در مردى نديد آمادگى
entries.english: In that world, if anyone has not found in his manhood the capacity, his femininity takes shape.

entries.farsi: روز عدل و عدل داد در خور است / ‏كفش آن پا كاله آن سر است
entries.english: The Day is justice, and justice consists in giving what is proper: the shoe belongs to the foot, and the cap belongs to the head.

entries.farsi: تا به مطلب در رسد هر طالبى / ‏تا به غرب خود رود هر غاربى
entries.english: In order that every seeker may attain to the object of his search, and that everything destined to set may go to its point of setting.

entries.farsi: نيست هر مطلوب از طالب دريغ / ‏جفت تابش شمس و جفت آب ميغ
entries.english: No object of search is withheld from the seeker: the sun is paired with heat and the cloud with water.

entries.farsi: هست دنيا قهر خانه‏ى كردگار / قهر بين چون قهر كردى اختيار
entries.english: The present world is the Creator’s penitentiary: since you have chosen punishment, suffer punishment!

entries.farsi: استخوان و موى مقهوران نگر / تيغ قهر افكنده اندر بحر و بر
entries.english: Contemplate the bones and hair of the punished ones the sword of punishment overthrew on sea and land.

entries.farsi: پر و پاى مرغ بين بر گرد دام / ‏شرح قهر حق كننده بى‏كالم
entries.english: Consider the bird’s feathers and feet around the trap and silently expounding God’s punishment.

entries.farsi: مرد او بر جاى خر پشته نشاند / وان كه كهنه گشت هم پشته نماند
entries.english: He dies and leaves a vault to occupy his place; and one who has lain for ages, even the vault has disappeared.

entries.farsi: هر كسى را جفت كرده عدل حق / ‏پيل را با پيل و بق را جنس بق
entries.english: The justice of God has mated every one— elephant with elephant and gnat with gnat.

entries.farsi: مونس احمد به مجلس چار يار / مونس بو جهل عتبه و ذو الخمار
entries.english: The familiar associates of Ahmad were the Four Friends, the familiars of Bu Jahl were Utba and Dhu ’l-Khimár.

entries.farsi: كعبه‏ى جبريل و جانها سدره‏اى / ‏قبله‏ى عبد البطون شد سفره‏اى
entries.english: The Ka‘ba of Gabriel and the spirits is a Lotus-tree; the qibla of the belly-slave is a table-cloth.

entries.farsi: قبله‏ى عارف بود نور وصال / ‏قبله‏ى عقل مفلسف شد خيال
entries.english: The qibla of the gnostic is the light of union; the qibla of the philosopher’s intellect is phantasy.

entries.farsi: قبله‏ى زاهد بود يزدان بر / قبله‏ى مطمع بود هميان زر
entries.english: The qibla of the ascetic is the Gracious God; the qibla of the flatterer is a purse of gold.

entries.farsi: قبله‏ى معنى‏وران صبر و درنگ / ‏قبله‏ى صورت پرستان نقش سنگ
entries.english: The qibla of the spiritual is patience and long-suffering; the qibla of form worshippers is the image of stone.

entries.farsi: قبله‏ى باطن نشينان ذو المنن / ‏قبله‏ى ظاهر پرستان روى زن
entries.english: The qibla of those who dwell on the inward is the Bounteous One; the qibla of those who worship the outward is a woman’s face.

entries.farsi: همچنين بر مى‏شمر تازه و كهن / ‏ور ملولى رو تو كار خويش كن
entries.english: Similarly reckon up new and old; and if you are weary, go about your business.

entries.farsi: رزق ما در كاس زرين شد عقار / و آن سگان را آب تتماج و تغار
entries.english: Our provision is wine in a golden cup, while those curs have the tutmáj broth and the trough.

entries.farsi: اليق آن كه بدو خو داده‏ايم / ‏در خور آن رزق بفرستاده‏ايم
entries.english: “To him on whom We have bestowed a disposition We have sent the appropriate provision accordingly.

entries.farsi: خوى آن را عاشق نان كرده‏ايم / ‏خوى اين را مست جانان كرده‏ايم
entries.english: We have made it that one’s disposition to be passionately fond of bread; We have made it this one’s disposition to be intoxicated with the Beloved.”

entries.farsi: چون به خوى خود خوشى و خرمى / ‏پس چه از در خورد خويت مى‏رمى
entries.english: Since you are pleased and happy with your disposition, then why are you fleeing from that which is appropriate to your disposition?

entries.farsi: مادگى خوش آمدت چادر بگير / رستمى خوش آمدت خنجر بگير
entries.english: If femininity pleases you, get a chador; if the prowess of Rustam pleases you, get a dagger.

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد و آن فقير / گشته است از زخم درويشى عقير
entries.english: This topic has no end, and the fakir has been sorely wounded by the blows of penury.

sections.title_fa: ‏قصه‏ى آن گنج نامه كه پهلوى قبه‏اى روى به قبله كن و تير در كمان نه و بينداز، آن جا كه افتد گنج است
sections.title_en: Story of the treasure-scroll, “Beside a certain domed building turn your face towards the qibla (Mecca) and put an arrow to the bow and shoot: the treasure is at the spot where it falls.”

entries.farsi: ديد در خواب او شبى و خواب كو / واقعه‏ى بى‏خواب صوفى راست خو
entries.english: One night he dreamed—but where was sleep? The vision without sleep is familiar to the Sufi—

entries.farsi: هاتفى گفتش كه اى ديده تعب / ‏رقعه‏اى در مشق وراقان طلب
entries.english: A heavenly voice said to him, “O you who have seen trouble, search among the leaves of handwriting sold by stationers for a certain scroll.

entries.farsi: خفيه ز آن وراق كت همسايه است / ‏سوى كاغذ پاره‏هاش آور تو دست
entries.english: Unobserved by the stationer who is your neighbour, bring your hand into touch with his papers.

entries.farsi: رقعه‏اى شكلش چنين رنگش چنين / ‏پس بخوان آن را به خلوت اى حزين
entries.english: It is a scroll of such a shape and such a colour: then read it in privacy, O sorrowful one.

entries.farsi: چون بدزدى آن ز وراق اى پسر / پس برون رو ز انبهى و شور و شر
entries.english: When you steal it from the stationer, my lad, then go out of the crowd and the noise and turmoil,

entries.farsi: تو بخوان آن را به خود در خلوتى / ‏هين مجو در خواندن آن شوكتى
entries.english: And read it by yourself in some lonely place: beware; do not seek any partnership in reading it.

entries.farsi: ور شود آن فاش هم غمگين مشو / كه نيابد غير تو ز آن نيم جو
entries.english: But even if it be divulged, do not be anxious, for none but you will get half a barley-corn thereof.

entries.farsi: ور كشد آن دير هان زنهار تو / ورد خود كن دم به دم ال تقنطوا
entries.english: And if it be long drawn out, beware and take heed! Make do not despair your litany at every moment.”

entries.farsi: اين بگفت و دست خود آن مژده‏ور / بر دل او زد كه رو زحمت ببر
entries.english: The announcer of the good news said this and put his hand on his heart, saying, “Go, and endure the toil.”

entries.farsi: چون به خويش آمد ز غيبت آن جوان / ‏مى‏نگنجيد از فرح اندر جهان
entries.english: When the youth came back to himself after the absence, on account of his joy he could not be contained in the world.

entries.farsi: زهره‏ى او بر دريدى از قلق / ‏گر نبودى رفق و حفظ و لطف حق
entries.english: Had it not been for the tender care and protection and favour of God, his gallbladder would have burst from agitation.

entries.farsi: يك فرح آن كز پس ششصد حجاب / ‏گوش او بشنيد از حضرت جواب
entries.english: One cause of joy was this, that after six hundred veils his ear had heard the answer from the Presence.

entries.farsi: از حجب چون حس سمعش در گذشت / ‏شد سرافراز و ز گردون بر گذشت
entries.english: When his auditory sense had pierced through the veils, he raised his head aloft and passed beyond the skies,

entries.farsi: كه بود كآن حس چشمش ز اعتبار / ز آن حجاب غيب هم يابد گذار
entries.english: That maybe, by taking the lesson to heart, his sense of sight would also find a passage through the veil of the Unseen,

entries.farsi: چون گذاره شد حواسش از حجاب / ‏پس پياپى گرددش ديد و خطاب
entries.english: And that when his senses had passed through the veil, his vision and allocution would then be continuous.

entries.farsi: جانب دكان وراق آمد او / دست مى‏برد او به مشقش سو به سو
entries.english: He came to the stationer’s shop and was laying his hand here and there on his models for writing.

entries.farsi: پيش چشمش آمد آن مكتوب زود / با عالماتى كه هاتف گفته بود
entries.english: Suddenly that piece of script, with the distinctive marks which the heavenly voice had mentioned, caught his eye.

entries.farsi: در بغل زد گفت خواجه خير باد / اين زمان وا مى‏رسم اى اوستاد
entries.english: He slipped it under his arm and said, “Good-bye, Khwaja: I will come back presently, O master.”

entries.farsi: رفت كنج خلوتى و آن را بخواند / وز تحير واله و حيران بماند
entries.english: He went into a solitary nook and read it and remained lost in bewilderment and amazement,

entries.farsi: باز اندر خاطرش اين فكر جست / ‏كز پى هر چيز يزدان حافظ است
entries.english: How a priceless treasure-scroll of this sort had fallen and been left among the (stationer’s) papers.

entries.farsi: كه بدين سان گنج نامه‏ى بى‏بها / چون فتاده ماند اندر مشقها
entries.english: Again the thought darted into his mind, that God is the guardian for everything,

entries.farsi: كى گذارد حافظ اندر اكتناف / ‏كه كسى چيزى ربايد از گزاف
entries.english: How should the Guardian, in circumspection, let anyone recklessly carry off anything?

entries.farsi: گر بيابان پر شود زر و نقود / بى‏رضاى حق جوى نتوان ربود
entries.english: Though the desert is filled with gold and money, not a single mite can be taken away without God’s approval;

entries.farsi: ور بخوانى صد صحف بى‏سكته‏اى / ‏بى‏قدر يادت نماند نكته‏اى
entries.english: And though you read a hundred volumes without a pause, you will not remember a single point without the Divine decree;

entries.farsi: ور كنى خدمت نخوانى يك كتاب / ‏علمهاى نادره يابى ز جيب
entries.english: But if you serve God and do not read a single book, you will learn rare sciences from your bosom.

entries.farsi: شد ز جيب آن كف موسى ضو فشان / ‏كآن فزون آمد ز ماه آسمان
entries.english: The hand of Moses was spreading from his bosom a radiance that surpassed the moon in the sky,

entries.farsi: كآن كه مى‏جستى ز چرخ با نهيب / ‏سر بر آورده‏ست اى موسى ز جيب
entries.english: Saying, “That which you were seeking from the terrible celestial sphere has risen up, O Moses, from your own bosom,

entries.farsi: تا بدانى كآسمانهاى سمى / ‏هست عكس مدركات آدمى
entries.english: In order that you may know that the lofty heavens are the reflection of the perceptive faculties of Man.”

entries.farsi: نى كه اول دست يزدان مجيد / از دو عالم پيشتر عقل آفريد
entries.english: Is it not that the hand of the Glorious God created Reason first, before the two worlds?

entries.farsi: اين سخن پيدا و پنهان است بس / ‏كه نباشد محرم عنقا مگس
entries.english: This discourse is clear and exceedingly recondite, for the fly is not intimate with the Anqa.

entries.farsi: باز سوى قصه باز آ اى پسر / قصه‏ى گنج و فقير آور به سر
entries.english: O son, return once more to the tale: bring the tale of the treasure and the fakir to an end.

sections.title_fa: ‏تمامى قصه‏ى آن فقير و نشان جاى آن گنج
sections.title_en: Conclusion of the Story of the fakir and the signs indicating the position of the treasure.

entries.farsi: آن فالن قبه كه در وى مشهد است / ‏پشت او در شهر و در در فدفد است
entries.english: This is what was written in the scroll— “Know that outside of the town a treasure is buried.

entries.farsi: اندر آن رقعه نبشته بود اين / ‏كه برون شهر گنجى دان دفين
entries.english: Such-and-such a domed building in which there is a martyr’s shrine, with its back to the town and its gate towards the desert.

entries.farsi: پشت با وى كن تو رو در قبله آر / و آنگهان از قوس تيرى در گذار
entries.english: Turn your back to it and face the qibla and then let loose an arrow from your bow.

entries.farsi: چون فگندى تير از قوس اى سعاد / بر كن آن موضع كه تيرت اوفتاد
entries.english: When you have shot the arrow from your bow, O fortunate one, dig up the place where your arrow fell.”

entries.farsi: زو تبر آورد و بيل او شاد شاد / كند آن موضع كه تيرش اوفتاد
entries.english: Thereupon the youth fetched a strong bow and let fly an arrow into the expanse of space,

entries.farsi: پس كمان سخت آورد آن فتى / تير پرانيد در صحن فضا
entries.english: And quickly and with great joy brought a pick-axe and mattock and dug up the spot where his arrow had fallen;

entries.farsi: كند شد هم او و هم بيل و تبر / خود نديد از گنج پنهانى اثر
entries.english: Both he and the mattock and pick-axe were worn out, and he found not even a trace of the hidden treasure.

entries.farsi: همچنين هر روز تير انداختى / ‏ليك جاى گنج را نشناختى
entries.english: Every day in like fashion he was shooting arrows, but never getting to know the situation of the treasure.

entries.farsi: چون كه اين را پيشه كرد او بر دوام / ‏فجفجى در شهر افتاد و عوام
entries.english: Since he made this his continual practice, a whispered rumour arose in the city and the people.

sections.title_fa: ‏فاش شدن خبر اين گنج و رسيدن به گوش پادشاه
sections.title_en: How the news of this treasure became known and reached the ears of the king.

entries.farsi: پس خبر كردند سلطان را از اين / ‏آن گروهى كه بدند اندر كمين
entries.english: Then the party who lay in ambush gave information of this to the king,

entries.farsi: عرضه كردند آن سخن را زير دست / ‏كه فالنى گنج‏نامه يافته‏ست
entries.english: And submitted the matter secretly, saying that such-and-such a one had found a treasure-scroll.

entries.farsi: چون شنيد آن شخص كاين با شه رسيد / جز كه تسليم و رضا چاره نديد
entries.english: When this person heard that it had come to the king, he saw no remedy but resignation and acquiescence;

entries.farsi: پيش از آنك اشكنجه بيند ز آن قباد / رقعه را آن شخص پيش او نهاد
entries.english: Before he should suffer the rack by order of the Emperor, that person laid the note before him,

entries.farsi: گفت تا اين رقعه را يابيده‏ام / ‏گنج نه و رنج بى‏حد ديده‏ام
entries.english: Saying, “since I found this scroll, I have seen no treasure but infinite trouble.

entries.farsi: خود نشد يك حبه از گنج آشكار / ليك پيچيدم بسى من همچو مار
entries.english: Not even a single mite of treasure has been discovered, but I have writhed very much, like a snake.

entries.farsi: مدت ماهى چنينم تلخ كام / ‏كه زيان و سود اين بر من حرام
entries.english: During a month I have been in bitter distress like this, for loss or gain from this is forbidden to me.

entries.farsi: بو كه بختت بر كند زين كان غطا / اى شه پيروز جنگ و دژگشا
entries.english: Maybe your fortune will disclose this mine, O king victorious in war and the conqueror of fortresses.”

entries.farsi: مدت شش ماه و افزون پادشاه / ‏تير مى‏انداخت و بر مى‏كند چاه
entries.english: For six long months and more the king shot arrows and dug pits.

entries.farsi: هر كجا سخته كمانى بود چست / ‏تير داد انداخت و هر سو گنج جست
entries.english: Wherever an energetic drawer of the strong bow was, he gave arrows to shoot and searched for the treasure in every direction.

entries.farsi: غير تشويش و غم و طامات نى / ‏همچو عنقا نام فاش و ذات نى
entries.english: Nothing but vexation and grief and futilities: as the Anqa, the name was known to all, but the essence was non-existent.

sections.title_fa: ‏نوميد شدن آن پادشاه از يافتن آن گنج و ملول شدن او از طلب آن
sections.title_en: How the king despaired of finding the treasure and became weary of searching for it.

entries.farsi: چون كه تعويق آمد اندر عرض و طول / ‏شاه شد ز آن گنج دل سرد و ملول
entries.english: When he met with obstacles in the breadth and length, the king became sick at heart and weary.

entries.farsi: دشتها را گز گز آن شه چاه كند / رقعه را از خشم پيش او فگند
entries.english: The king dug pits in the deserts, yard by yard, he threw the scroll wrathfully before him.

entries.farsi: گفت گير اين رقعه كش آثار نيست / ‏تو بدين اوليترى كت كار نيست
entries.english: “Take this scroll,” said he, “which has no effects; you are the fittest for it, since you have no work.

entries.farsi: نيست اين كار كسى كش هست كار / كه بسوزد گل بگردد گرد خار
entries.english: It is no use for one who has work (to do) that he should burn the rose and go about the thorn.

entries.farsi: سخت جانى بايد اين فن را چو تو / تو كه دارى جان سخت اين را بجو
entries.english: It is singular the victims of this melancholy madness expect grass to grow from iron.

entries.farsi: نادر افتد اهل اين ماخوليا / منتظر كه رويد از آهن گيا
entries.english: This specialty needs a man of stout heart like you: do you, who have a stout heart, search for this.

entries.farsi: گر نيابى نبودت هرگز مالل / ‏ور بيابى آن به تو كردم حالل
entries.english: If you cannot find it, you will never weary; and if you find it, I grant you the right of possession.”

entries.farsi: عقل راه نااميدى كى رود / عشق باشد كآن طرف بر سر دود
entries.english: How should Reason wend the way of despair? It is Love that runs on its head in that direction.

entries.farsi: ال ابالى عشق باشد نى خرد / عقل آن جويد كز آن سودى برد
entries.english: Love is reckless, not Reason: Reason seeks that from which it may get some profit.

entries.farsi: ترك تاز و تن گداز و بى‏حيا / در بال چون سنگ زير آسيا
entries.english: Fierce in onset and body-consuming and unabashed: in tribulation, like the nether millstone;

entries.farsi: سخت رويى كه ندارد هيچ پشت / ‏بهره جويى را درون خويش كشت
entries.english: A hard-faced one that has no back: he has killed in himself the seeking of self interest.

entries.farsi: پاك مى‏بازد نباشد مزد جو / آن چنان كه پاك مى‏گيرد ز هو
entries.english: He gambles clean away, he seeks no reward, even as he receives clean from Him.

entries.farsi: كه فتوت دادن بى‏علت است / ‏پاك بازى خارج هر ملت است
entries.english: God gives him his existence without any cause: the devoted yields it up again without cause;

entries.farsi: مى‏دهد حق هستى‏اش بى‏علتى / ‏مى‏سپارد باز بى‏علت فتى
entries.english: For devotion consists in giving without cause: gambling clean away is outside of every religion.

entries.farsi: ز انكه ملت فضل جويد يا خالص / ‏پاك بازانند قربانان خاص
entries.english: Forasmuch as religion seeks grace or salvation, those who gamble clean away are chosen favourites.

entries.farsi: نى خدا را امتحانى مى‏كنند / نى در سود و زيانى مى‏زنند
entries.english: Neither do they put God to any test, nor do they knock at the door of any profit or loss.

sections.title_fa: ‏باز دادن پادشاه گنج‏نامه را به آن فقير كه بگير، ما از سر اين برخاستيم
sections.title_en: How the king gave back the treasure-scroll to the fakir, saying, “Take it: we are quit of it.”

entries.farsi: چون كه رقعه‏ى گنج پر آشوب را / شه مسلم داشت آن مكروب را
entries.english: When the king handed over to that grief-stricken man the treasure-scroll fraught with commotion,

entries.farsi: گشت ايمن او ز خصمان و ز نيش / ‏رفت و مى‏پيچيد در سوداى خويش
entries.english: He became secure from rivals and annoyance; he went and wrapped himself in his melancholy madness.

entries.farsi: يار كرد او عشق درد انديش را / كلب ليسد خويش ريش خويش را
entries.english: He made sad-thoughted Love his friend: a dog licks his own sore himself.

entries.farsi: عشق را در پيچش خود يار نيست / ‏محرمش در ده يكى ديار نيست
entries.english: Love has none to help him in his torment: there is not in the village one inhabitant familiar with him.

entries.farsi: نيست از عاشق كسى ديوانه‏تر / عقل از سوداى او كور است و كر
entries.english: None is madder than the lover, Reason is blind and deaf to his melancholia,

entries.farsi: ز انكه اين ديوانگى عام نيست / ‏طب را ارشاد اين احكام نيست
entries.english: Because this is no common madness: in these cases Medicine cannot give right guidance.

entries.farsi: طب جمله‏ى عقلها منقوش اوست / ‏روى جمله‏ى دلبران رو پوش اوست
entries.english: If frenzy of this kind overtake a physician, he will wash out the book of Medicine with blood.

entries.farsi: گر طبيبى را رسد زين گون جنون / ‏دفتر طب را فرو شويد به خون
entries.english: The Medicine of all intellects is a picture of him; the faces of all sweethearts are a veil of him.

entries.farsi: روى در روى خود آر اى عشق كيش / ‏نيست اى مفتون ترا جز خويش خويش
entries.english: O votary of Love; turn your face towards your own face: you have no kinsman but yourself, O distraught one.

entries.farsi: قبله از دل ساخت آمد در دعا / ‏لَيْسَ لِلإِْنْسانِ إِلاَّ ما سعى
entries.english: He made a qibla of his heart and began to pray: man has naught but that for which he labours.

entries.farsi: بى‏اجابت بر دعاها مى‏تنيد / از كرم لبيك پنهان مى‏شنيد
entries.english: Before he had heard any answer he had been engaged in praying for years.

entries.farsi: پيش از آن كاو پاسخى بشنيده بود / سالها اندر دعا پيچيده بود
entries.english: He was always praying intently without any response, but he was hearing Labbayka in secret from the grace.

entries.farsi: چون كه بى‏دف رقص مى‏كرد آن عليل / ‏ز اعتماد جود خالق جليل
entries.english: Since that sickly man was always dancing without the tambourine, in reliance upon the bounty of the Almighty Creator,

entries.farsi: بى‏زبان مى‏گفت اوميدش تعال / ‏از دلش مى‏روفت آن دعوت مالل
entries.english: Neither a heavenly voice nor a messenger was beside him, the ear of his hope was filled with Labbayka;

entries.farsi: سوى او نه هاتف و نه پيك بود / گوش اوميدش پر از لبيك بود
entries.english: His hope was always saying, without tongue, “Come!” and that call was sweeping weariness from his heart.

entries.farsi: آن كبوتر را كه بام آموخته‏ست / ‏تو مخوان مى‏رانش كآن پر دوخته‏ست
entries.english: Do not call the pigeon that has learned the roof: drive it away, for its wings are stuck.

entries.farsi: اى ضياء الحق حسام الدين برانش / ‏كز مالقات تو بر رسته‏ست جانش
entries.english: Do you, O Radiance of God, Husámu’ddín, drive him away, for through meeting with you his spirit has grown up in him.

entries.farsi: چينه و نقلش همه بر بام تست / ‏پر زنان بر اوج مست دام تست
entries.english: If you unconscionably drive away the bird, his spirit, it will still circle about your roof.

entries.farsi: گر برانى مرغ جانش از گزاف / ‏هم به گرد بام تو آرد طواف
entries.english: All its grain and food is on your roof: flying in the zenith, it is intoxicated with your snare.

entries.farsi: گر دمى منكر شود دزدانه روح / ‏در اداى شكرت اى فتح و فتوح
entries.english: If for one moment the spirit stealthily disbelieve in rendering thanks to you, O victory and favour,

entries.farsi: شحنه‏ى عشق مكرر كينه‏اش / ‏طشت آتش مى‏نهد بر سينه‏اش
entries.english: Love, the magistrate who exacts vengeance repeatedly, will lay the fiery cauldron on its breast,

entries.farsi: كه بيا سوى مه و بگذر ز گرد / شاه عشقت خواند زوتر باز گرد
entries.english: Saying, “Come to the Moon and leave the dust behind; Love, the King, calls you: return with all speed!”

entries.farsi: گرد اين بام و كبوتر خانه من / ‏چون كبوتر پر زنم مستانه من
entries.english: I am flying ecstatically, like a pigeon, about this roof and pigeon-house.

entries.farsi: جبرئيل عشقم و سدره‏م توى / ‏من سقيمم عيسى مريم توى
entries.english: I am Love’s Gabriel, and you are my Lotus-tree; I am the sick man, and you are Jesus son of Mary.

entries.farsi: جوش ده آن بحر گوهربار را / خوش بپرس امروز اين بيمار را
entries.english: Let that pearl-shedding sea break into surge: to-day ask kindly after this ailing one.

entries.farsi: چون تو آن او شدى بحر آن اوست / ‏گر چه اين دم نوبت بحران اوست
entries.english: When you have become his, the sea is his, even though this is the hour of his crisis.

entries.farsi: اين خود آن ناله است كاو كرد آشكار / آن چه پنهان است يا رب زينهار
entries.english: This Masnavi is only the wailful music that he has uttered; that which is hidden, mercy, O Lord!

entries.farsi: دو دهان داريم گويا همچو نى / ‏يك دهان پنهانست در لبهاى وى
entries.english: We have two vocal mouths, like the reed: one mouth is hidden in his lips.
