entries.english: I will sacrifice my life for you in raising you to your feet. You are a Rustam, a lion: come on, be manful!’”

entries.farsi: سوى كفرش آورد زين عشوه‏ها / آن جوال خدعه و مكر و دها
entries.english: By means of these wiles that bag of deceit and cunning and craft leads him to infidelity.

entries.farsi: هى بيا من طمعها دارم ز تو / گويدش رو رو كه بيزارم ز تو
entries.english: As soon as he sets foot and falls into the moat, he opens his lips with a loud ha, ha.

entries.farsi: چون قدم بنهاد در خندق فتاد / او بقاها قاه خنده لب گشاد
entries.english: “Hey, come! I have hopes of you.” He says, “Begone, leave, for I am quit of you.

entries.farsi: تو نترسيدى ز عدل كردگار / من همى‏ترسم دو دست از من بدار
entries.english: You did not fear the justice of the Creator, I fear: keep your hands off me!”

entries.farsi: گفت حق خود او جدا شد از بهى / ‏تو بدين تزويرها هم كى رهى
entries.english: God says, “He, indeed, is parted from felicity, and how should you be saved by these hypocrisies?”

entries.farsi: فاعل و مفعول در روز شمار / رو سياهند و حريف سنگسار
entries.english: On the Day of Reckoning the active and passive will be shamed-faced and partners in stoning.

entries.farsi: ره زده و ره زن يقين در حكم وداد / در چه بعدند و در بئس المهاد
entries.english: Assuredly, by the decree and just dispensation, the waylaid and the waylayer are in the pit of farness and in an evil resting-place.

entries.farsi: گول را و غول را كاو را فريفت / ‏از خالص و فوز مى‏بايد شكيفت
entries.english: The fool and the ghoul who deceived him must ever endure to be deprived of salvation and felicity.

entries.farsi: هم خر و خر گير اينجا در گلند / غافلند اينجا و آن جا آفلند
entries.english: Both the ass and he that caught the ass are in the mud here: here they are forgetful of and there they are sunk—

entries.farsi: جز كسانى را كه وا گردند از ان / ‏در بهار فضل آيند از خزان
entries.english: Except those who turn back from that and come from the autumn into the springtide of grace,

entries.farsi: توبه آرند و خدا توبه پذير / امر او گيرند و او نعم الامير
entries.english: And who repent, for God is ready to accept repentance, and cleave to His command, for a goodly Commander is He!

entries.farsi: چون بر آرند از پشيمانى حنين / ‏عرش لرزد از انين المذنبين
entries.english: When, by sorrow, they raise a piteous cry, the highest Heaven trembles at the moaning of the sinners.

entries.farsi: آن چنان لرزد كه مادر بر ولد / دستشان گيرد به بالا مى‏كشد
entries.english: It trembles even as a mother for her child: it takes them by the hand and draws them upward,

entries.farsi: كاى خداتان واخريده از غرور / نك رياض فضل و نك رب غفور
entries.english: Saying, “O you whom God has redeemed from delusion, behold the gardens of grace and behold the forgiving Lord!

entries.farsi: بعد ازينتان برگ و رزق جاودان / ‏از هواى حق بود نه از ناودان
entries.english: Henceforth you have everlasting provision and sustenance from God’s air, not from the gutter.”

entries.farsi: چون كه دريا بر وسايط رشك كرد / تشنه چون ماهى بترك مشك كرد
entries.english: Inasmuch as the Sea is jealous of intermediaries, he that is thirsty as a fish takes leave of the water-skin.

sections.title_fa: روان شدن شه زادگان در ممالك پدر بعد از وداع كردن ايشان شاه را و اعادت كردن شاه وقت وداع وصيت را
sections.title_en: How the princes, having bidden the King farewell, set out on a journey through their father’s em­ pire, and how the King repeated his injunctions at the moment of farewell.

entries.farsi: عزم ره كردند آن هر سه پسر / سوى امالك پدر رسم سفر
entries.english: The three sons set out, in the fashion of travel, to their father’s possessions,

entries.farsi: در طواف شهرها و قلعه‏هاش / ‏از پى تدبير ديوان و معاش
entries.english: And to make a tour of his cities and fortresses for the purpose of regulating the administrative and economic conditions.

entries.farsi: دستبوس شاه كردند و وداع / ‏پس بديشان گفت آن شاه مطاع
entries.english: They kissed the King’s hand and bade him farewell; then the King, obeyed, said to them:

entries.farsi: هر كجاتان دل كشد عازم شويد / فى امان اللهَّ دست افشان رويد
entries.english: “Direct your course wherever your heart may lead you, go under the protection of God, waving your hands.

entries.farsi: اللهَّ اللهَّ ز آن دژ ذات الصور / دور باشيد و بترسيد از خطر
entries.english: Except to one fortress, the name of which is ‘the robber of reason’: it makes the coat tight for wearers of the tiara.

entries.farsi: غير آن يك قلعه نامش هش ربا / تنگ آرد بر كله داران قبا
entries.english: For God’s sake, for God’s sake, keep far away from that castle adorned with pictures, and beware of the peril!

entries.farsi: رو و پشت برجهاش و سقف و پست / ‏جمله تمثال و نگار و صورت است
entries.english: The front and back of its towers and its roof and floor are all images and decorations and pictures,

entries.farsi: همچو آن حجره‏ى زليخا پر صور / تا كند يوسف به ناكامش نظر
entries.english: Like the chamber of Zalikha full of pictures in order that Joseph should look upon her willy-nilly.

entries.farsi: چون كه يوسف سوى او مى‏ننگريد / خانه را پر نقش خود كرد از مكيد
entries.english: Since Joseph would not look at her, she cunningly filled the room with portraits of herself,

entries.farsi: بهر ديده روشنان يزدان فرد / شش جهت را مظهر آيات كرد
entries.english: So that, wherever the fair-cheeked looked, he might see her face without having the power to choose.

entries.farsi: تا به هر سو كه نگرد آن خوش عذار / روى او را بيند او بى‏اختيار
entries.english: The peerless God has made the six directions a theatre for the display of His signs to the clairvoyant,

entries.farsi: تا به هر حيوان و نامى كه نگرند / از رياض حسن ربانى چرند
entries.english: In order that, whatever animal or plant they look upon, they may feed on the meadows of Divine Beauty.

entries.farsi: بهر اين فرمود با آن اسپه او / ‏حيث وليتم فثم وجهه
entries.english: Hence He said to the company, ‘Wherever you turn, His Face is there.

entries.farsi: آنكه عاشق نيست او در آب در / صورت خود بيند اى صاحب بصر
entries.english: If in thirst you drink some water from a cup, you are beholding God within the water.’

entries.farsi: از قدح گر در عطش آبى خوريد / در درون آب حق را ناظريد
entries.english: He that is not a lover sees in the water his own image, O man of insight;

entries.farsi: صورت عاشق چو فانى شد در او / پس در آب اكنون كه را بنيد بگو
entries.english: Since the lover’s image has disappeared in Him, whom now should he behold in the water? Tell!

entries.farsi: حسن حق بيند اندر روى حور / همچو مه در آب از صنع غيور
entries.english: Through the working of the Jealous One, they behold the beauty of God in the faces of the houris, like the moon in water.

entries.farsi: غيرتش بر عاشقى و صادقى است / ‏غيرتش بر ديو و بر استور نيست
entries.english: His jealousy is against a lover and sincere; His jealousy is not against a devil and beast;

entries.farsi: ديو اگر عاشق شود هم گوى برد / جبرئيلى گشت و آن ديوى بمرد
entries.english: If the devil becomes a lover, he has carried off the palm: he has become a Gabriel and his devilish nature is dead.

entries.farsi: اسلم الشيطان آن جا شد پديد / كه يزيدى شد ز فضلش بايزيد
entries.english: The Devil became a true believer’ is made manifest on the occasion when by His grace a Yazíd becomes a Bayazid.

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد اين گروه / ‏هين نگه داريد ز آن قلعه وجوه
entries.english: This topic is endless. Beware, O company, keep your faces from that fortress!

entries.farsi: هين مبادا كه هوستان ره زند / كه فتيد اندر شقاوت تا ابد
entries.english: Oh, let not vain desire waylay you, or you will fall into everlasting misery.

entries.farsi: از خطر پرهيز آمد مفترض / ‏بشنويد از من حديث بى‏غرض
entries.english: It is a bounden duty to abstain from peril: hear disinterested advice from me.

entries.farsi: در فرج جويى خرد سر تيز به / ‏از كمين‏گاه بال پرهيز به
entries.english: In seeking relief it is better that one’s wits should be sharp: it is better to abstain from the ambuscade of tribulation.”

entries.farsi: گر نمى‏گفت اين سخن را آن پدر / ور نمى‏فرمود ز آن قلعه حذر
entries.english: If their father had not spoken these words and had not warned them against that fortress,

entries.farsi: خود بدان قلعه نمى‏شد خيلشان / ‏خود نمى‏افتاد آن سو ميلشان
entries.english: Their party would never have approached the fortress; their desire would never have inclined towards it;

entries.farsi: كان نبد معروف بس مهجور بود / از قالع و از مناهج دور بود
entries.english: For it was not well-known: it was exceedingly remote and aloof from the fortresses and the highways.

entries.farsi: چون بكرد آن منع دلشان ز آن مقال / ‏در هوس افتاد و در كوى خيال
entries.english: When he uttered that prohibition, their hearts were thrown by his speech into vain desire and into the quarter of phantasy,

entries.farsi: رغبتى زين منع در دلشان برست / ‏كه ببايد سر آن را باز جست
entries.english: And, because of this prohibition, a craving arose in their hearts to investigate the secret of that.

entries.farsi: كيست كز ممنوع گردد ممتنع / ‏چون كه الانسان حريص ما منع
entries.english: Who is that will refrain from the forbidden thing, since man longs eagerly for what is forbidden?

entries.farsi: نهى بر اهل تقى تبغيض شد / نهى بر اهل هوا تحريض شد
entries.english: The veto causes the devout to hate; the veto incites the sensual to covet it.

entries.farsi: پس از اين يغوى به قوما كثير / هم از اين يهدى به قلبا خبير
entries.english: Therefore He leads many folk astray by this means, and by the same means He guides aright a knowing heart.

entries.farsi: كى رمد از نى حمام آشنا / بل رمد ز آن نى حمامات هوا
entries.english: How should the friendly dove be scared by the pipe? Nay, the doves in the air are scared by that pipe.

entries.farsi: رو نگردانيم از فرمان تو / كفر باشد غفلت از احسان تو
entries.english: Then they said to him, “We will perform the services, we will be intent on hearing and obeying.

entries.farsi: پس بگفتندش كه خدمتها كنيم / ‏بر سمعنا و اطعناها تنيم
entries.english: We will not turn aside from your commands: it would be ingratitude to forget your kindness”;

entries.farsi: ليك استثنا و تسبيح خدا / ز اعتماد خود بد از ايشان جدا
entries.english: But, because of their reliance upon themselves, it was far from them to pronounce the saving clause and glorify God.

entries.farsi: ذكر استثنا و حزم ملتوى / ‏گفته شد در ابتداى مثنوى
entries.english: Mention of the saving clause and manifold precaution was made at the beginning of the Masnavi.

entries.farsi: صد كتاب ار هست جز يك باب نيست / ‏صد جهت را قصد جز محراب نيست
entries.english: If there are a hundred books, they are but one chapter: a hundred regions seek but one place of worship.

entries.farsi: اين طرق را مخلصش يك خانه است / ‏اين هزاران سنبل از يك دانه است
entries.english: These roads end in one House: these thousand ears of corn are from one Seed.

entries.farsi: گونه گونه خوردنيها صد هزار / جمله يك چيز است اندر اعتبار
entries.english: All the hundred thousand sorts of food and drink are one thing in respect.

entries.farsi: از يكى چون سير گشتى تو تمام / ‏سرد شد اندر دلت پنجه طعام
entries.english: When you are entirely satiated with one, fifty foods become cold to your heart.

entries.farsi: در مجاعت پس تو احول ديده‏اى / ‏كه يكى را صد هزاران ديده‏اى
entries.english: In hunger, then, you are seeing double, for you have regarded a single one as a hundred thousand.

entries.farsi: گفته بوديم از سقام آن كنيز / و ز طبيبان و قصور فهم نيز
entries.english: We had told of the sickness of the handmaiden and of the physicians and also their lack of understanding—

entries.farsi: كان طبيبان همچو اسب بى‏عذار / غافل و بى‏بهره بودند از سوار
entries.english: How those physicians were like an unbridled horse, heedless of the rider and having no profit.

entries.farsi: كامشان پر زخم از قرع لگام / ‏سمشان مروح از تحويل گام
entries.english: Their palates were covered with sores made by the impact of the bit, and their hooves wounded by changing step,

entries.farsi: ناشده واقف كه نك بر پشت ما / رايض چستى است استادى نما
entries.english: They had not become aware, “Lo, on our back is a nimble Trainer who displays masterly skill.

entries.farsi: نيست سر گردانى ما زين لگام / ‏جز ز تصريف سوار دوست كام
entries.english: Our turning the head to and fro is not caused by this bit, but only by the control of a successful Rider.

entries.farsi: ما پى گل سوى بستانها شده / ‏گل نموده آن و آن خارى بده
entries.english: We went into the gardens to gather roses: they seemed to be roses, but they were thorns.”

entries.farsi: آن طبيبان آن چنان بنده‏ى سبب / ‏گشته‏اند از مكر يزدان محتجب
entries.english: It never occurred to them to ask, by reason, “Who is kicking our throats?”

entries.farsi: هيچشان اين نى كه گويند از خرد / بر گلوى ما كه مى‏كوبد لگد
entries.english: Those physicians so enthralled by the secondary cause they have become blind to God’s contrivance.

entries.farsi: از خرى باشد تغافل خفته‏وار / كه نجويى تا كى است آن خفيه كار
entries.english: If you tether an ox in a stall and then find an ass in the place of the ox,

entries.farsi: گر ببندى در صطبلى گاو نر / باز يابى در مقام گاو خر
entries.english: It would be asinine carelessness, like a man in slumber, not to inquire who the secret agent is.

entries.farsi: خود نگفته كاين مبدل تا كى است / ‏نيست پيدا او مگر افلاکى است
entries.english: You never said, “Let me see who this changer is: he is not visible; surely, he is a celestial being.”

entries.farsi: تير سوى راست پرانيده‏اى / ‏سوى چپ رفته است تيرت ديده‏اى
entries.english: You have shot an arrow to the right and have seen your arrow go to the left.

entries.farsi: سوى آهويى به صيدى تاختى / ‏خويش را تو صيد خوكى ساختى
entries.english: You have ridden in chase of a deer and have made yourself the prey of a hog.

entries.farsi: در پى سودى دويده بهر كبس / ‏نارسيده سود افتاده به حبس
entries.english: You have run after some gain for the purpose of stuffing yourself: the gain has not reached and you have been cast into prison.

entries.farsi: چاهها كنده براى ديگران / ‏خويش را ديده فتاده اندر آن
entries.english: You have dug pits for others and have seen yourself fall into them.

entries.farsi: در سبب چون بى‏مرادت كرد رب / ‏پس چرا بد ظن نگردى در سبب
entries.english: Since the Lord has disappointed you in regard to the means, then why do not you become suspicious of the means?

entries.farsi: بس كس از عقد زنان قارون شده / ‏بس كس از عقد زنان مديون شده
entries.english: Many a one has become an emperor by dint of toil, while another has been made destitute by that toil.

entries.farsi: بس كسى از مكسبى خاقان شده / ‏ديگرى ز آن مسكبه عريان شده
entries.english: Many a one has been made Qárún by marriage, and many a one has been made bankrupt by marriage.

entries.farsi: پس سبب گردان چو دم خر بود / تكيه بر وى كم كنى بهتر بود
entries.english: The means, then, is turning about, like the tail of an ass: it is better not to rely upon it.

entries.farsi: ور سبب گيرى نگيرى هم دلير / كه بس آفتهاست پنهانش به زير
entries.english: And if you take the means, you should not take it boldly, for beneath it there are many hidden annoyances.

entries.farsi: سر استثناست اين حزم و حذر / ز انكه خر را بز نمايد اين قدر
entries.english: This prudence and precaution is the gist of the saving clause, for this decree makes the ass appear to be a goat.

entries.farsi: آن كه چشمش بست گر چه گر بز است / ‏ز احولى اندر دو چشمش خر بز است
entries.english: Although he whose eye it has bandaged is clever, because of his seeing double, in his eyes the ass is a goat.

entries.farsi: چون مقلب حق بود ابصار را / كه بگرداند دل و افكار را
entries.english: Since God is the Turner of eyes, who should turn the heart and the thoughts?

entries.farsi: چاه را تو خانه‏اى بينى لطيف / ‏دام را تو دانه‏اى بينى ظريف
entries.english: You deem a pit to be a pleasant house; you deem a trap to be dainty bait.

entries.farsi: اين تسفسط نيست تقليب خداست / ‏مى‏نمايد كه حقيقتها كجاست
entries.english: This is not sophistry, it is God’s turning: it shows where the realities are.

entries.farsi: آن كه انكار حقايق مى‏كند / جملگى او بر خيالى مى‏تند
entries.english: He who denies the realities is wholly involved in a phantasy.

entries.farsi: او نمى‏گويد كه حسبان خيال / ‏هم خيالى باشدت چشمى بمال
entries.english: He does not say, “Your thinking phantasy is also a phantasy: rub an eye!”

sections.title_fa: ،رفتن پسران سلطان به حكم آن كه الانسان حريص على ما منع ‏خوب بد تو بنده ندانست خريدن ما بندگى خويش نموديم و ليكن به سوى آن قلعه‏ى ممنوع عنه، آن همه وصيتها و اندرزهاى پدر را زير پا نهادند تا در چاه بال افتاند ُو مى‏گفتند ايشان را نفوس لوامه أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌايشان مى‏گفتند گريان و پشيمان لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْ مَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِل
ِما كُنَّا فِي أَصْ حابِ السَّ عِير
sections.title_en: How the Sultan’s sons went to the forbidden fortress, inasmuch as man eagerly covets that which he is refused—“We rendered our service, but your evil nature could not buy the servant.”

They trod all their father’s injunctions and counsels underfoot, so that they fell into the pit of tribulation, and their reproachful souls were saying to them, “Did not a warner come to you?” while they, weeping and contrite, replied, “If we had been wont to listen or understand we should not have been among those who dwell in the flaming Fire.”

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد آن فريق / ‏بر گرفتند از پى آن دژ طريق
entries.english: This discourse has no end. The party took their way to seek that castle.

entries.farsi: بر درخت گندم منهى زدند / از طويله‏ى مخلصان بيرون شدند
entries.english: They approached the tree of the forbidden fruit; they went forth from the file of the sincere.

entries.farsi: چون شدند از منع و نهيش گرمتر / سوى آن قلعه بر آوردند سر
entries.english: Since they were made more ardent by their father’s prohibition and veto, they raised their heads towards that fortress.

entries.farsi: بر ستيز قول شاه مجتبى / تا به قلعه‏ى صبر سوز هش ربا
entries.english: In spite of the orders of the elect King to the fortress which is the destroyer of self-restraint and the robber of rationality.

entries.farsi: آمدند از رغم عقل پند توز / در شب تاريك بر گشته ز روز
entries.english: Turning their backs on the day, they came in the dark night in defiance of counsel-bestowing Reason

entries.farsi: اندر آن قلعه‏ى خوش ذات الصور / پنج در در بحر و پنجى سوى بر
entries.english: Into the beautiful fortress adorned with pictures, five gates to the sea and five to the land—

entries.farsi: پنج از آن چون حس به سوى رنگ و بو / پنج از آن چون حس باطن راز جو
entries.english: Five of those, like the senses, facing towards colour and perfume; five of them, like the interior senses, seeking the mystery.

entries.farsi: ز آن هزاران صورت و نقش و نگار / مى‏شدند از سو به سو خوش بى‏قرار
entries.english: By those thousands of pictures and designs and decorations they were made mightily restless to and fro.

entries.farsi: زين قدحهاى صور كم باش مست / ‏تا نگردى بت تراش و بت پرست
entries.english: Do not be intoxicated with these cups, which are forms, lest you become a carver of idols and an idolater.

entries.farsi: از قدحهاى صور بگذر مه ايست / ‏باده در جام است ليك از جام نيست
entries.english: Abandon the cups, namely, the forms: do not tarry! There is wine in the cup, but it is not from the cup.

entries.farsi: سوى باده بخش بگشا پهن فم / ‏چون رسد باده نيايد جام كم
entries.english: Open your mouth wide to the Giver of the wine: when the wine comes, the cup will not be lacking.

entries.farsi: آدما معنى دل بندم بجوى / ‏ترك قشر و صورت گندم بگوى
entries.english: “O Adam, seek My heart-enthralling Reality: take leave of the husk and form of the wheat.”

entries.farsi: چون كه رنگى آرد شد بهر خليل / ‏دان كه معزول است گندم اى نبيل
entries.english: Since sand was turned into flour for the Friend, know that the wheat is deposed from its office, O noble one.

entries.farsi: صورت از بى‏صورت آيد در وجود / همچنانك از آتشى زاده‏ست دود
entries.english: Form is brought into existence by the Formless, just as smoke is produced by a fire.

entries.farsi: كمترين عيب مصور در خصال / ‏چون پياپى بينى‏اش آيد مالل
entries.english: The least blemish in the qualities of that which is endowed with form becomes annoying when you regard it continually;

entries.farsi: حيرت محض آردت بى‏صورتى / ‏زاده صد گون آلت از بى‏آلتى
entries.english: Formlessness throws you into absolute bewilderment: from non-instrumentality a hundred kinds of instruments are born.

entries.farsi: بى‏ز دستى دستها بافد همى / ‏جان جان سازد مصور آدمى
entries.english: Handlessness is weaving hands: the Soul of the soul makes a formed Man.

entries.farsi: آن چنانك اندر دل از هجر و وصال / ‏مى‏شود بافيده گوناگون خيال
entries.english: It is like as from separation and union diverse fancies are woven in the heart.

entries.farsi: هيچ ماند اين موثر با اثر / هيچ ماند بانگ و نوحه با ضرر
entries.english: Does this cause ever resemble its effect? Does the cry and lamentation ever resemble the loss?

entries.farsi: نوحه را صورت ضرر بى‏صورت است / ‏دست خايند از ضرر كه نيست دست
entries.english: The lamentation has a form, the loss is formless: they gnaw their hands on account of a loss that has no hand.

entries.farsi: اين مثل نالايق است اى مستدل / ‏حيله‏ى تفهيم را جهد المقل
entries.english: This comparison, O seeker of guidance, does not fit; it is the best effort a poor man can make to explain it.

entries.farsi: صنع بى‏صورت بكارد صورتى / ‏تن برويد با حواس و آلتى
entries.english: The formless working sows a form, there grows up a body endowed with senses and a faculty,

entries.farsi: تا چه صورت باشد آن بر وفق خود / اندر آرد جسم را در نيك و بد
entries.english: So that the form, whatever it be, according to its own nature brings the body into good or evil.

entries.farsi: صورت رحمى بود بالان شود / صورت زخمى بود نالان شود
entries.english: If it be a form of beneficence, it turns to thanksgiving; if it be a form of deferment, it becomes patient;

entries.farsi: صورت نعمت بود شاكر شود / صورت مهلت بود صابر شود
entries.english: If it be a form of mercy, it becomes flourishing; if it be a form of repulse, it becomes full of moans;

entries.farsi: صورت شهرى بود گيرد سفر / صورت تيرى بود گيرد سپر
entries.english: If it be the form of a city, it takes a journey; if it be the form of an arrow, it takes a shield;

entries.farsi: صورت خوبان بود عشرت كند / صورت غيبى بود خلوت كند
entries.english: If it be the form of fair ones, it indulges in enjoyment; if it be a form of the unseen world, it practices religious seclusion.

entries.farsi: صورت محتاجى آرد سوى كسب / ‏صورت باز و ورى آرد به غصب
entries.english: The form of want leads to earn; the form of strength of arm leads to seize by force.

entries.farsi: اين ز حد و اندازه‏ها باشد برون / ‏داعى فعل از خيال گونه گون
entries.english: These are boundless and immeasurable: the motive to action from various sorts of phantasy.

entries.farsi: بر لب بام ايستاده قوم خوش / ‏هر يكى را بر زمين بين سايه‏اش
entries.english: All the infinite ways of life and the crafts are the shadow of the form of thoughts.

entries.farsi: بى‏نهايت كيشها و پيشه‏ها / جمله ظل صورت انديشه‏ها
entries.english: Happy folk standing on the edge of a roof, observe the shadow of each one on the ground.

entries.farsi: صورت فكر است بر بام مشيد / و آن عمل چون سايه بر اركان پديد
entries.english: The form of thought is on the lofty roof, while the action appears, like a shadow, on the pillars.

entries.farsi: فعل بر اركان و فكرت مكتتم / ‏ليك در تاثير و وصلت دو بهم
entries.english: The action is on the pillars, while the thought is concealed; but the two are combined in the correlation of cause and effect.

entries.farsi: آن صور در بزم كز جام خوشى است / ‏فايده‏ى او بى‏خودى و بى‏هشى است
entries.english: The forms that arise at a banquet from the festive cup have as their result unconsciousness and senselessness.

entries.farsi: صورت مرد و زن و لعب و جماع / ‏فايده‏ش بى‏هوشى وقت وقاع
entries.english: The forms of man and woman and sport and sexual intercourse— their result at the time of sexual union distraction

entries.farsi: صورت نان و نمك كان نعمت است / ‏فايده‏ش آن قوت بى‏صورت است
entries.english: The form of bread and salt, which is a benefit, has as its result strength, which is formless.

entries.farsi: در مصاف آن صورت تيغ و سپر / فايده‏ش بى‏صورتى يعنى ظفر
entries.english: On the battle-field the form of sword and shield has as its result a formless thing, i.e. victory.

entries.farsi: مدرسه و تعليق و صورتهاى وى / ‏چون به دانش متصل شد گشت طى
entries.english: College and learning and the forms thereof are done with, as soon as they have reached knowledge.

entries.farsi: اين صور دارد ز بى‏صورت وجود / چيست پس بر موجد خويشش جحود
entries.english: Since these forms are the slaves of the Formless, why, then, are they denying their Benefactor?

entries.farsi: اين صور چون بنده‏ى بى‏صورتند / پس چرا در نفى صاحب نعمتند
entries.english: These forms have their existence from the Formless: what means, then, their denial of Him who brought them into existence?

entries.farsi: خود از او يابد ظهور انكار او / نيست غير عكس خود اين كار او
entries.english: His disbelief is really manifested by Him: in truth this act of his is nothing but a reflection.

entries.farsi: صورت ديوار و سقف هر مكان / ‏سايه‏ى انديشه‏ى معمار دان
entries.english: Know that the form of the walls and roof of every dwelling-place is a shadow of the thought of the architect,

entries.farsi: گر چه خود اندر محل افتكار / نيست سنگ و چوب و خشتى آشكار
entries.english: Even though in the seat of his thought there is no visible stone and wood and brick.

entries.farsi: فاعل مطلق يقين بى‏صورت است / ‏صورت اندر دست او چون آلت است
entries.english: Assuredly the Absolute Agent is formless: form is as a tool in His hand.

entries.farsi: گه گه آن بى‏صورت از كتم عدم / ‏مر صور را رو نمايد از كرم
entries.english: Sometimes the Formless One graciously shows His face to the forms from the concealment of non-existence,

entries.farsi: تا مدد گيرد از او هر صورتى / ‏از كمال و از جمال و قدرتى
entries.english: In order that every form may thereby be replenished with some perfection and beauty and power.

entries.farsi: باز بى‏صورت چو پنهان كرد رو / آمدند از بهر كد در رنگ و بو
entries.english: When, again, the Formless One has hidden His face, they come to beg in colour and perfume.

entries.farsi: صورتى از صورتى ديگر كمال / ‏گر بجويد باشد آن عين ضالل
entries.english: If one form seeks perfection from another form, it is the quintessence of error.

entries.farsi: پس چه عرضه مى‏كنى اى بى‏گهر / احتياج خود به محتاجى دگر
entries.english: Why, then, O worthless man, are you submitting your need to another needy?

entries.farsi: چون صور بنده‏ست بر يزدان مگو / ظن مبر صورت به تشبيهش مجو
entries.english: Inasmuch as forms are slaves, do not say or deem that form is applicable to God: do not seek Him by tashbíh.

entries.farsi: در تضرع جوى و در افناى خويش / ‏كز تفكر جز صورت نايد به پيش
entries.english: Seek in self-abasement and in self-extinction, for nothing but forms is produced by thinking.

entries.farsi: ور غير صورتت نبود فره / ‏صورتى كان بى‏تو زايد در تو به
entries.english: And if you derive no advantage except from form, the form that comes to birth within you involuntarily is the best.

entries.farsi: صورت شهرى كه آن جا مى‏روى / ‏ذوق بى‏صورت كشيدت اى روى
entries.english: The form of a city to which you are going: you are drawn by a formless feeling of pleasure, O dependent one;

entries.farsi: پس به معنى مى‏روى تا المكان / ‏كه خوشى غير مكان است و زمان
entries.english: Therefore you are really going to that which has no locality, for pleasure is different from place and time.

entries.farsi: صورت يارى كه سوى او شوى / ‏از براى مونسى‏اش مى‏روى
entries.english: The form of a friend to whom you would go: you are going for the sake of enjoying his society;

entries.farsi: پس به معنى سوى بى‏صورت شدى / گر چه ز ان مقصود غافل آمدى
entries.english: Therefore in reality you go to the formless, though you are unaware of that object.

entries.farsi: ليك بعضى رو سوى دم كرده‏اند / گر چه سر اصل است سر گم كرده‏اند
entries.english: In truth, then, God is worshipped by all, since wayfaring is for the sake of the pleasure.

entries.farsi: پس حقيقت حق بود معبود كل / ‏كز پى ذوق است سيران سبل
entries.english: But some have set their face towards the tail and have lost the Head, although the Head is the principal;

entries.farsi: ليك آن سر پيش اين ضالان گم / ‏مى‏دهد داد سرى از راه دم
entries.english: But that Head is bestowing on these lost and erring ones the bounty proper to Headship by way of the tail.

entries.farsi: آن ز سر مى‏يابد آن داد اين ز دم / ‏قوم ديگر پا و سر كردند گم
entries.english: That one obtains the bounty from the Head, this one from the tail; another company has lost foot and head.

entries.farsi: چون كه گم شد جمله جمله يافتند / از كم آمد سوى كل بشتافتند
entries.english: Since all has been lost, they have gained all: through dwindling away they have sped towards the Whole.

sections.title_fa: ديدن ايشان در قصر اين قلعه‏ى ذات الصور نقش روى دختر شاه چين را و بى‏هوش شدن هر سه و ‏در فتنه افتادن و تفحص كردن كه اين صورت كيست
sections.title_en: How in the pavilion of the fortress adorned with pictures they saw a portrait of the daughter of the King of China and how all three lost their senses and fell into distraction and made inquiries,
asking, “Whose portrait is this?”

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد آن گروه / ‏صورتى ديدند با حسن و شكوه
entries.english: This topic is endless. The company espied a beauteous and majestic portrait.

entries.farsi: خوب تر ز آن ديده بودند آن فريق / ‏ليك زين رفتند در بحر عميق
entries.english: The party had seen more beautiful than that, but at this one they were plunged in the deep sea,

entries.farsi: ز انكه افيونشان در اين كاسه رسيد / كاسه‏ها محسوس و افيون ناپديد
entries.english: Because opium came to them in this cup: the cups are visible, but the opium is unseen.

entries.farsi: كرد فعل خويش قلعه‏ى هش ربا / هر سه را انداخت در چاه بال
entries.english: The fortress, the destroyer of reason, wrought its work: it cast them, all three, into the pit of tribulation.

entries.farsi: تير غمزه دوخت دل را بى‏كمان / ‏الامان و الامان اى بى‏امان
entries.english: Without a bow the arrow-like glances pierce the heart— mercy, mercy, O merciless one!

entries.farsi: قرنها را صورت سنگين بسوخت / ‏آتشى در دين و دلشان بر فروخت
entries.english: A stone image consumed the generations and kindled a fire in their religion and their hearts.

entries.farsi: چون كه او جانى بود خود چون بود / فتنه‏اش هر لحظه ديگرگون بود
entries.english: When it is spiritual, how must it be! Its fascination changes at every moment.

entries.farsi: اشك مى‏باريد هر يك همچو ميغ / ‏دست مى‏خاييد و مى‏گفت اى دريغ
entries.english: Since love of the pictured form was stabbing the hearts of the princes like a spear-point,

entries.farsi: عشق صورت در دل شه زادگان / ‏چون خلش مى‏كرد مانند سنان
entries.english: Each was shedding tears, like a cloud, and gnawing his hands and crying, “Oh, alas!

entries.farsi: ما كنون ديديم شه ز آغاز ديد / چندمان سوگند داد آن بى‏نديد
entries.english: Now we see the King saw at the beginning. How often did that peerless one adjure us?”

entries.farsi: انبيا را حق بسيار است از آن / ‏كه خبر كردند از پايانمان
entries.english: The prophets have conferred a great obligation because they have made us aware of the end,

entries.farsi: كانچه مى‏كارى نرويد جز كه خار / وين طرف پرى نيابى زو مطار
entries.english: Saying, “That which you are sowing will produce nothing but thorns; and you fly in this direction you will find there no room to fly.

entries.farsi: تخم از من بر كه تا ريعى دهد / با پر من پر كه تير آن سو جهد
entries.english: Get the seed from me, that it may yield a crop; fly with my wings that the arrow may speed Yonder.

entries.farsi: تو ندانى واجبى آن و هست / ‏هم تو گويى آخر آن واجب بده‏ست
entries.english: If you do not recognise the necessity and existence of that, yet in the end you will confess that it was necessary.”

entries.farsi: او تو است اما نه اين تو آن تو است / ‏كه در آخر واقف بيرون شو است
entries.english: He is you, but not this “you”: that “you” which in the end is conscious of escape.

entries.farsi: توى آخر سوى توى اولت / ‏آمده‏ست از بهر تنبيه و صلت
entries.english: Your last “you” has come to your first “you” to receive admonition and gifts.

entries.farsi: توى تو در ديگرى آمد دفين / ‏من غالم مرد خود بينى چنين
entries.english: Your “you” is buried in another: I am the slave of a man who thus sees himself.

entries.farsi: آن چه در آيينه مى‏بيند جوان / ‏پير اندر خشت بيند پيش از آن
entries.english: That which the youth sees in the mirror the Elder sees beforehand in the brick.

entries.farsi: ز امر شاه خويش بيرون آمديم / ‏با عنايات پدر ياغى شديم
entries.english: “We have transgressed the command of our King, we have rebelled against the favours of our father.

entries.farsi: سهل دانستيم قول شاه را / و آن عنايتهاى بى‏اشباه را
entries.english: We have lightly esteemed the King’s word and those incomparable favours.

entries.farsi: نك در افتاديم در خندق همه / ‏كشته و خسته‏ى بال بى‏ملحمه
entries.english: Lo, we all are fallen into the moat, killed and wounded by affliction without combat.

entries.farsi: تكيه بر عقل خود و فرهنگ خويش / ‏بودمان تا اين بال آمد به پيش
entries.english: We relied on our own intelligence and wisdom, so that this tribulation has come to pass.

entries.farsi: بى‏مرض ديديم خويش و بى‏ز رق / ‏آن چنان كه خويش را بيمار دق
entries.english: We regarded ourselves as being without disease and emancipated, just as one suffering from lung disease regards himself.

entries.farsi: سايه‏ى رهبر به است از ذكر حق / ‏يك قناعت به كه صد لوت و طبق
entries.english: Now, after we have been made prisoners and a prey, the hidden malady has become apparent.”

entries.farsi: علت پنهان كنون شد آشكار / بعد از آن كه بند گشتيم و شكار
entries.english: The shadow of the Guide is better than praising God: a single contentment is better than a hundred delicacies and trays.

entries.farsi: چشم بينا بهتر از سيصد عصا / چشم بشناسد گهر را از حصا
entries.english: A seeing eye is better than three hundred staves: the eye knows pearls from pebbles.

entries.farsi: در تفحص آمدند از اندهان / ‏صورت كه بود عجب اين در جهان
entries.english: By sorrows they began to make inquiry, saying, “Who in the world, we wonder, is she of whom this is the portrait?”

entries.farsi: بعد بسيارى تفحص در مسير / كشف كرد آن راز را شيخى بصير
entries.english: After much inquiry in travel, a Shaykh endowed with insight disclosed the mystery,

entries.farsi: نه از طريق گوش بل از وحى هوش / ‏رازها بد پيش او بى‏روى پوش
entries.english: Not by way of the ear, but by inspiration from Reason: to him mysteries were unveiled.

entries.farsi: گفت نقش رشك پروين است اين / ‏صورت شه زاده‏ى چين است اين
entries.english: He said, “This is the portrait of an object of envy to the Pleiades: this is the picture of the Princess of China.

entries.farsi: همچو جان و چون جنين پنهانست او / در مكتم پرده و ايوانست او
entries.english: She is hidden like the spirit and like the embryo: she is in a secret bower and palace.

entries.farsi: سوى او نه مرد ره دارد نه زن / ‏شاه پنهان كرد او را از فتن
entries.english: Neither man nor woman is admitted to her: the King has concealed her on account of her fascinations.

entries.farsi: اين سزاى آن كه تخم جهل كاشت / ‏و آن نصيحت را كساد و سهل داشت
entries.english: The King has a jealousy for her name, so that not even a bird flies above her roof.”

entries.farsi: غيرتى دارد ملك بر نام او / كه نپرد مرغ هم بر بام او
entries.english: Alas for the heart that such an insane passion has stricken: may no one feel a passion like this!

entries.farsi: واى آن دل كش چنين سودا فتاد / هيچ كس را اين چنين سودا مباد
entries.english: This is the retribution due to him, who sowed the seed of ignorance and held light and cheap that counsel,

entries.farsi: اعتمادى كرد بر تدبير خويش / ‏كه برم من كار خود با عقل پيش
entries.english: And put trust in his own management, saying, “By dint of intelligence I will carry my affair to success.”

entries.farsi: نيم ذره ز آن عنايت به بود / كه ز تدبير خرد سيصد رصد
entries.english: Half a mite of the favour is better than three hundred spells devised by the intellect.

entries.farsi: ترك مكر خويشتن گير اى امير / پا بكش پيش عنايت خوش بمير
entries.english: Abandon your own cunning, O Amír: draw back your foot before the favour and gladly die.

entries.farsi: اين بقدر حيله‏ى معدود نيست / ‏زين حيل تا تو نميرى سود نيست
entries.english: This is not by a certain amount of contrivance: nothing avails until you die to these plans.

sections.title_fa: حكايت صدر جهان بخارا كه هر سائلى كه به زبان بخواستى از صدقه‏ى عام بى‏دريغ او محروم شدى و آن دانشمند درويش به فراموشى و فرس حرص و تعجيل به زبان بخواست در موكب، صدر جهان از وى رو بگردانيد و او هر روز حيله‏ى نو ساختى و خود را گاه زن كردى زير چادر و گاه نابينا ‏كردى و چشم و روى خود بسته به فراستش بشناختى الى آخره

sections.title_en: Story of the Sadr-i Jahan of Bukhara. Any beggar who begged with his tongue was excluded from his universal and unstinted charity. A certain poor savant, forgetting and being excessively eager and in a hurry, begged with his tongue amidst his cavalcade. The Sadr-i Jahan averted his face from him, and he contrived a new trick every day and disguised himself, now as a woman
veiled in a chador and now as a blind man with bandaged eyes and face, he always had discernment enough to recognize him, etc.

entries.farsi: در بخارا خوى آن خواجه‏ى اجل / ‏بود با خواهندگان حسن عمل
entries.english: It was the habit of that most noble lord in Bukhara to deal kindly with beggars.

entries.farsi: داد بسيار و عطاى بى‏شمار / تا به شب بودى ز جودش زر نثار
entries.english: His great bounty and immeasurable munificence were always scattering gold till nightfall.

entries.farsi: زر به كاغذ پاره‏اى پيچيده بود / تا وجودش بود مى‏افشاند جود
entries.english: The gold was wrapped in bits of paper: he continued to lavish bounty as long as he lived.

entries.farsi: همچو خورشيد و چو ماه پاك باز / آن چه گيرند از ضيا بدهند باز
entries.english: Like the sun and the spendthrift moon; they give back the radiance that they receive.

entries.farsi: خاك را زر بخش كى بود آفتاب / ‏زر از او در كان و گنج اندر خراب
entries.english: Who bestows gold on the earth? The sun. Through him, gold is in the mine and treasure in the ruin.

entries.farsi: مبتاليان را بدى روزى عطا / روز ديگر بيوگان را آن سخا
entries.english: Every morning an allowance to a set of people, in order that no class should be left disappointed by him.

entries.farsi: هر صباحى يك گره را راتبه / ‏تا نماند امتى زو خايبه
entries.english: On one day his gifts were made to those afflicted; next day the same generosity to widows;

entries.farsi: روز ديگر بر علويان مقل / ‏با فقيهان فقير مشتغل
entries.english: Next day to impoverished descendants of Alí together with poor jurists engaged in study;

entries.farsi: روز ديگر بر تهى دستان عام / ‏روز ديگر بر گرفتاران وام
entries.english: Next day to empty-handed common folk; next day to persons fallen into debt.

entries.farsi: شرط او آن بود كه كس با زبان / ‏زر نخواهد هيچ نگشايد لبان
entries.english: His rule was that no one should beg for gold with his tongue or open his lips at all;

entries.farsi: ليك خامش بر حوالى رهش / ‏ايستاده مفلسان ديواروش
entries.english: But the paupers stood in silence, like a wall, on the outskirts of his path,

entries.farsi: هر كه كردى ناگهان با لب سؤال / ‏زو نبردى زين گنه يك حبه مال
entries.english: And anyone who suddenly begged with his lips was punished for this offence by not getting from him a mite of money.

entries.farsi: من صمت منكم نجا بد ياسه‏اش / ‏خامشان را بود كيسه و كاسه‏اش
entries.english: His maxim was “Those of you who keep silence are saved”: his purses and bowls were for the silent.

entries.farsi: نادرا روزى يكى پيرى بگفت / ‏ده زكاتم كه منم با جوع جفت
entries.english: One day extraordinarily an old man said, “Give me alms, for I am hungry.”

entries.farsi: منع كرد از پير و پيرش جد گرفت / ‏مانده خلق از جد پير اندر شگفت
entries.english: He refused to the old man, but the old man importuned him: the people were astounded by the old man’s importunity.

entries.farsi: گفت بس بى‏شرم پيرى اى پدر / پير گفت از من تويى بى‏شرم‏تر
entries.english: He said, “You are a very shameless old man, O father.” The old man replied, “You are more shameless than I,

entries.farsi: كاين جهان خوردى و خواهى تو ز طمع / ‏كان جهان با اين جهان گيرى به جمع
entries.english: For you have enjoyed this world, and in your greed you would happily take the other world together with this world.”

entries.farsi: خنده‏ش آمد مال داد آن پير را / پير تنها برد آن توفير را
entries.english: He laughed and gave the old man some money: the old man alone obtained the bounty.

entries.farsi: غير آن پير ايچ خواهنده از او / نيم حبه زر نديد و نه تسو
entries.english: Except that old man none of those who begged saw half a mite or a single farthing of his money.

entries.farsi: نوبت روز فقيهان ناگهان / ‏يك فقيه از حرص آمد در فغان
entries.english: On the day when it was the turn of the jurists, a certain jurist, by cupidity, suddenly began to whine.

entries.farsi: كرد زاريها بسى چاره نبود / گفت هر نوعى نبودش هيچ سود
entries.english: He made many piteous appeals, but there was no help; he uttered every kind, but it availed him naught.

entries.farsi: روز ديگر با رگو پيچيد پا / ناكس اندر صف قوم مبتال
entries.english: Next day he wrapped his leg in rags in the row of the sufferers, hanging his head.

entries.farsi: تخته‏ها بر ساق بست از چپ و راست / ‏تا گمان آيد كه او اشكسته پاست
entries.english: He tied splints on his shank, left and right, in order that it might be supposed that his leg was broken.

entries.farsi: ديدش و بشناختش چيزى نداد / روز ديگر رو بپوشيد از لباد
entries.english: He saw and recognised him and did not give him anything. Next day he covered his face with a rain-cloak,

entries.farsi: هم بدانستش ندادش آن عزيز / از گناه و جرم گفتن هيچ چيز
entries.english: The noble lord knew him still and gave him nothing because of the sin and crime by speaking.

entries.farsi: چون كه عاجز شد ز صد گونه مكيد / چون زنان او چادرى بر سر كشيد
entries.english: When he had failed in a hundred sorts of trickery, he drew a chador over his head, like women,

entries.farsi: در ميان بيوگان رفت و نشست / ‏سر فرو افكند و پنهان كرد دست
entries.english: And went and sat down amongst the widows, and let his head droop and concealed his hands.

entries.farsi: هم شناسيدش ندادش صدقه‏اى / ‏در دلش آمد ز حرمان حرقه‏اى
entries.english: Still he recognised him and did not give him any alms: on account of the disappointment a burning grief came into his heart.

entries.farsi: رفت او پيش كفن خواهى پگاه / ‏كه بپيچم در نمد نه پيش راه
entries.english: He went early in the morning to a purveyor of grave-clothes, saying, “Wrap me in a felt and lay me out on the road.

entries.farsi: هيچ مگشا لب نشين و مى‏نگر / تا كند صدر جهان اينجا گذر
entries.english: Do not open your lips at all, sit down and look on till the Sadr-i Jahan passes here.

entries.farsi: بو كه بيند مرده پندارد به ظن / ‏زر در اندازد پى وجه كفن
entries.english: Maybe he will see and suppose that I am dead and drop some money to cover the cost of the shroud.

entries.farsi: هر چه بدهد نيم آن بدهم به تو / همچنان كرد آن فقير صله جو
entries.english: I will pay you half of whatever he may give.” The poor man, desiring the present, did just as he was told.

entries.farsi: در نمد پيچيد و بر راهش نهاد / معبر صدر جهان آن جا فتاد
entries.english: He wrapped him in the felt and laid him out on the road. The Sadr-i Jahan happened to pass that way

entries.farsi: زر در اندازيد بر روى نمد / دست بيرون كرد از تعجيل خود
entries.english: And dropped some gold on the felt. He put forth his hand in his haste.

entries.farsi: تا نگيرد آن كفن خواه آن صله / ‏تا نهان نكند از او آن ده دله
entries.english: Lest the purveyor of the grave-clothes should seize the gift of money and lest that perfidious rascal should hide it from him.

entries.farsi: مرده از زير نمد بر كرد دست / ‏سر برون آمد پى دستش ز پست
entries.english: The dead man raised his hand from beneath the felt, a nd, following his hand, his head came forth from below.

entries.farsi: گفت با صدر جهان چون بستدم / ‏اى ببسته بر من ابواب كرم
entries.english: He said to the Sadr-i Jahan, “how I have received, O you who did shut the doors of generosity against me!”

entries.farsi: گفت ليكن تا نمردى اى عنود / اى جناب من نبردى هيچ جود
entries.english: He replied, “, but until you died, O obstinate man, you got no bounty from me.”

entries.farsi: سر موتوا قبل موت اين بود / كز پس مردن غنيمتها رسد
entries.english: The mystery of “Die before death” is this that the prizes come after dying.

entries.farsi: غير مردن هيچ فرهنگى دگر / در نگيرد با خداى اى حيله گر
entries.english: Except dying, no other skill avails with God, O artful schemer.

entries.farsi: يك عنايت به ز صد گون اجتهاد / جهد را خوف است از صد گون فساد
entries.english: One favour is better than a hundred kinds of effort: exertion is in danger from a hundred kinds of mischief.

entries.farsi: بلكه مرگش بى‏عنايت نيز نيست / ‏بى‏عنايت هان و هان جايى مه‏ايست
entries.english: And the favour depends on dying: the trustworthy have put this way to the test.

entries.farsi: و آن عنايت هست موقوف ممات / ‏تجربه كردند اين ره را ثقات
entries.english: Nay, not even his death is without the favour: listen, listen, and do not tarry anywhere without the favour!

entries.farsi: آن زمرد باشد اين افعى پير / بى‏زمرد كى شود افعى ضرير
entries.english: That is an emerald, and this is an old viper: without the emerald how should the viper be blinded?

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن دو برادر يكى كوسه و يك امرد، در عزب خانه‏اى خفتند، شبى اتفاقا امرد خشتها بر پشت خود انبار كرد، عاقبت دباب دب آورد و آن خشتها را به حيله و نرمى از پس او بر داشت، كودك بيدار ‏شد به جنگ كه اين خشتها كو كجا بردى و چرا بردى، او گفت تو اين خشتها را چرا نهادى الى آخره

sections.title_en: Story of two brothers, one of whom had a few hairs on his chin while the other was a beardless boy. They went to sleep in a house for celibates. One night, as it happened, the boy piled bricks over his buttocks. At length, a crawler (sodomizer) crept and craftily and softly took off the bricks from his back. The boy awoke and began to quarrel, saying, “Where are these bricks?
Where have you taken them to? Why did you take them?” He replied, “Why did you put these bricks there?” etc.

entries.farsi: امردى و كوسه‏اى در انجمن / ‏آمدند و مجمعى بد در وطن
entries.english: A beardless boy and a youth with a few hairs on his chin came to a festive gathering, for there was an assembly-place in the town.

entries.farsi: مشتغل ماندند قوم محتجب / ‏روز رفت و شد زمانه ثلث شب
entries.english: The select party remained busy till the day was gone and a third of the night had passed.

entries.farsi: ز آن عزب‏خانه نرفتند آن دو كس / ‏هم بخفتند آن سو از بيم عسس
entries.english: The two did not leave that house for celibates: they lay down to sleep there for fear of the night-patrol.

entries.farsi: كودك امرد به صورت بود زشت / ‏هم نهاد اندر پس كون بيست خشت
entries.english: The youth had four hairs on his chin, but his face was like the full-moon.

entries.farsi: كوسه را بد بر زنخدان چار مو / ليك همچون ماه بدرش بود رو
entries.english: The beardless boy was ugly in appearance: placed twenty bricks on his backside.

entries.farsi: لوطيى دب برد شب در انبهى / ‏خشتها را نقل كرد آن مشتهى
entries.english: A homosexual waited for an opportunity and slowly removed the bricks.

entries.farsi: دست چون بر وى زد او از جا بجست / ‏گفت هى تو كيستى اى سگ پرست
entries.english: When he began to approach him, the startled boy awoke from his sleep.

entries.farsi: گفت اين سى خشت چون انباشتى / ‏گفت تو سى خشت چون بر داشتى
entries.english: He asked why the boy had placed the bricks on his back.

entries.farsi: كودك بيمارم و از ضعف خود / كردم اينجا احتياط و مرتقد
entries.english: I am a sick boy and because of my weakness I took precautions and made here a place to lie down.”

entries.farsi: گفت اگر دارى ز رنجورى تفى / چون نرفتى جانب دار الشفا
entries.english: He replied, “If you are ill with a fever, why didn’t you go to the hospital

entries.farsi: يا به خانه‏ى يك طبيبى مشفقى / ‏كه گشادى از سقامت مغلقى
entries.english: Or to the house of a kindly physician, in order that he might relieve you of your malady?”

entries.farsi: گفت آخر من كجا دانم شدن / ‏كه به هر جا مى‏روم من ممتحن
entries.english: “Why,” said he, “where can I go? For wherever I go, persecuted,

entries.farsi: چون تو زنديقى پليدى ملحدى / ‏مى‏برآرد سر به پيشم چون ددى
entries.english: Some foul ungodly miscreant like you springs up before me like a wild beast.

entries.farsi: خانقاهى كه بود بهتر مكان / ‏من نديدم يك دمى در وى امان
entries.english: The dervish-convent, which is the best place— not there do I find safety for one moment.

entries.farsi: رو به من آرند مشتى حمزه خوار / چشمها پر نطفه كف خايه فشار
entries.english: A handful of pottage-eaters direct their looks at me: their eyes bursting with lust

entries.farsi: و آن كه ناموسى است خود از زير زير / غمزه دزدد مى‏دهد مالش به كير
entries.english: And even he that has regard for decorum steals covert glances at my penis strokes.

entries.farsi: خانقه چون اين بود بازار عام / ‏چون بود خر گله و ديوان خام
entries.english: Since the convent is this, what must the public market be like? A herd of asses and boorish devils!

entries.farsi: خر كجا ناموس و تقوى از كجا / خر چه داند خشيت و خوف و رجا
entries.english: What has an ass to do with decorum and piety? How should an ass know reverence and fear and hope?

entries.farsi: عقل باشد ايمنى و عدل جو / بر زن و بر مرد اما عقل كو
entries.english: Intelligence consists in being safe and in the desire to act justly towards woman and towards man; but where is intelligence?

entries.farsi: ور گريزم من روم سوى زنان / ‏همچو يوسف افتم اندر افتتان
entries.english: And if I run away and go to the women, I should fall into tribulation like Joseph.

entries.farsi: يوسف از زن يافت زندان و فشار / من شوم توزيع بر پنجاه دار
entries.english: Joseph suffered imprisonment and torment at the hands of a woman: I should be divided amongst fifty gibbets.

entries.farsi: آن زنان از جاهلى بر من تنند / اولياشان قصد جان من كنند
entries.english: Those women in their foolishness would attach themselves to me, and their nearest and dearest would seek my life.

entries.farsi: بعد از آن كودك به كوسه بنگريست / ‏گفت او با آن دو مو از غم برى است
entries.english: I have no means of escape either from men or women: what can I do, since I belong neither to these nor to those?”

entries.farsi: نه ز مردان چاره دارم نه از زنان / ‏چون كنم كه نى از اينم نه از آن
entries.english: After that the boy looked at the youth and said, “He is quit of trouble by reason of the two hairs.

entries.farsi: فارغ است از خشيت و از پيكار خشت / ‏و ز چو تو مادر فروش كنگ زشت
entries.english: He is independent of the bricks and of quarrelling over the bricks and of a wicked young ruffian like you who would sell his own mother.

entries.farsi: بر زنخ سه چار مو بهر نمون / ‏بهتر از سى خشت گرداگرد كون
entries.english: Three or four hairs on the chin as a notice are better than thirty bricks around my ass.”

entries.farsi: ذره‏اى سايه‏ى عنايت بهتر است / ‏از هزاران كوشش طاعت پرست
entries.english: One atom of the shade of favour is better than a thousand endeavours of the devout pietist,

entries.farsi: ز انكه شيطان خشت طاعت بر كند / گرد و صد خشت است خود را ره كند
entries.english: Because the Devil will remove the bricks of piety: if there are two hundred bricks he will make a way for himself.

entries.farsi: خشت اگر پر است بنهاده‏ى تو است / ‏آن دو سه مو از عطاى آن سو است
entries.english: If the bricks are numerous, they are laid by you; those two or three hairs are a gift from Yonder.

entries.farsi: در حقيقت هر يكى مو ز آن كهى است / ‏كان امان نامه‏ى صله‏ى شاهنشهى است
entries.english: In reality each one of those is as a mountain, for it is a safe conduct bestowed by an Emperor.

entries.farsi: تو اگر صد قفل بنهى بر درى / ‏بر كند آن جمله را خيره سرى
entries.english: If you put a hundred locks on a door, some reckless fellow may remove them all;

entries.farsi: شحنه‏اى از موم اگر مهرى نهد / پهلوانان را از آن دل بشكهد
entries.english: If a police magistrate put a wax seal, at that the hearts of doughty champions will quail.

entries.farsi: آن دو سه تار عنايت همچو كوه / ‏سد شده چون فر سيما در وجوه
entries.english: Those two or three hair-threads of favour form a barrier as a mountain, like majesty of aspect in the faces.

entries.farsi: خشت را مگذار اى نيكو سرشت / ‏ليك هم ايمن مخسب از ديو زشت
entries.english: Do not neglect the bricks, O man of goodly nature; but at the same time do not sleep safe from the wicked Devil.

entries.farsi: رو دو تا مو ز آن كرم با دست آر / و آن گهان ايمن بخسب و غم مدار
entries.english: Go and get two hairs of that grace, and then sleep safe and have no anxiety.

entries.farsi: نوم عالم از عبادت به بود / آن چنان علمى كه مستنبه بود
entries.english: The sleep of the wise (‘alim)is better than worship, such a wisdom (‘ilm) as brings awakening.

entries.farsi: آن سكون سابح اندر آشنا / به ز جهد اعجمى با دست و پا
entries.english: The quiet of the swimmer in swimming is better than the exertion with hands and feet of one who is unable.

entries.farsi: اعجمى زد دست و پا و غرق شد / مى‏رود سباح ساكن چون عمد
entries.english: He that cannot swim throws out his hands and feet and drowns, the swimmer moves quietly like pillars.

entries.farsi: علم دريايى است بى‏حد و كنار / طالب علم است غواص بحار
entries.english: Knowledge is an ocean without bound or shore: the seeker of knowledge is the diver in seas.

entries.farsi: گر هزاران سال باشد عمر او / او نگردد سير خود از جستجو
entries.english: Though his life is a thousand years, never will he become weary of seeking,

entries.farsi: كان رسول حق بگفت اندر بيان / ‏اينكه منهومان هما ال يشبعان
entries.english: For the Messenger of God said in explanation this— “There are two greedy ones who are never satisfied.”

sections.title_fa: در تفسير اين خبر كه مصطفى صلوات اللهَّ عليه فرمود منهومان ال يشبعان طالب الدنيا و طالب العلم كه اين علم غير علم دنيا بايد تا دو قسم باشد اما علم دنيا هم دنيا باشد الى آخره و اگر همچنين شود ‏كه طالب الدنيا و طالب الدنيا تكرار بود نه تقسيم، مع تقريره

sections.title_en: Commentary on the Tradition that Mustafá—the blessings of God be upon him!—said, “There are two greedy ones who will never be satisfied: the seeker of the present world and the seeker of knowledge.” This “knowledge” must be different from “knowledge of the present world,” in order that there may be the two classes; but “knowledge of the present world” is just the same as
“the present world,” etc.; and if it be equivalent to “the seeker of the present world and the seeker of the present world,” that would be repetition, not division. With the exposition thereof.

entries.farsi: طالب الدنيا و توفيراتها / طالب العلم و تدبيراتها
entries.english: The seeker of the present world and its abundant opportunities for acquisition, and the seeker of knowledge and the considerations proper to it. Now, when you fix your attention on this division, this knowledge must be other than the present world, O father.

entries.farsi: پس در اين قسمت چو بگمارى نظر / غير دنيا باشد اين علم اى پدر
entries.english: What, then, is other than the present world? The next world, which will take you away from here and be your guide.

sections.title_fa: ‏بحث كردن آن سه شه زاده در تدبير آن واقعه
sections.title_en: How the three princes discussed the plan to adopt in view of what had occurred.

entries.farsi: غير دنيا پس چه باشد آخرت / ‏كت كند ز ينجا و باشد رهبرت
entries.english: The three afflicted ones put their heads together: all three felt the same grief and pain and sorrow.

entries.farsi: رو به هم كردند هر سه مفتتن / ‏هر سه را يك رنج و يك درد و حزن
entries.english: All three were comrades in one meditation and one passion; all three were sick with one disease and one malady.

entries.farsi: هر سه در يك فكر و يك سودا نديم / ‏هر سه از يك رنج و يك علت سقيم
entries.english: At the time of silence all three had one thought; at the time of speech, too, all three had one argument.

entries.farsi: در خموشى هر سه را خطرت يكى / ‏در سخن هم هر سه را حجت يكى
entries.english: At one moment they all were shedding tears and weeping blood on the dining-table of calamity;

entries.farsi: يك زمانى اشك ريزان جمله‏شان / ‏بر سر خوان مصيبت خون فشان
entries.english: At another moment all three, from the fire in their hearts, heaved burning sighs as a chafing-pan.

sections.title_fa: ‏مقالت برادر بزرگين
sections.title_en: The discourse of the eldest brother.

entries.farsi: يك زمان از آتش دل هر سه كس / ‏بر زده با سوز چون مجمر نفس
entries.english: The eldest said, “O men of integrity were not we masculine in giving counsel to others?

entries.farsi: آن بزرگين گفت اى اخوان خير / ما نه نر بوديم اندر نصح غير
entries.english: Whenever one of the retainers complained to us of affliction and poverty and fear and agitation,

entries.farsi: ‏صبر كن كالصبر مفتاح الفرج / اين كليد صبر را اكنون چه شد
entries.english: We used to say, ‘do not bewail your hardships: be patient, for patience is the key to relief from pain.’

entries.farsi: اى عجب منسوخ شد قانون چه شد / ما نمى‏گفتيم اندر كش مكش
entries.english: What has become now of this key, fortitude? Wonderful! The rule is null and void: what has become of it?

entries.farsi: ‏اندر آتش همچو زر خنديد خوش / مر سپه را وقت تنگاتنگ جنگ
entries.english: Did not we always say, ‘In the struggle laugh happily like gold in the fire’?

entries.farsi: ‏گفته ما كه هين مگردانيد رنگ / آن زمان كه بود اسبان را وطا
entries.english: We said to the soldiers at the time of conflict in battle, ‘Listen, do not change colour!’

entries.farsi: جمله سرهاى بريده زير پا / ما سپاه خويش را هى‏هى‏كنان
entries.english: At the time when the ground trodden underfoot by the horses was entirely severed heads,

entries.farsi: ‏كه به پيش آييد قاهر چون سنان / جمله عالم را نشان داده به صبر
entries.english: We were shouting to our troops, ‘On, on! Advance irresistibly like the spear point!’

entries.farsi: ز انكه صبر آمد چراغ و نور صدر / نوبت ما شد چه خيره‏سر شديم
entries.english: We preached fortitude to the entire world, because fortitude is a lamp and light in the breast.

entries.farsi: ‏چون زنان زشت در چادر شديم / اى دلى كه جمله را كردى تو گرم
entries.english: Now it is our turn. Why have we become distracted and gone under the chador like cowardly women?”

entries.farsi: ‏گرم كن خود را و از خود دار شرم / اى زبان كه جمله را ناصح بدى
entries.english: O heart that did inspire all with ardour, inspire yourself with ardour and be ashamed of yourself!

entries.farsi: ‏نوبت تو گشت از چه تن زدى / اى خرد كو پند شكر خاى تو
entries.english: O tongue that was a mentor to all, now it is your turn: why are you silent?

entries.farsi: دور تست اين دم چه شد هيهاى تو / اى ز دلها برده صد تشويش را
entries.english: O reason, where is your eloquent and persuasive counsel? Now it is your turn: what has become of your admonitions?

entries.farsi: نوبت تو شد بجنبان ريش را / از غرى ريش ار كنون دزديده‏اى
entries.english: O you who has removed a hundred anxieties from hearts, now it is your turn: wag your beard!

entries.farsi: ‏پيش از اين بر ريش خود خنديده‏اى / وقت پند ديگرانى هاى هاى
entries.english: If now, in your vile cowardice, you have stolen a beard, formerly you must have been laughing at your beard.

entries.farsi: ‏در غم خود چون زنانى واى واى / چون به درد ديگران درمان بدى
entries.english: When you exhort others, “Come on! Come on!” In your own anguish, “Alas, alas!” like women.

entries.farsi: بانگ بر زن چه گرفت آواز تو / آن چه پنجه سال بافيدى به هوش
entries.english: Since you were a cure for others’ pain, you are silent when pain has become your guest?

entries.farsi: ‏درد مهمان تو آمد تن زدى / بانگ بر لشكر زدن بد ساز تو
entries.english: It was your fashion to shout at the soldiers: shout: why is your voice choked?

entries.farsi: ‏ز آن نسيج خود بغلتاقى بپوش / از نوايت گوش ياران بود خوش
entries.english: For fifty years you have woven on your intelligence: put on an under shirt of the fabric which you yourself have woven.

entries.farsi: ‏دست بيرون آر و گوش خود بكش / سر بدى پيوسته خود را دم مكن
entries.english: The ears of your friends were delighted by your song: put forth your hand and pull your own ear.

entries.farsi: خويش را در طبع آر و در نشاط
entries.english: You were always a head: do not make yourself a tail, do not lose your feet and hands and beard and moustache.

entries.farsi: ‏پا و دست و ريش و سبلت گم مكن / بازى آن تست بر روى بساط
entries.english: It is for you to make a move on the board: restore yourself to your normal state and your vigour.

sections.title_fa: ذكر آن پادشاه كه ان دانشمند را به اكراه در مجلس آورد و بنشاند و ساقى شراب بر دانشمند عرضه كرد ساغر پيش او داشت رو بگردانيد و ترشى و تندى آغاز كرد، شاه ساقى را گفت كه هين در ‏طبعش آر، ساقى چندى بر سرش كوفت و شرابش در خورد داد الى آخره
sections.title_en: Anecdote of a king who brought a learned doctor into his banquet-hall by force and made him sit down. The cup-bearer offered him wine and held out the goblet to him, the doctor averted his face and began to look sour and behave rudely. The king said to the cup-bearer, “Come, and put him in a good humour.” The cup-bearer beat him on the head several times and made him drink the wine, etc.

entries.farsi: پادشاهى مست اندر بزم خوش / ‏مى‏گذشت آن يك فقيهى بر درش
entries.english: A drunk king was feasting merrily, a certain jurist passed by his gate.

entries.farsi: كرد اشارت كش درين مجلس كشيد / وز شراب لعل در خوردش دهيد
entries.english: He gave directions, saying, “Bring him into this hall and give him a drink of the ruby wine.”

entries.farsi: پس كشيدندش به شه بى‏اختيار / شست در مجلس ترش چون زهر مار
entries.english: So they brought him to the king, he had no choice: he sat down in the hall, sour as poison and snakes.

entries.farsi: عرضه كردش مى‏نپذرفت او به خشم / ‏از شه و ساقى بگردانيد چشم
entries.english: He offered him wine; he angrily refused it and averted his eyes from the king and the cup-bearer,

entries.farsi: كه به عمر خود نخورده‏ستم شراب / ‏خوشتر آيد از شرابم زهر ناب
entries.english: Saying, “I have never drunk wine in my life: rank poison would please me better than wine.

entries.farsi: هين به جاى مى به من زهرى دهيد / تا من از خويش و شما زين وا رهيد
entries.english: Hey, give me some poison instead of the wine that I may be delivered from myself and you from this.”

entries.farsi: مى نخورده عربده آغاز كرد / گشته در مجلس گران چون مرگ و درد
entries.english: Without having drunk wine, he began to make a row and became as disagreeable to the company as death and pangs.

entries.farsi: همچو اهل نفس و اهل آب و گل / ‏در جهان بنشسته با اصحاب دل
entries.english: Like carnal earthly-minded people in the world when they sit with spiritual folk.

entries.farsi: حق ندارد خاصگان را در كمون / ‏از مى احرار جز در يشربون
entries.english: God keeps His elect drinking secretly the wine of the free.

entries.farsi: عرضه مى‏دارند بر محجوب جام / ‏حس نمى‏يابد از آن غير كالم
entries.english: They offer the cup to one who is veiled; perception apprehends nothing of it except the words.

entries.farsi: رو همى‏گرداند از ارشادشان / ‏كه نمى‏بيند به ديده دادشان
entries.english: He averts his face from their guidance because he does not see their gift with his eye.

entries.farsi: گر ز گوشش تا به حلقش ره بدى / ‏سر نصح اندر درونشان در شدى
entries.english: If there were a passage from his ear to his throat, the hidden meaning of their admonition would have entered his inward parts.

entries.farsi: چون همه نار است جانش نيست نور / كه افكند در نار سوزان جز قشور
entries.english: Inasmuch as his spirit is wholly fire, not light, who would throw anything but husks into a blazing fire?

entries.farsi: مغز بيرون ماند و قشر گفت رفت / ‏كى شود از قشر معده گرم و زفت
entries.english: The kernel remains outside and the husk, words, goes: how should the stomach be made warm and stout by husks?

entries.farsi: نار دوزخ جز كه قشر افشار نيست / ‏نار را با هيچ مغزى كار نيست
entries.english: The Fire of Hell torments only the husks: the Fire has nothing to do with any kernel;

entries.farsi: ور بود بر مغز نارى شعله زن / ‏بهر پختن دان نه بهر سوختن
entries.english: And if a fire should dart its flames at the kernel, know that it is in order to cook it, not to burn it.

entries.farsi: تا كه باشد حق حكيم اين قاعده / ‏مستمر دان در گذشته و نامده
entries.english: So long as God is the Wise, know that this law is perpetual in the past and in the time that has not come.

entries.farsi: غز نغز و قشرها مغفور از او / مغز را پس چون بسوزد دور از او
entries.english: The pure kernels and the husks are pardoned by Him: how, then, should He burn the kernel? Far from Him!

entries.farsi: از عنايت گر بكوبد بر سرش / ‏اشتها آيد شراب احمرش
entries.english: If in His grace He beat the head of him, he will feel an eager desire for the red wine;

entries.farsi: ور نكوبد ماند او بسته دهان / ‏چون فقيه از شرب و بزم اين شهان
entries.english: And if He does not beat him, he will remain, like the jurist, with his mouth closed against the potations and festivity of these kings. The king said to his cup-bearer, “O well-conducted, why are you silent? Give and put him in good humour.”

entries.farsi: گفت شه با ساقى‏اش اى نيك پى / ‏چه خموشى ده به طبعش آر هى
entries.english: Over every mind there is a hidden Ruler, cunningly diverts from his purpose whomever He will.

entries.farsi: هست پنهان حاكمى بر هر خرد / هر كه را خواهد به فن از سر برد
entries.english: The sun in the East and his radiance are bound like captives in His chain.

entries.farsi: آفتاب مشرق و تنوير او / چون اسيران بسته در زنجير او
entries.english: He causes the sphere to revolve immediately when He chants half of a cunning spell in its brain.

entries.farsi: عقل كاو عقل دگر را سخره كرد / مهره زو دارد وى است استاد نرد
entries.english: The mind which dominates another mind has the dice from Him: He is the Master-player.

entries.farsi: چرخ را چرخ اندر آرد در زمن / ‏چون بخواند در دماغش نيم فن
entries.english: He gave him several cuffs on the head, saying, “Take the cup!” The tormented man drained it in dread of blows.

entries.farsi: چند سيلى بر سرش زد گفت گير / در كشيد از بيم سيلى آن زحير
entries.english: He became tipsy and merry and smiling as a garden: he began to act like a boon-companion and tell ridiculous stories and make jokes.

entries.farsi: مست گشت و شاد و خندان شد چو باغ / ‏در نديمى و مضاحك رفت و الغ
entries.english: He became brave and jolly and snapped his fingers: he went to the bathroom.

entries.farsi: شير گير و خوش شد انگشتك بزد / سوى مبرز رفت تا ميزك كند
entries.english: There he encountered a very beautiful slave girl who belonged to the royal household.

entries.farsi: يك كنيزك بود در مبرز چو ماه / ‏سخت زيبا و ز قرناقان شاه
entries.english: When he espied her, his mouth gaped, his reason fled and his body was ready for violence.

entries.farsi: عمرها بوده عزب مشتاق و مست / ‏بر كنيزك در زمان در زد دو دست
entries.english: He immediately jumped on her.

entries.farsi: بس طپيد آن دختر و نعره فراشت / ‏بر نيامد با وى و سودى نداشت
entries.english: The girl screamed and resisted but to no avail.

entries.farsi: بسرشد گاهيش نرم و گه درشت / ‏زو بر آرد چاق چاقى زير مشت
entries.english: A woman in the hands of a man at such a time is like dough in the hands of a baker.

entries.farsi: زن به دست مرد در وقت لقا / چون خمير آمد به دست نانبا
entries.english: He kneads it now gently, now roughly, and makes it groan under his fist;

entries.farsi: گاه پهنش واكشد بر تخته‏اى / ‏در همش آرد گهى يك لخته‏اى
entries.english: Now he draws it out flat on a board, now for a bit he rolls it up;

entries.farsi: گاه در وى ريزد اب و گه نمك / ‏از تنور و آتشش سازد محك
entries.english: Now he pours water on it and now salt: he puts it to the ordeal of oven and fire.

entries.farsi: اين لعب تنها نه شو را با زن است / ‏هر عشيق و عاشقى را اين فن است
entries.english: Thus are the sought and the seeker intertwined: the conquered and the conqueror are in this sport.

entries.farsi: اين چنين پيچند مطلوب و طلوب / ‏اندر اين لعبند مغلوب و غلوب
entries.english: This sport is not between husband and wife only: this is the practice of everything that is loved and loves.

entries.farsi: از قديم و حادث و عين و عرض / ‏پيچشى چون ويس و رامين مفترض
entries.english: A mutual embracing, like Wís and Rámín, is obligatory between eternal and non-eternal and between substance and accident;

entries.farsi: ليك لعب هر يكى رنگى دگر / پيچش هر يك ز فرهنگى دگر
entries.english: But the sport is of a different character in each case: the embracing is for a different reason in each instance.

entries.farsi: شوى و زن را گفته شد بهر مثيل / ‏كه مكن اى شوى زن را بد گسيل
entries.english: This is said as a parable for husband and wife, meaning, “O husband, do not dismiss your wife unkindly.

entries.farsi: آن شب گردك نه ينگا دست او / خوش امانت داد اندر دست تو
entries.english: On your wedding-night did not the bridesmaid place her (the wife’s) hand in your hand as a goodly trust?

entries.farsi: كانچه با او تو كنى اى معتمد / از بد و نيكى خدا با تو كند
entries.english: For the evil or good which you do unto her, O man worthy of confidence, God will do unto you.”

entries.farsi: حاصل اينجا اين فقيه از بى‏خودى / ‏نه عفيفى ماندش و نه زاهدى
entries.english: To resume, on this occasion this jurist was so beside himself that neither continence nor asceticism remained in him.

entries.farsi: آن فقيه افتاد بر آن حور زاد / آتش او اندر آن پنبه فتاد
entries.english: The jurist threw himself on the nymph: his fire caught hold of her cotton.

entries.farsi: جان به جان پيوست و قالبها چخيد / چون دو مرغ سر بريده مى‏طپيد
entries.english: Soul was joined to soul and their bodies strove in mutual embrace, trembling like two decapitated birds.

entries.farsi: چه سقايه چه ملك چه ارسالن / ‏چه حيا چه دين چه بيم و خوف جان
entries.english: What was the wine-party or the king or Arslan? What were modesty, religion, fear and dread of their lives?

entries.farsi: چشمشان افتاده اندر عين و غين / ‏نه حسن پيداست اينجا نه حسين
entries.english: Their eyes were contorted like ‘ayn and ghayn: here neither Hasan nor Husayn is seen distinctly.

entries.farsi: شد دراز و كو طريق باز گشت / ‏انتظار شاه هم از حد گذشت
entries.english: It became protracted, and how could he return? The king’s expectancy too passed beyond bounds.

entries.farsi: شاه آمد تا ببيند واقعه / ‏ديد آن جا زلزله القارعه
entries.english: The king came to see what had happened: he beheld there the commotion of Calamity.

entries.farsi: آن فقيه از بيم بر جست و برفت / ‏سوى مجلس جام را بربود تفت
entries.english: The jurist sprang up in terror and fled to the banquet-hall and hastily seized the wine-cup.

entries.farsi: شه چو دوزخ پر شرار و پر نكال / ‏تشنه‏ى خون دو جفت بد فعال
entries.english: The king, full of fire and fury like Hell, was thirsting for the blood of the guilty pair.

entries.farsi: چون فقيهش ديد رخ پر خشم و قهر / تلخ و خونى گشته همچون جام زهر
entries.english: When the jurist saw his enraged and wrathful countenance, which had become bitter and murderous as a cup of poison,

entries.farsi: بانگ زد بر ساقى‏اش كاى گرم‏دار / چه نشستى خيره ده در طبعش آر
entries.english: He shouted to his cup-bearer, “O solicitous, why do you sit dumbfounded? Give and put him in good humour!”

entries.farsi: خنده آمد شاه را گفت اى كيا / آمدم با طبع آن دختر ترا
entries.english: The king laughed and said, “O sir, I am restored to my good humour: the girl is yours.

entries.farsi: آن چه آن را من ننوشم همچو نوش / ‏كى دهم در خورد يار و خويش و توش
entries.english: I am the king: my business is justice and bounty: I drink of that which my munificence bestowed on my friend.

entries.farsi: پادشاهم كار من عدل است و داد / ز آن خورم كه يار را جودم بداد
entries.english: How should I give friend and kinsman for food and drink what I would not drink as honey?

entries.farsi: ز آن خورانم من غالمان را كه من / ‏مى‏خورم بر خوان خاص خويشتن
entries.english: I let my pages eat and drink of that which I eat and drink at my own private table.

entries.farsi: ز آن خورانم بندگان را از طعام / ‏كه خورم من خود ز پخته يا ز خام
entries.english: I give my slaves the same food, cooked or raw, as I eat myself.

entries.farsi: من چو پوشم از خز و اطلس لباس / ز آن بپوشانم حشم را نه پالس
entries.english: When I put on a robe of silk or satin, I clothe my retainers in the same, not in coarse woollen garments.

entries.farsi: شرم دارم از نبى ذو فنون / ‏ألبسوهم گفت مما تلبسون
entries.english: I feel reverence for the all-accomplished Prophet, who said, ‘Clothe them in that which you clothe yourselves.’

entries.farsi: ديگران را بس به طبع آورده‏اى / ‏در صبورى چست و راغب كرده‏اى
entries.english: Mustafá gave his sons this injunction — Feed your dependents with what you eat.’”

entries.farsi: مصطفى كرد اين وصيت با بنون / اطعموا الاذناب مما تاكلون
entries.english: You have often restored others to a good disposition: you have made them ready and willing to show fortitude.

entries.farsi: هم به طبع آور به مردى خويش را / پيشوا كن عقل دور انديش را
entries.english: Manfully restore yourself too to disposition: take the reason that meditates on fortitude as your guide.

entries.farsi: چون قالووزى صبرت پر شود / جان به اوج عرش و كرسى بر شود
entries.english: When the guidance of fortitude becomes a wing for you, your spirit will soar to the zenith of the Throne and Footstool.

entries.farsi: مصطفى بين كه چو صبرش شد براق / ‏بر كشانيدش به بالاى طباق
entries.english: See, when fortitude became a Buraq for him, how it carried Mustafá up to the top of the spheres.

sections.title_fa: روان گشتن شاه زادگان بعد از تمام بحث و ماجرا به جانب واليت چين سوى معشوق و مقصود تا به قدر امكان به مقصود نزديكتر باشند، اگر چه راه وصل مسدود است به قدر امكان نزديكتر شدن

entries.farsi: محمود است الى آخره / اين بگفتند و روان گشتند زود

sections.title_en: their beloved and the object, in order that they might be as near as possible to that object; although the way to union is barred, it is praiseworthy to approach as near as is possible.

entries.farsi: هر چه بود اى يار من آن لحظه بود / صبر بگزيدند و صديقين شدند
entries.english: They said this and immediately set out: O my friend, everything that was at that moment.

entries.farsi: بعد از آن سوى بالد چين شدند / والدين و ملك را بگذاشتند
entries.english: They chose fortitude and became true witnesses; then they set off towards the land of China.

entries.farsi: راه معشوق نهان برداشتند / همچو ابراهيم ادهم از سرير
entries.english: They left their parents and kingdom, they took the way to the hidden beloved.

entries.farsi: عشقشان بى‏پا و سر كرد و فقير / يا چو ابراهيم مرسل سر خوشى
entries.english: Like Ibrahim son of Adham, Love from the throne made them footless and headless and destitute.

entries.farsi: پيش عشق و خنجرش حلقى كشيد
entries.english: Either, like Abraham who was sent, one intoxicated cast himself into a fire,

entries.farsi: ‏خويش را افكند اندر آتشى / يا چو اسماعيل صبار مجيد
entries.english: Or, like the much-enduring and glorious Ismail, offered a throat to Love and his dagger.

sections.title_fa: حكايت امرؤ القيس كه پادشاه عرب بود و به صورت عظيم بجمال بود، يوسف وقت خود بود و زنان عرب چون زليخا مرده‏ى او و او شاعر طبع، قفا نبك من ذكرى حبيب و منزل، چون همه زنان او را به جان مى‏جستند اى عجب غزل او و ناله‏ى او بهر چه بود، مگر دانست كه اينها همه تمثال صورتى‏اند كه بر تختهاى خاك نقش كرده‏اند، عاقبت اين امرؤ القيس را حالى پيدا شد كه نيم شب از
ملك و فرزند گريخت و خود را در دلقى پنهان كرد و از آن اقليم به اقليم ديگر رفت در طلب آن كس كه از اقليم منزه است، يَخْ تَصُّ بِرَحْ مَتِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ، الى آخره
sections.title_en: Story of Imra’u ’l-Qays, who was the king of the Arabs and exceedingly handsome: he was the Joseph of his time, and the Arab women were desperately in love with him, like Zalikha. He had the poetic genius— “Halt, let us weep in memory of a beloved and a dwelling-place.”

Since all the women desired him with soul, one may well wonder what was the object of his love-songs and lamentations. Surely he knew that all these are copies of a picture which have been drawn on frames of earth. At last there came to this Imra’u ’l-Qays such a experience that in the middle of the night he fled from his kingdom and children and concealed himself in the garb of a
dervish and wandered from that clime to another clime in search of Him who transcends all climes: “He chooses for His mercy whom He will”; and so forth.

entries.farsi: امرؤ القيس از ممالك خشك لب / ‏هم كشيدش عشق از خطه‏ى عرب
entries.english: Imra’u ’l-Qays was weary of his empire: Love carried him away from the country of the Arabs,

entries.farsi: تا بيامد خشت مى‏زد در تبوك / امرؤ القيس آمده‏ست اينجا به كد
entries.english: So that he came and worked as a brick-maker at Tabuk. The king was told that a royal personage,

entries.farsi: در شكار عشق خشتى مى‏زند / آن ملك بر خاست شب شد پيش او
entries.english: Imra’u ’l-Qays, having fallen a prey to Love, had come there and was making bricks by labour.

entries.farsi: گفت او را اى مليك خوب رو / يوسف وقتى دو ملكت شد كمال
entries.english: The king rose up and went to him at night and said to him, “O king of beauteous countenance,

entries.farsi: ‏مر ترا رام از بالد و از جمال / گشته مردان بندگان از تيغ تو
entries.english: You are the Joseph of the age. Two empires have become entirely subject to you—, of the territories and, of Beauty.

entries.farsi: و آن زنان ملك مه بى‏ميغ تو / پيش ما باشى تو بخت ما بود
entries.english: Men are enslaved by your sword, while women are the chattels of your cloudless moon.

entries.farsi: جان ما از وصل تو صد جان شود / هم من و هم ملك من مملوك تو
entries.english: You will dwell with me, it will be my fortune: by union with you my soul will be made a hundred souls.

entries.farsi: اى به همت ملكها متروك تو / فلسفه گفتش بسى و او خموش
entries.english: Both I and my kingdom are your to hold as your own, O you who in high aspiration have abandoned kingdoms!”

entries.farsi: ‏ناگهان وا كرد از سر روى پوش / تا چه گفتش او به گوش از عشق و درد
entries.english: He reasoned with him for a long time, and he kept silence, suddenly he unveiled the mystery.

entries.farsi: همچو خود در حال سر گردانش كرد / دست او بگرفت و با او يار شد
entries.english: Think what of love and passion he whispered into his ear! Immediately he made him a crazy wanderer like himself.

entries.farsi: او هم از تخت و كمر بيزار شد / تا بالد دور رفتند اين دو شه
entries.english: He took his hand and accompanied him: he too renounced his throne and belt.

entries.farsi: ‏عشق يك كرت نكرده‏ست اين گنه / بر بزرگان شهد و بر طفالنست شير
entries.english: These two kings went to distant lands: not once has Love committed this crime.

entries.farsi: او به هر كشتى بود من الاخير / غير اين دو بس ملوك بى‏شمار
entries.english: It is honey for the grown-up and milk for children: for every boat it is the last bale.

entries.farsi: عشقشان از ملك بربود و تبار / جان اين سه شه بچه هم گرد چين
entries.english: Besides these two, many kings, beyond number, have Love torn from their kingdoms and families.

entries.farsi: ‏همچو مرغان گشته هر سو دانه چين / زهره نى تا لب گشايند از ضمير
entries.english: The souls of these three princes also were roaming around China in every direction, like birds picking up grain.

entries.farsi: ز انكه رازى با خطر بود و خطير / صد هزاران سر به پولى ان زمان
entries.english: They dare not open their lips to utter the thoughts hidden, because it was a perilous and grave secret.

entries.farsi: ‏عشق خشم آلوده زه كرده كمان / عشق خود بى‏خشم در وقت خوشى
entries.english: A hundred thousand heads for a penny at the moment Love strings his bow in anger.

entries.farsi: ‏خوى دارد دم به دم خيره كشى / اين بود آن لحظه كاو خشنود شد
entries.english: Even without anger, at the time when he is well-pleased, Love is always accustomed to kill recklessly.

entries.farsi: من چه گويم چون كه خشم آلود شد / ليك مرج جان فداى شير او
entries.english: This is at the moment when he is contented: how shall I describe when he is angered?

entries.farsi: كش كشد اين عشق و اين شمشير او / كشتنى به از هزاران زندگى
entries.english: But may the soul’s pasture be the ransom for his lion that is killed by this Love and his scimitar!

entries.farsi: ‏سلطنتها مرده‏ى اين بندگى / با كنايت رازها با همدگر
entries.english: It is a killing better than a thousand lives: sovereignties are mortally enamoured of this servitude.
